topics.title: Verification of Superposition theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL307 - CIRCUITS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Verification of Superposition theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Superposition Theorem (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> In any linear bilateral network containing more than one independent source, the response (voltage across any element or current through any element) is the algebraic sum of the responses produced by each source acting independently, with all other independent sources deactivated — **voltage sources replaced by short circuits** and **current sources replaced by open circuits**.

Mathematically, for a network with *n* independent sources $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n$, the response $R$ at any branch is:

$$R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \cdots + R_n$$

where $R_k$ is the response due to source $S_k$ alone, with all other sources deactivated.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Lightbulb Illumination" Picture**
> Imagine a dark room being lit by **two separate bulbs** placed at different spots on the ceiling. The brightness at any point on the floor is the **sum** of brightness contributions from Bulb-1 alone and Bulb-2 alone.
> - If you *switch off* Bulb-2, you measure the brightness from Bulb-1 alone.
> - If you *switch off* Bulb-1, you measure the brightness from Bulb-2 alone.
> - Adding these two gives the total brightness when **both bulbs are on**.
> This is exactly how the Superposition Theorem works with voltage and current sources in a circuit.

## 1.3 Key Operational Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Independent Source** | A source whose value is not controlled by any circuit variable (e.g., battery, DC supply). |
| **Dependent Source** | Source whose value depends on a circuit variable (e.g., VCVS, VCCS). Superposition is **not directly applicable** when only dependent sources exist. |
| **Deactivation of Voltage Source** | Replace the ideal voltage source with a **short circuit (V = 0)** keeping its internal resistance if specified. |
| **Deactivation of Current Source** | Replace the ideal current source with an **open circuit (I = 0)** keeping its internal resistance if specified. |
| **Linearity** | The property that the output is proportional to the input and obeys the additive principle. |

## 1.4 Conditions and Applicability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When to Apply Superposition Theorem**
> 1. The network must be **linear** (obey Ohm's Law, KVL, KCL).
> 2. The network must be **bilateral** (same response for both directions of current).
> 3. All elements must have **constant (time-invariant)** values.
> 4. The theorem is valid only for finding the response due to **independent sources** operating simultaneously.
> 5. **Power cannot be calculated** by superposition — it is a non-linear quantity (depends on $I^2$ or $V^2$).

## 1.5 Real-World Engineering Relevance

- **Power system analysis** with multiple generators feeding a common load.
- **Electronic circuit design** where biasing sources and signal sources coexist.
- **Network theorems simplification** — forms the foundation for Thevenin's and Norton's theorems.
- **Communication circuits** where multiple AC signals overlap on the same node.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Procedure

The following systematic procedure is used to apply the Superposition Theorem:

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Application Logic**
> 1. **Identify** all independent sources in the network and label them ($V_1$, $V_2$, $I_1$, $I_2$, etc.).
> 2. **Deactivate all sources except one.** This means:
>    - Replace every *other* voltage source by a **short circuit**.
>    - Replace every *other* current source by an **open circuit**.
> 3. **Compute the response** (current or voltage) at the desired element due to the *single active source* using standard techniques (Ohm's Law, KVL, KCL, mesh/nodal analysis).
> 4. **Repeat Steps 2 and 3** for each independent source, one at a time.
> 5. **Algebraically add** all individual responses, observing sign conventions (current directions and voltage polarities) carefully.
> 6. The sum is the **total response** when all sources are active simultaneously.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind the Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical Justification**
> The theorem is a direct consequence of the **linearity of network equations**. The KVL and KCL equations of any linear resistive network yield a system of linear algebraic equations in the unknown currents/voltages. Because the system is linear, the **principle of superposition of solutions** applies — the total solution is the sum of solutions produced by each forcing function (source) acting alone.

For example, the mesh equation for mesh *k* in a linear network takes the form:

$$\sum_{j} R_{kj} I_j = V_k$$

If $V_k = V_{k}^{(1)} + V_{k}^{(2)}$, then $I_j = I_j^{(1)} + I_j^{(2)}$ by linear algebra.

## 2.3 Deactivation Rules — Visual Summary

| Source Type | Deactivation Method | Physical Reasoning |
|-------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Ideal Voltage Source ($V_s$) | **Short circuit** ($V = 0$, but allows current) | Zero potential difference, but no resistance to current flow. |
| Ideal Current Source ($I_s$) | **Open circuit** ($I = 0$, but allows voltage) | Zero current through the branch, but no constraint on voltage. |
| Practical Voltage Source | Short the ideal part; keep $R_s$ in the circuit. | Internal resistance still affects the network. |
| Practical Current Source | Open the ideal part; keep $R_s$ in parallel. | Internal resistance still shunts the network. |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet — Key Formulas for Superposition Problems**

| Concept | Formula | Unit | Application |
|---------|---------|------|-------------|
| Ohm's Law | $V = I \cdot R$ | V, A, $\Omega$ | Fundamental V–I relation for linear resistors. |
| Superposition (General) | $R_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} R_k$ | depends | Total response = sum of individual responses. |
| Voltage Divider (2 resistors) | $V_x = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_x}{R_1 + R_2}$ | V | Finds voltage when one source is active. |
| Current Divider (2 resistors) | $I_x = I_s \cdot \dfrac{R_{other}}{R_1 + R_2}$ | A | Finds branch current when one current source is active. |
| Parallel Combination | $R_p = \dfrac{R_1 \cdot R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | $\Omega$ | Simplifies networks when one source is shorted/open. |
| Series Combination | $R_s = R_1 + R_2 + \cdots$ | $\Omega$ | Simplifies networks. |
| Power (DO NOT use Superposition) | $P = I^2 R = \dfrac{V^2}{R}$ | W | Calculate only after final $I$ or $V$ is found. |
| Sign Convention | $+I$ if direction matches assumed; $-I$ if opposite. | — | Critical for algebraic sum in superposition. |

## 2.5 Limitations of Superposition Theorem

> [!WARNING]
> **Where the Theorem Fails**
> 1. **Non-linear networks** (diodes, transistors in saturation, saturated inductors).
> 2. **Power dissipation** — Cannot add powers: $P_{total} \ne P_1 + P_2 + \cdots$
> 3. Networks with **only dependent sources** and no independent sources (no independent forcing function).
> 4. Circuits with **time-varying parameters** (e.g., switched capacitor networks).
> 5. The theorem does **not apply to AC circuits with different frequencies** directly — each frequency must be handled separately (used as a basis for Fourier analysis).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Detailed Step-by-Step Solution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Circuit for Verification**
> Consider the following linear DC network:
> - $V_1 = 10\text{ V}$ in series with $R_1 = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, connected between Node A and ground.
> - $V_2 = 5\text{ V}$ in series with $R_2 = 2\text{ k}\Omega$, connected between Node A and ground.
> - $R_3 = 3\text{ k}\Omega$ connected between Node A and ground.
> - **Find the current through $R_3$ using Superposition Theorem.**

```
   +---[R1 = 1kΩ]---+---[R2 = 2kΩ]---+
   |                  |                |
  V1 = 10V          Node A          V2 = 5V
   |                  |                |
   +-------[R3 = 3kΩ]-+----------------+
                          |
                       (GND)
```

### Step 1 — Direct Nodal Analysis (Reference Solution)

Apply KCL at Node A (sum of currents leaving = 0):

$$\frac{V_A - V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_A - V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_A}{R_3} = 0$$

Substitute numerical values (in volts and kΩ):

$$\frac{V_A - 10}{1} + \frac{V_A - 5}{2} + \frac{V_A}{3} = 0$$

Multiply the entire equation by the LCM = 6 to clear denominators:

$$6(V_A - 10) + 3(V_A - 5) + 2V_A = 0$$

$$6V_A - 60 + 3V_A - 15 + 2V_A = 0$$

$$(6 + 3 + 2)V_A = 60 + 15$$

$$11 V_A = 75$$

$$V_A = \frac{75}{11} \approx 6.818 \text{ V}$$

The current through $R_3$ is therefore:

$$I_{R_3} = \frac{V_A}{R_3} = \frac{6.818}{3} \approx 2.273 \text{ mA}$$

### Step 2 — Superposition: Activate $V_1$ Only (Deactivate $V_2$)

When $V_2$ is deactivated, it is **replaced by a short circuit**. The right branch now contains only $R_2 = 2\text{ k}\Omega$ between Node A and ground.

The new equivalent circuit has $R_2$ and $R_3$ in **parallel**, then in series with $R_1$:

$$R_{23}^{(1)} = R_2 \parallel R_3 = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = \frac{2 \times 3}{2 + 3} = \frac{6}{5} = 1.2 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Using the voltage divider rule, the voltage at Node A due to $V_1$ alone:

$$V_{A1} = V_1 \cdot \frac{R_{23}^{(1)}}{R_1 + R_{23}^{(1)}} = 10 \cdot \frac{1.2}{1 + 1.2} = 10 \cdot \frac{1.2}{2.2} = \frac{12}{2.2} \approx 5.455 \text{ V}$$

The current through $R_3$ due to $V_1$ alone:

$$I_{R_3}^{(1)} = \frac{V_{A1}}{R_3} = \frac{5.455}{3} \approx 1.818 \text{ mA}$$

### Step 3 — Superposition: Activate $V_2$ Only (Deactivate $V_1$)

When $V_1$ is deactivated, it is **replaced by a short circuit**. The left branch now contains only $R_1 = 1\text{ k}\Omega$ between Node A and ground.

The new equivalent circuit has $R_1$ and $R_3$ in **parallel**, then in series with $R_2$:

$$R_{13}^{(2)} = R_1 \parallel R_3 = \frac{R_1 \cdot R_3}{R_1 + R_3} = \frac{1 \times 3}{1 + 3} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Using the voltage divider rule, the voltage at Node A due to $V_2$ alone:

$$V_{A2} = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_{13}^{(2)}}{R_2 + R_{13}^{(2)}} = 5 \cdot \frac{0.75}{2 + 0.75} = 5 \cdot \frac{0.75}{2.75} = \frac{3.75}{2.75} \approx 1.364 \text{ V}$$

The current through $R_3$ due to $V_2$ alone:

$$I_{R_3}^{(2)} = \frac{V_{A2}}{R_3} = \frac{1.364}{3} \approx 0.455 \text{ mA}$$

### Step 4 — Algebraic Sum of Individual Responses

Both currents flow from Node A to ground through $R_3$ (same direction), so they add:

$$I_{R_3}^{(total)} = I_{R_3}^{(1)} + I_{R_3}^{(2)} = 1.818 + 0.455 = 2.273 \text{ mA}$$

### Step 5 — Verification

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Check with Step 1 (Nodal Analysis)**
> The Superposition result $I_{R_3} = 2.273 \text{ mA}$ **matches exactly** with the nodal analysis result, confirming the theorem's validity.

## 3.2 Power Cannot Be Superposed — Demonstration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Power Calculations Require Special Care**
> Let us calculate power dissipated in $R_3$ for our example:

**Method 1 (Correct — using total current):**

$$P_{R_3} = (I_{R_3}^{total})^2 \cdot R_3 = (2.273 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 3000 = 15.50 \text{ mW}$$

**Method 2 (Incorrect — superposition of powers):**

$$P_1 = (1.818 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 3000 = 9.92 \text{ mW}$$

$$P_2 = (0.455 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 3000 = 0.62 \text{ mW}$$

$$P_1 + P_2 = 9.92 + 0.62 = 10.54 \text{ mW} \ne 15.50 \text{ mW}$$

The discrepancy occurs because power is a **quadratic (non-linear)** function of current: $(I_1 + I_2)^2 = I_1^2 + I_2^2 + 2I_1 I_2 \ne I_1^2 + I_2^2$.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Theorem

| Network Condition | Best Method | Reason |
|-------------------|-------------|--------|
| One source, find one response | **Ohm's Law** | Simplest path. |
| Multiple sources, single response | **Superposition** | Theorem's ideal use case. |
| Multiple sources, many responses | **Nodal/Mesh Analysis** | One setup yields all variables. |
| Load variations, single source | **Thevenin/Norton** | Reduces network to a single equivalent. |
| Non-linear elements present | **Graphical/Iterative methods** | Superposition fails. |
| AC circuits of different frequencies | **Superposition per frequency + Phasor sum** | Each frequency handled separately. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Experimental Setup

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the laboratory verification procedure for the Superposition Theorem, showing the interconnection of source modules, the test network, and the measurement chain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POW1["DC Power Supply Module A"]
        V1Unit["V1 = 10V Regulated DC Source"]
        V1Adj["Voltage Adjustment Knob"]
        V1Unit --- V1Adj
    end

    subgraph POW2["DC Power Supply Module B"]
        V2Unit["V2 = 5V Regulated DC Source"]
        V2Adj["Voltage Adjustment Knob"]
        V2Unit --- V2Adj
    end

    subgraph TBN["Test Breadboard Network"]
        R1Comp["R1 = 1kΩ Resistor"]
        R2Comp["R2 = 2kΩ Resistor"]
        R3Comp["R3 = 3kΩ Resistor Load Resistor"]
        NA["Node A Junction"]
        GNDREF["Ground Reference Rail"]
    end

    subgraph MEAS["Measurement Instruments"]
        DMM1["Digital Multimeter 1 Voltage Mode"]
        DMM2["Digital Multimeter 2 Current Mode"]
    end

    V1Adj -- positive lead --> R1Comp
    R1Comp --> NA
    V2Adj -- positive lead --> R2Comp
    R2Comp --> NA
    NA --> R3Comp
    R3Comp --> GNDREF
    V1Adj -- negative lead --> GNDREF
    V2Adj -- negative lead --> GNDREF

    DMM1 -- probes across R3Comp --> DMM1
    DMM2 -- in series with R3Comp --> DMM2
```

## 4.2 Stepwise Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEP 1: Identify all independent sources in the given network"]
    S2["STEP 2: Label the desired response element — Example: current through R3"]
    S3["STEP 3: Activate V1 only and short circuit V2"]
    S4["STEP 4: Measure the response due to V1 alone using multimeter"]
    S5["STEP 5: Activate V2 only and short circuit V1"]
    S6["STEP 6: Measure the response due to V2 alone using multimeter"]
    S7["STEP 7: Keep both V1 and V2 active simultaneously"]
    S8["STEP 8: Measure the total response with both sources active"]
    S9["STEP 9: Verify that measured total equals algebraic sum of individual responses"]
    S10["STEP 10: Record results in tabular form and calculate percentage error"]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10
```

## 4.3 Circuit Schematic Diagram (ASCII Representation)

```
         R1 = 1kΩ         R2 = 2kΩ
   o---/\/\/\---o-------/\/\/\---o
   |            |                |
 (+)           Node A          (+)
  V1            |                V2
 (10V)          |              (5V)
 (-)            |                (-)
   |            |                |
   o-----/\/\/\/o----------------o
              R3 = 3kΩ
              (LOAD)
                 |
                GND
```

## 4.4 Tabular Result Format (Expected for KTU Lab Record)

| Sl. No. | Condition | Theoretical Value | Measured Value | Percentage Error |
|---------|-----------|-------------------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | $V_1$ alone active, $V_2$ shorted | $I_{R_3} = 1.818$ mA | ___ mA | ___% |
| 2 | $V_2$ alone active, $V_1$ shorted | $I_{R_3} = 0.455$ mA | ___ mA | ___% |
| 3 | Both $V_1$ and $V_2$ active | $I_{R_3} = 2.273$ mA | ___ mA | ___% |
| 4 | Algebraic sum of (1) and (2) | 2.273 mA | — | Comparison with (3) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Acceptance Criterion (KTU Lab Standard)**
> The percentage error between the directly measured total and the algebraic sum of individual responses should typically be **less than 5%** for the verification to be considered successful.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024 Style]**

### **Q1. State the Superposition Theorem. List any two of its limitations.**
**[3 Marks] | CO1 | Bloom Level: Remember/Understand**

**Model Answer:**
The Superposition Theorem states that in any linear bilateral network containing multiple independent sources, the response (voltage or current) at any element is the **algebraic sum of the responses** produced by each independent source acting alone, with all other independent sources deactivated (voltage sources short-circuited and current sources open-circuited).

**Limitations:**
1. The theorem is applicable **only to linear networks** — it fails for non-linear elements such as diodes, transistors, and saturated inductors.
2. The theorem **cannot be used to calculate power** dissipated in an element, because power is a non-linear (quadratic) function of current or voltage.
3. The theorem requires **separate analysis for each frequency** in AC circuits with sources of different frequencies.

---

### **Q2. How are an ideal voltage source and an ideal current source deactivated while applying the Superposition Theorem? Justify.**
**[3 Marks] | CO1 | Bloom Level: Understand/Apply**

**Model Answer:**
- **Ideal Voltage Source:** Deactivated by replacing it with a **short circuit (a plain wire)**. This is because an ideal voltage source maintains a constant voltage irrespective of current. Setting $V_s = 0$ reduces it to a wire of zero resistance, but it can still carry any current.
- **Ideal Current Source:** Deactivated by replacing it with an **open circuit (a break)**. This is because an ideal current source delivers a constant current regardless of the voltage across it. Setting $I_s = 0$ means the branch carries no current, equivalent to a break.

The internal resistances (if any) of practical sources are **retained** in the circuit during deactivation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Using Superposition Theorem, find the current through the $4\text{ Ω}$ resistor in the network shown below.** **[7 Marks]**

```
   +---[2Ω]---+---[4Ω]---+
   |          |          |
  12V         R3 = 4Ω    6V
   |          |          |
   +----------+----------+
            Node B
              |
             [4Ω]
              |
             GND
```

- Left branch: $V_1 = 12\text{ V}$ in series with $R_1 = 2\text{ Ω}$, connected to Node B.
- Middle branch: $R_3 = 4\text{ Ω}$ from Node B to ground.
- Right branch: $V_2 = 6\text{ V}$ in series with $R_2 = 4\text{ Ω}$, connected to Node B.

#### **Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Activate $V_1 = 12\text{ V}$ alone; short circuit $V_2$.**

With $V_2$ shorted, $R_2 = 4\text{ Ω}$ comes directly from Node B to ground. So $R_2 \parallel R_3$:

$$R_{23} = \frac{4 \times 4}{4 + 4} = 2 \text{ Ω}$$

Total resistance seen by $V_1$:

$$R_{eq}^{(1)} = R_1 + R_{23} = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ Ω}$$

Current from $V_1$:

$$I_1 = \frac{V_1}{R_{eq}^{(1)}} = \frac{12}{4} = 3 \text{ A}$$

Voltage at Node B due to $V_1$:

$$V_{B1} = I_1 \cdot R_{23} = 3 \times 2 = 6 \text{ V}$$

Current through $R_3$ due to $V_1$ (flowing B → GND):

$$I_{R_3}^{(1)} = \frac{V_{B1}}{R_3} = \frac{6}{4} = 1.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Activate $V_2 = 6\text{ V}$ alone; short circuit $V_1$.**

With $V_1$ shorted, $R_1 = 2\text{ Ω}$ comes directly from Node B to ground. So $R_1 \parallel R_3$:

$$R_{13} = \frac{2 \times 4}{2 + 4} = \frac{8}{6} = \frac{4}{3} \text{ Ω}$$

Total resistance seen by $V_2$:

$$R_{eq}^{(2)} = R_2 + R_{13} = 4 + \frac{4}{3} = \frac{16}{3} \text{ Ω}$$

Current from $V_2$:

$$I_2 = \frac{V_2}{R_{eq}^{(2)}} = \frac{6}{16/3} = \frac{18}{16} = 1.125 \text{ A}$$

Voltage at Node B due to $V_2$:

$$V_{B2} = I_2 \cdot R_{13} = 1.125 \times \frac{4}{3} = 1.5 \text{ V}$$

Current through $R_3$ due to $V_2$:

$$I_{R_3}^{(2)} = \frac{V_{B2}}{R_3} = \frac{1.5}{4} = 0.375 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Algebraic Sum (both currents flow B → GND, same direction):**

$$I_{R_3}^{total} = I_{R_3}^{(1)} + I_{R_3}^{(2)} = 1.5 + 0.375 = 1.875 \text{ A}$$

> **Final Answer:** $I_{R_3} = 1.875 \text{ A}$ flowing from Node B to ground.

---

#### **(b) Describe, with a neat procedure, how the Superposition Theorem is verified experimentally in the laboratory. List the apparatus required and the precautions to be observed.** **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. DC Regulated Power Supply (two units, 0–30 V variable).
2. Resistors: $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$ (as per circuit values, $\pm 5\%$ tolerance).
3. Breadboard or PCB for circuit assembly.
4. Digital Multimeters (DMM) — at least two, for simultaneous voltage and current measurement.
5. Connecting wires, crocodile clips, and soldering accessories.

**Experimental Procedure:**

1. **Circuit Assembly:** Construct the given network on the breadboard with the specified values of $V_1$, $V_2$, $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$. Verify each connection with the circuit diagram.

2. **Initial Check:** Set $V_1$ and $V_2$ to zero; check the supply polarity and ensure all grounds are common.

3. **Case 1 — $V_1$ alone active:** Switch ON $V_1$ to the rated value (e.g., 10 V). Keep $V_2$ off / at zero. Measure the current through $R_3$ (or voltage across it) using the DMM. Record the value.

4. **Case 2 — $V_2$ alone active:** Switch OFF $V_1$ (or set to zero) and switch ON $V_2$ to its rated value (e.g., 5 V). Measure the current through $R_3$ again. Record the value.

5. **Case 3 — Both sources active:** Switch ON both $V_1$ and $V_2$ simultaneously. Measure the current through $R_3$. Record the value.

6. **Verification:** Check that the measured value in Case 3 equals the **algebraic sum** of Case 1 and Case 2 values, within acceptable error (typically ≤ 5%).

7. **Repeat for different source values** (e.g., $V_1 = 8$ V, $V_2 = 4$ V) to confirm the linear relationship.

**Precautions:**
- Ensure the **polarity of all DC sources** is correctly observed.
- Use a **common ground** reference for all instruments and the circuit.
- Use **precision resistors** (1% tolerance) for accurate comparison.
- **Calibrate the DMMs** before measurement.
- Avoid **loose connections** on the breadboard to prevent intermittent readings.
- Always keep the **current limit** of the power supply within the safe range to protect resistors.
- Do **not** calculate power by superposition; compute it only from the final total current.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) For the circuit given below, calculate the voltage across $R_L = 6\text{ k}\Omega$ using the Superposition Theorem.** **[7 Marks]**

- $V_1 = 15\text{ V}$ in series with $R_1 = 3\text{ k}\Omega$.
- $V_2 = 9\text{ V}$ in series with $R_2 = 3\text{ k}\Omega$.
- Both branches connect to a common node N; $R_L = 6\text{ k}\Omega$ is connected from N to ground.
- **Find $V_{R_L}$.**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — $V_1$ alone active, $V_2$ shorted:**

$R_2 \parallel R_L$ from N to ground:

$$R_{2L} = \frac{3 \times 6}{3 + 6} = \frac{18}{9} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Voltage at N (voltage divider):

$$V_{N1} = V_1 \cdot \frac{R_{2L}}{R_1 + R_{2L}} = 15 \cdot \frac{2}{3 + 2} = 15 \cdot \frac{2}{5} = 6 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — $V_2$ alone active, $V_1$ shorted:**

$R_1 \parallel R_L$ from N to ground:

$$R_{1L} = \frac{3 \times 6}{3 + 6} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Voltage at N:

$$V_{N2} = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_{1L}}{R_2 + R_{1L}} = 9 \cdot \frac{2}{3 + 2} = 9 \cdot \frac{2}{5} = 3.6 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Total Voltage across $R_L$:**

Both voltages at N have the same polarity (positive at N), so:

$$V_{R_L} = V_{N1} + V_{N2} = 6 + 3.6 = 9.6 \text{ V}$$

> **Final Answer:** $V_{R_L} = 9.6 \text{ V}$ (positive terminal at node N).

---

#### **(b) Discuss the common sources of error encountered in the laboratory verification of the Superposition Theorem. How can these errors be minimized?** **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Common Sources of Error:**

1. **Resistor Tolerance:** Carbon-film resistors typically have $\pm 5\%$ tolerance. The measured currents and voltages therefore differ from theoretical predictions.

2. **Multimeter Accuracy:** Digital multimeters have an inherent accuracy class (typically $\pm 0.5\%$ to $\pm 2\%$). This introduces reading errors.

3. **Power Supply Drift:** DC regulated supplies may have small ripple and may drift from the set value over time.

4. **Contact Resistance:** Loose breadboard connections or poorly soldered joints add unknown series resistance.

5. **Internal Resistance of Source / Meter:** The internal resistance of practical sources and the loading effect of voltmeters alter the circuit behaviour.

6. **Temperature Effects:** Resistor values drift with temperature, especially when current flows for a long time.

7. **Parasitic Capacitance / Inductance:** At high frequencies, breadboard wiring introduces stray reactances.

**Minimization Strategies:**

1. Use **precision metal-film resistors** (1% or better) to reduce tolerance errors.
2. **Calibrate the DMM** before taking measurements; use the same meter for related readings to maintain consistency.
3. Use **well-regulated, low-ripple DC supplies**; re-check the voltage before each reading.
4. Use **clean, tight breadboard connections** or assemble on a soldered PCB.
5. Use **high-impedance voltmeters** (≥ 10 MΩ) to avoid loading the circuit.
6. Allow the circuit to **stabilize thermally** before recording readings.
7. **Repeat each measurement** several times and take the average.
8. Compute the **percentage error** and report the mean error for the experiment.

$$\text{Percentage Error} = \frac{\vert \text{Measured} - \text{Theoretical} \vert}{\text{Theoretical}} \times 100\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Sign Convention Error (–2 marks):** Students frequently forget to check the **direction of currents** before adding them. If the two contributions flow in **opposite directions**, the algebraic sum must be a **subtraction**, not addition. Always mark assumed directions on the circuit diagram.
> - **Power Calculation by Superposition (–3 marks):** A very common mistake is computing $P_1 = I_1^2 R$ and $P_2 = I_2^2 R$ and then adding them. This is **fundamentally wrong** because power is non-linear. Compute $P = (I_{total})^2 R$ using the **final total current**.
> - **Deactivation Mistake (–2 marks):** Some students open-circuit voltage sources and short-circuit current sources — the rule is the **opposite**.
> - **Missing Internal Resistance (–1 mark):** If a practical source has internal resistance, that resistance **must be retained** in the circuit during deactivation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Superposition Theorem (Module Experiment 2)**

- **Core Statement:** Total response in a linear bilateral network = algebraic sum of responses from each independent source acting alone.
- **Deactivation Rules:** Voltage source → **short circuit**; Current source → **open circuit**. Internal resistances (if any) are **kept** in place.
- **Applicable to:** Linear, bilateral, time-invariant networks with multiple **independent** sources.
- **Not Applicable to:** Non-linear elements, power calculations, single-source networks (trivial case).
- **Procedure:** Identify sources → Deactivate all but one → Compute response → Repeat for each source → Algebraically add all responses with proper **sign convention**.
- **Sign Convention:** Always mark the assumed direction of current and polarity of voltage on the diagram. Add if actual direction matches assumption; subtract if opposite.
- **Power Rule:** $P_{total} \ne P_1 + P_2 + \cdots$ — compute power **only** from the final total current/voltage.
- **Experimental Verification:** Three measurements needed: (1) source 1 alone, (2) source 2 alone, (3) both sources together. The sum of (1) and (2) must equal (3) within ≤ 5% error.
- **Apparatus:** Two DC regulated supplies, precision resistors, two DMMs, breadboard.
- **Key Precautions:** Common ground, correct polarity, calibrated meters, tight connections, observe current limits.
- **Cross-Verification:** Always cross-check the superposition result with **nodal or mesh analysis** to validate.
- **AC Caveat:** For AC sources of different frequencies, superposition is applied **per frequency** and the time-domain response is obtained by inverse transformation.
- **Pedagogical Link:** Superposition forms the **mathematical basis** for Thevenin's, Norton's, and Maximum Power Transfer theorems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verification of Thevenin's theorem and Maximum Power Transfer theorem; Calculation of Norton's equivalent circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL307 - CIRCUITS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Verification of Thevenin's theorem and Maximum Power Transfer theorem; Calculation of Norton's equivalent circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Thevenin's Theorem (Formally stated):** *Any linear bilateral two-terminal network containing voltage and current sources and resistors can be replaced by an equivalent circuit consisting of a single voltage source $V_{Th}$ (Thevenin voltage) in series with a single resistor $R_{Th}$ (Thevenin resistance), where $V_{Th}$ is the open-circuit voltage across the load terminals and $R_{Th}$ is the equivalent resistance looking back into the network from the open-circuited terminals with all independent sources deactivated.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Norton's Theorem (Formally stated):** *Any linear bilateral two-terminal network can be replaced by an equivalent circuit consisting of a current source $I_N$ (Norton current) in parallel with a single resistor $R_N$ (Norton resistance). $I_N$ is the short-circuit current through the load terminals, and $R_N$ is the equivalent resistance of the deactivated network.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem (Formally stated):** *For a linear bilateral network represented by its Thevenin equivalent ($V_{Th}$, $R_{Th}$), maximum power is delivered to a variable load resistance $R_L$ when $R_L = R_{Th}$. The maximum power delivered is $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{Th}^2}{4 R_{Th}}$.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water supply pipeline** delivering water from a reservoir to your home:

- **Thevenin Voltage $V_{Th}$** is the *pressure of water available at your tap* when no tap is open (open-circuit condition).
- **Thevenin Resistance $R_{Th}$** is the *internal pipe friction* — the resistance the pipe offers against the water flow.
- **Norton Current $I_N$** is the *maximum flow rate* possible when you completely remove the tap (short-circuit condition).
- **Maximum Power Transfer** occurs when the *tap restriction equals the pipe friction* — neither too open nor too closed. At this point, the "useful work" of water delivered per second is maximized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are these theorems so important in the KTU lab?**
> They let you **predict the behaviour of a complicated network** at one specific pair of terminals **without re-analyzing the entire circuit** each time you change the load. This is essential for amplifier output stages, transmission line matching, and power supply design.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used

- **Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{OC}$):** measured in **Volts (V)** using a high-impedance voltmeter (ideally infinite internal resistance).
- **Short-Circuit Current ($I_{SC}$):** measured in **Amperes (A)** using a low-impedance ammeter (ideally zero internal resistance).
- **Thevenin/Norton Resistance:** measured in **Ohms (Ω)**.
- **Power delivered:** measured in **Watts (W)**.
- **Standard Temperature Reference:** $T = 27^\circ C$ (**300 K**) for laboratory calibration unless otherwise specified.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Theorem Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Thevenin's Theorem — Underlying Logic

1. **Identify the load terminals (A–B)** where the equivalent is required.
2. **Remove the load resistor $R_L$** to make the terminals open-circuited.
3. **Measure (or calculate) the open-circuit voltage $V_{OC}$** between A and B. This becomes $V_{Th}$.
4. **Deactivate all independent sources:**
   - Replace every **independent voltage source** with a **short circuit**.
   - Replace every **independent current source** with an **open circuit**.
5. **Look back into terminals A–B** and calculate the equivalent resistance. This becomes $R_{Th}$.
6. **Reconstruct the equivalent:** $V_{Th}$ in series with $R_{Th}$ feeding $R_L$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why deactivate sources?** A deactivated source represents "what the rest of the network looks like" without the source's influence — only its internal impedance (zero for ideal V-source, infinite for ideal I-source) remains.

### 2.1.2 Norton's Theorem — Underlying Logic

1. **Short-circuit the load terminals A–B.**
2. **Measure (or calculate) the short-circuit current $I_{SC}$** flowing through the short. This becomes $I_N$.
3. **Apply the same deactivation rules** as in Thevenin to find $R_N$.
4. **Reconstruct the equivalent:** current source $I_N$ in parallel with $R_N$ feeding $R_L$.

### 2.1.3 Source Transformation (Thevenin ↔ Norton Link)

Thevenin and Norton equivalents are **dual representations of the same network**. The relationship between them is called **Source Transformation**.

### 2.1.4 Maximum Power Transfer Theorem — Underlying Logic

1. **Thevenize** the network seen by the load.
2. The load current is $I_L = \dfrac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L}$.
3. The power delivered to the load is $P_L = I_L^2 \cdot R_L = \dfrac{V_{Th}^2 \cdot R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^2}$.
4. **Take derivative $\dfrac{dP_L}{dR_L} = 0$** and solve to find the optimum.
5. The optimum occurs at $R_L = R_{Th}$.
6. Substituting back gives $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{Th}^2}{4 R_{Th}}$.
7. **Efficiency at MPT:** $\eta = 50\%$ — half the power is dissipated in $R_{Th}$ itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off at MPT:** Maximum power transfer *always* implies **only 50% efficiency**. In power systems (grid transmission) we operate far from MPT for efficiency; in communication circuits (antenna matching) we operate *at* MPT for signal strength.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Thevenin Voltage | $V_{Th} = V_{OC}$ (open-circuit voltage at load terminals) | V | Measured with high-Z voltmeter |
| 2 | Thevenin Resistance | $R_{Th} = \dfrac{V_{OC}}{I_{SC}}$ (practical method) | Ω | Measured with sources deactivated for analytical method |
| 3 | Norton Current | $I_N = I_{SC}$ (short-circuit current at load terminals) | A | Measured with low-Z ammeter |
| 4 | Norton Resistance | $R_N = R_{Th}$ | Ω | Same deactivation rules |
| 5 | Source Transformation (V → I) | $I_N = \dfrac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th}}$ | A | — |
| 6 | Source Transformation (I → V) | $V_{Th} = I_N \cdot R_N$ | V | — |
| 7 | Load Current (Thevenin form) | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L}$ | A | Series loop equation |
| 8 | Load Voltage (Thevenin form) | $V_L = V_{Th} \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_{Th} + R_L}$ | V | Voltage divider |
| 9 | Load Power | $P_L = I_L^2 \cdot R_L$ | W | Dissipated in load |
| 10 | MPT Condition | $R_L = R_{Th}$ | Ω | Equal source and load impedance |
| 11 | Maximum Power | $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{Th}^2}{4 R_{Th}}$ | W | Occurs at MPT condition |
| 12 | MPT Efficiency | $\eta_{MPT} = 50\%$ | % | Fixed, independent of $R_{Th}$ |
| 13 | Half-Power Points | $R_L = R_{Th} \pm R_{Th} = 2R_{Th}$ or $\dfrac{R_{Th}}{2}$ | Ω | Where $P_L = \dfrac{P_{max}}{2}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The condition $R_L = R_{Th}$ gives maximum power, **NOT maximum efficiency**. Efficiency drops to 50%. If the question asks "maximum efficiency" the answer is $\eta \to 100\%$ as $R_L \to \infty$ (no current, no loss).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Thevenin / Norton / MPT |
|---|---|
| **Power Systems** | Modelling transformers, transmission lines as Thevenin sources for fault analysis. |
| **Electronics** | Output stage of an amplifier = Thevenin source; speaker is the load. |
| **Communication** | Antenna impedance must match transmission line ($R_L = R_{Th}$) for maximum signal transfer. |
| **Battery Modelling** | A battery is modelled as $V_{Th}$ (EMF) with internal resistance $R_{Th}$. |
| **Photovoltaic Systems** | Solar cells operate near MPT — Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) algorithms. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: MPT from Power Equation

Starting from the Thevenin equivalent, the load power expression is derived as follows.

The load current in the Thevenin series loop is:

$$I_L = \frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L}$$

**Step 1 —** Substitute this current into the power equation $P_L = I_L^2 \cdot R_L$:

$$P_L = \left(\frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L}\right)^2 \cdot R_L$$

**Step 2 —** Expand and rearrange into a form suitable for differentiation:

$$P_L = \frac{V_{Th}^2 \cdot R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^2}$$

**Step 3 —** To find the maximum, differentiate $P_L$ with respect to $R_L$ and equate to zero. Using the quotient rule:

$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = V_{Th}^2 \cdot \frac{(R_{Th} + R_L)^2 \cdot 1 - R_L \cdot 2(R_{Th} + R_L)}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^4} = 0$$

**Step 4 —** Simplify the numerator (denominator is always positive and non-zero):

$$(R_{Th} + R_L)^2 - 2 R_L (R_{Th} + R_L) = 0$$

**Step 5 —** Factor out the common term $(R_{Th} + R_L)$:

$$(R_{Th} + R_L) \left[ (R_{Th} + R_L) - 2 R_L \right] = 0$$

**Step 6 —** Since $R_{Th} + R_L \neq 0$, the second factor must be zero:

$$R_{Th} + R_L - 2 R_L = 0$$

**Step 7 —** Solve for $R_L$:

$$\boxed{R_L = R_{Th}}$$

**Step 8 —** Substitute $R_L = R_{Th}$ back into the power expression:

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{Th}^2 \cdot R_{Th}}{(R_{Th} + R_{Th})^2} = \frac{V_{Th}^2 \cdot R_{Th}}{4 R_{Th}^2}$$

**Step 9 —** Simplify:

$$\boxed{P_{max} = \frac{V_{Th}^2}{4 R_{Th}}}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Complete Numerical Solution

### 3.2.1 Given Circuit

A linear DC network consists of:

- Source voltage: $V_S = 12 \text{ V}$
- Series resistor: $R_1 = 4 \text{ Ω}$
- Parallel resistor (across load terminals): $R_2 = 6 \text{ Ω}$
- Load resistor (variable): $R_L$ connected across terminals A–B

We must verify Thevenin's theorem, find Norton's equivalent, and confirm the Maximum Power Transfer theorem.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

#### PART A — Calculation of Thevenin Equivalent

**Step 1 — Open-circuit voltage $V_{OC}$:**

With $R_L$ removed, no current flows in the circuit because $R_2$ alone forms a closed loop with the source. Wait — actually current does flow through $R_1$ and $R_2$ in series.

The series current when $R_L = \infty$:

$$I_{series} = \frac{V_S}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{12}{4 + 6} = \frac{12}{10} = 1.2 \text{ A}$$

The open-circuit voltage across A–B equals the voltage across $R_2$ (voltage divider):

$$V_{OC} = V_S \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 12 \cdot \frac{6}{4 + 6} = \frac{72}{10} = 7.2 \text{ V}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{V_{Th} = V_{OC} = 7.2 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 2 — Thevenin resistance $R_{Th}$:**

Deactivate the voltage source (replace $V_S$ with a short circuit). Looking back into terminals A–B, $R_1$ and $R_2$ appear in parallel:

$$R_{Th} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{R_1 \cdot R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{4 \times 6}{4 + 6} = \frac{24}{10} = 2.4 \text{ Ω}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{R_{Th} = 2.4 \text{ Ω}}$$

#### PART B — Calculation of Norton Equivalent

**Step 3 — Short-circuit current $I_{SC}$:**

With terminals A–B shorted, $R_2$ is bypassed. Current flows only through $R_1$:

$$I_{SC} = \frac{V_S}{R_1} = \frac{12}{4} = 3 \text{ A}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{I_N = I_{SC} = 3 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 4 — Verify Source Transformation:**

$$I_N = \frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th}} = \frac{7.2}{2.4} = 3 \text{ A} \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms **Thevenin and Norton are dual representations** of the same network. Also $R_N = R_{Th} = 2.4 \text{ Ω}$.

#### PART C — Maximum Power Transfer Verification

**Step 5 — Optimal load resistance:**

By MPT theorem:

$$R_L^{opt} = R_{Th} = 2.4 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum power:**

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{Th}^2}{4 R_{Th}} = \frac{(7.2)^2}{4 \times 2.4} = \frac{51.84}{9.6} = 5.4 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7 — Verification table** — Vary $R_L$ and compute $P_L$ to verify the maximum occurs at $R_L = 2.4 \text{ Ω}$:

| $R_L$ (Ω) | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L}$ (A) | $P_L = I_L^2 \cdot R_L$ (W) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | $7.2 / 2.9 = 2.483$ | $3.083$ |
| 1.0 | $7.2 / 3.4 = 2.118$ | $4.485$ |
| 1.5 | $7.2 / 3.9 = 1.846$ | $5.114$ |
| 2.0 | $7.2 / 4.4 = 1.636$ | $5.358$ |
| **2.4** | $7.2 / 4.8 = 1.500$ | **5.400** ← Maximum |
| 3.0 | $7.2 / 5.4 = 1.333$ | $5.333$ |
| 4.0 | $7.2 / 6.4 = 1.125$ | $5.063$ |
| 6.0 | $7.2 / 8.4 = 0.857$ | $4.408$ |
| 10.0 | $7.2 / 12.4 = 0.581$ | $3.371$ |

**Verification confirmed:** Power peaks at $R_L = 2.4 \text{ Ω}$ with $P_{max} = 5.4 \text{ W}$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Experimental Verification Procedure

| Step | Action | Instrument Used | Reading Recorded | Use in Verification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Connect circuit as per diagram; keep $R_L$ disconnected | DMM (Voltmeter mode) | — | Setup |
| 2 | Measure voltage across A–B (no load) | Voltmeter | $V_{OC} = 7.2$ V | $V_{Th} = V_{OC}$ |
| 3 | Connect ammeter across A–B (short-circuit) | Ammeter | $I_{SC} = 3.0$ A | $I_N = I_{SC}$ |
| 4 | Compute $R_{Th} = V_{OC} / I_{SC}$ | Calculation | $2.4$ Ω | Verification of $R_{Th}$ |
| 5 | Build Thevenin equivalent (separate) with measured $V_{Th}$, $R_{Th}$ | Variable DC source + resistor | — | — |
| 6 | Connect $R_L = 100$ Ω to original circuit; measure $V_L$, $I_L$ | Voltmeter + Ammeter | $V_L$, $I_L$ | Compare with Thevenin equivalent's $V_L$, $I_L$ |
| 7 | Repeat step 6 for $R_L = 220$ Ω, 330 Ω, 470 Ω, 1 kΩ | DMM | Tabulate | Build V–I characteristics |
| 8 | Compare original circuit V–I curve with Thevenin equivalent V–I curve | Plot/graph | Overlay | **Theorem verified if curves match** |
| 9 | Vary $R_L$ over a wide range; compute $P_L$ for each | Calculation | Tabulate $P_L$ vs $R_L$ | Identify peak |
| 10 | Confirm peak occurs at $R_L \approx R_{Th}$ | Graph | $P_{max}$ at $R_L = R_{Th}$ | **MPT verified** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Original Network → Thevenin Equivalent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LINEAR TWO TERMINAL NETWORK N]:::orig -->|Remove RL| B[TERMINALS A AND B OPEN]
    B -->|Measure| C[VOC OPEN CIRCUIT VOLTAGE]
    B -->|Deactivate Sources| D[RTH EQUIVALENT RESISTANCE]
    C --> E[VTH = VOC]
    D --> F[RTH VALUE IN OHMS]
    E --> G[THEVENIN EQUIVALENT CIRCUIT]
    F --> G
    G --> H[VTH IN SERIES WITH RTH FEEDING RL]

    classDef orig fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Source Transformation Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[VTH VOLTAGE SOURCE]:::vt -->|Convert| Q1[IN NORTON FORM]
    P2[RTH SERIES RESISTOR]:::vt -->|Becomes| Q2[IN PARALLEL WITH IN]
    Q1 --> R1[IN = VTH BY RTH]
    Q2 --> R2[RN = RTH]
    R1 --> S[FINAL NORTON EQUIVALENT]
    R2 --> S
    S --> T[CURRENT SOURCE IN IN PARALLEL WITH RN FEEDING RL]

    classDef vt fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Maximum Power Transfer Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[START EXPERIMENT] --> S2[MEASURE VOC]
    S2 --> S3[MEASURE ISC]
    S3 --> S4[CALCULATE RTH = VOC BY ISC]
    S4 --> S5[CONNECT VARIABLE RL]
    S5 --> S6[MEASURE VL AND IL FOR EACH RL]
    S6 --> S7[COMPUTE PL = IL SQUARED INTO RL]
    S7 --> S8[PLOT PL VS RL CURVE]
    S8 --> S9[IDENTIFY PEAK POWER PMAX]
    S9 --> S10{CHECK RL AT PEAK EQUALS RTH}
    S10 -->|YES| S11[MPT VERIFIED]
    S10 -->|NO| S12[REPEAT WITH FINER RL STEPS]

    classDef startNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef checkNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class S1 startNode
    class S10 checkNode
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Complete Experimental Test Bench

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PS[POWER SUPPLY SECTION]
        V1[DC REGULATED POWER SUPPLY 0 TO 30V]:::ps
    end

    subgraph NET[NETWORK UNDER TEST]
        R1A[RESISTOR R1]:::net
        R2A[RESISTOR R2]:::net
    end

    subgraph LOAD[LOAD SECTION VARIABLE]
        RLV[DECADE RESISTANCE BOX]:::load
    end

    subgraph MEAS[MEASUREMENT INSTRUMENTS]
        V2[DIGITAL VOLTMETER ACROSS LOAD]:::meas
        A1[DIGITAL AMMETER IN SERIES WITH LOAD]:::meas
    end

    V1 --> R1A --> R2A --> V1
    R2A -.->|TERMINAL A| V2
    V2 -.->|TERMINAL B| A1
    A1 --> RLV
    RLV --> R2A

    classDef ps fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef net fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef meas fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.5 Conceptual Schematic — Thevenin & Norton Dual Forms (Tabular Block View)

| Original Linear Network | Thevenin Form | Norton Form |
|---|---|---|
| Contains $V_S$, $R_1$, $R_2$, …, $R_n$ | One $V_{Th}$ + one $R_{Th}$ (series) | One $I_N$ + one $R_N$ (parallel) |
| $V_{Th} = V_{OC}$ measured at A–B | $R_{Th}$ = resistance seen with sources zeroed | $I_N = I_{SC}$ measured at A–B |
| $R_{Th} = R_N$ | $V_{Th} = I_N \cdot R_N$ | $I_N = V_{Th} / R_{Th}$ |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State Thevenin's theorem. Mention the two quantities required to construct a Thevenin equivalent circuit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Thevenin's Theorem (2 marks):** Any linear bilateral two-terminal network containing voltage sources, current sources, and resistors can be replaced by an equivalent circuit consisting of a single voltage source $V_{Th}$ in series with a single resistor $R_{Th}$.

**Two Quantities Required (1 mark):**
1. **Thevenin voltage $V_{Th}$** — equal to the open-circuit voltage $V_{OC}$ measured across the load terminals.
2. **Thevenin resistance $R_{Th}$** — equal to the equivalent resistance looking back into the network from the open-circuited terminals with all independent sources deactivated.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the Maximum Power Transfer theorem. What is the efficiency at maximum power transfer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (2 marks):** For a linear bilateral network represented by its Thevenin equivalent ($V_{Th}$, $R_{Th}$), the power delivered to a variable load resistance $R_L$ is maximum when $R_L = R_{Th}$. The maximum power is $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{Th}^2}{4 R_{Th}}$.

**Efficiency (1 mark):** At maximum power transfer, the efficiency is exactly **50%**, because the same amount of power is dissipated in the Thevenin resistance as is delivered to the load.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**For the DC network shown (assume the worked-example circuit with $V_S = 12$ V, $R_1 = 4$ Ω in series, $R_2 = 6$ Ω in parallel across load terminals A–B):**

**(a)** Determine the Thevenin equivalent circuit across terminals A–B. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the Norton equivalent of the same network and verify the source transformation relation. Compute the maximum power that can be delivered to a variable load $R_L$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Remove $R_L$ to make terminals A–B open (½ mark):**

With $R_L$ removed, the current path is $V_S \to R_1 \to R_2 \to$ back to $V_S$.

**Step 2 — Calculate the open-circuit voltage $V_{OC}$ (2 marks):**

The series current is:

$$I = \frac{V_S}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{12}{4 + 6} = 1.2 \text{ A}$$

The voltage across $R_2$ (which is the voltage across A–B) is:

$$V_{OC} = I \cdot R_2 = 1.2 \times 6 = 7.2 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Identify $V_{Th}$ (½ mark):**

$$\boxed{V_{Th} = V_{OC} = 7.2 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 4 — Deactivate $V_S$ (replace with short) (½ mark):**

With the source shorted, looking back from A–B, $R_1$ and $R_2$ are in parallel.

**Step 5 — Calculate $R_{Th}$ (2 marks):**

$$R_{Th} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{4 \times 6}{4 + 6} = \frac{24}{10} = 2.4 \text{ Ω}$$

$$\boxed{R_{Th} = 2.4 \text{ Ω}}$$

**Step 6 — Draw the Thevenin equivalent (1 mark):**

A voltage source $V_{Th} = 7.2$ V in series with $R_{Th} = 2.4$ Ω, with terminals A–B across which $R_L$ is connected.

---

#### **Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Short terminals A–B and find $I_{SC}$ (2 marks):**

With A–B shorted, $R_2$ is bypassed. The current through the short is:

$$I_{SC} = \frac{V_S}{R_1} = \frac{12}{4} = 3 \text{ A}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{I_N = I_{SC} = 3 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 2 — Norton resistance (1 mark):**

$$R_N = R_{Th} = 2.4 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 3 — Verify source transformation (2 marks):**

$$I_N = \frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th}} = \frac{7.2}{2.4} = 3 \text{ A} = I_{SC} \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms the Thevenin and Norton equivalents are valid dual representations.

**Step 4 — Maximum power calculation (2 marks):**

By MPT, optimum load is $R_L = R_{Th} = 2.4$ Ω:

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{Th}^2}{4 R_{Th}} = \frac{(7.2)^2}{4 \times 2.4} = \frac{51.84}{9.6} = 5.4 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} = 5.4 \text{ W} \text{ at } R_L = 2.4 \text{ Ω}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat procedure how Thevenin's theorem is verified experimentally in the laboratory. List the instruments required and the observations to be recorded. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DC source of 15 V is connected in series with $R_1 = 5$ Ω and $R_2 = 10$ Ω. A load $R_L$ is connected across $R_2$. Determine (i) Thevenin voltage and Thevenin resistance across the load terminals, (ii) the value of $R_L$ for maximum power transfer, and (iii) the value of maximum power. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 marks**

**Experimental Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Circuit assembly (1 mark):** Connect the given network on a breadboard as per the circuit diagram. Identify load terminals A–B. Keep load $R_L$ disconnected.

2. **Measurement of $V_{OC}$ (1 mark):** Connect a digital voltmeter (high impedance) across terminals A–B. Switch on the DC supply and record $V_{OC} = V_{Th}$.

3. **Measurement of $I_{SC}$ (1 mark):** Disconnect the voltmeter. Connect a digital ammeter (low impedance) across A–B. Record $I_{SC} = I_N$.

4. **Compute $R_{Th}$ (½ mark):** Calculate $R_{Th} = V_{OC} / I_{SC}$.

5. **Verification with load (1 mark):** Construct a separate Thevenin equivalent circuit using a variable DC source set to $V_{Th}$ in series with a resistor equal to $R_{Th}$. Connect various known $R_L$ values (e.g., 100 Ω, 220 Ω, 330 Ω, 470 Ω, 1 kΩ) one at a time to **both** the original network and the Thevenin equivalent. Measure $V_L$ and $I_L$ in each case. If the values match (within tolerance), Thevenin's theorem is verified.

6. **Tabulation and plotting (½ mark):** Tabulate observations; plot $V_L$ vs $I_L$ for both circuits — the curves should overlap.

**Instruments Required (1 mark):** DC regulated power supply, breadboard, fixed resistors ($R_1$, $R_2$, etc.), decade resistance box, digital voltmeter, digital ammeter, connecting wires.

**Precautions (1 mark):** Always start with supply voltage at zero and increase slowly. Ensure correct polarity. Use appropriate meter ranges to avoid damage.

---

#### **Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $V_S = 15$ V, $R_1 = 5$ Ω, $R_2 = 10$ Ω across which $R_L$ is connected.

**(i) Thevenin Equivalent (3 marks):**

Open-circuit voltage across $R_2$ (voltage divider):

$$V_{Th} = V_{OC} = V_S \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 15 \cdot \frac{10}{5 + 10} = 15 \cdot \frac{10}{15} = 10 \text{ V}$$

Thevenin resistance (sources deactivated, $R_1 \parallel R_2$):

$$R_{Th} = \frac{R_1 \cdot R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{5 \times 10}{5 + 10} = \frac{50}{15} = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.333 \text{ Ω}$$

$$\boxed{V_{Th} = 10 \text{ V}, \quad R_{Th} = 3.333 \text{ Ω}}$$

**(ii) Optimum Load (2 marks):**

By Maximum Power Transfer theorem:

$$\boxed{R_L^{opt} = R_{Th} = 3.333 \text{ Ω}}$$

**(iii) Maximum Power (2 marks):**

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{Th}^2}{4 R_{Th}} = \frac{(10)^2}{4 \times 3.333} = \frac{100}{13.333} = 7.5 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} = 7.5 \text{ W}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where KTU students lose marks:**
>
> 1. **Deactivation Mistake (–2 marks):** Replacing a voltage source with an *open circuit* instead of a *short circuit*, or a current source with a *short* instead of an *open*. Memorize: **V-source → Short, I-source → Open.**
>
> 2. **Confusing $V_{Th}$ with Source Voltage (–2 marks):** $V_{Th}$ is the voltage at the *load terminals*, not the source EMF. Apply the voltage divider rule correctly.
>
> 3. **MPT vs Efficiency Confusion (–2 marks):** Writing "$R_L = R_{Th}$ gives maximum efficiency" — **WRONG**. MPT gives *maximum power*, but efficiency is only 50%. Maximum efficiency occurs as $R_L \to \infty$.
>
> 4. **Wrong Units or Missing Units (–1 mark):** Always quote units (V, A, Ω, W). A numerically correct answer without units loses a mark in KTU valuation.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to Verify (–1 mark):** KTU expects a comparison of *original vs. Thevenin equivalent* measurements, not just calculation. Always tabulate and plot.
>
> 6. **Parallel vs Series in $R_{Th}$ Calculation (–2 marks):** After deactivating sources, $R_1$ and $R_2$ become parallel only if one end of each was at the source terminal. Draw the deactivated network carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Thevenin / Norton / MPT Lab**

- **Thevenin Theorem:** Replace any linear 2-terminal network with one voltage source $V_{Th}$ in series with one resistor $R_{Th}$.
- **$V_{Th} = V_{OC}$** at the load terminals (with load removed).
- **$R_{Th}$:** Deactivate V-sources (short) and I-sources (open), then find equivalent resistance looking back into the open terminals.
- **Norton Theorem:** Dual of Thevenin — current source $I_N$ in parallel with $R_N$.
- **$I_N = I_{SC}$** at the load terminals (load shorted).
- **$R_N = R_{Th}$.**
- **Source Transformation:** $I_N = V_{Th} / R_{Th}$ and $V_{Th} = I_N \cdot R_N$.
- **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem:** $P_L$ is maximum when $R_L = R_{Th}$.
- **$P_{max} = V_{Th}^2 / (4 R_{Th})$** — independent of source magnitude other than through $V_{Th}$.
- **Efficiency at MPT = 50%** — half the power is lost in $R_{Th}$.
- **Experimental Verification:** Measure $V_{OC}$, $I_{SC}$; compute $R_{Th} = V_{OC}/I_{SC}$; build separate Thevenin circuit; compare $V_L$, $I_L$ for various $R_L$.
- **MPT Verification:** Plot $P_L$ vs $R_L$; peak occurs at $R_L = R_{Th}$.
- **Practical Method to Find $R_{Th}$:** Measure $V_{OC}$ (voltmeter) and $I_{SC}$ (ammeter) — useful when circuit has dependent sources where direct analytical deactivation fails.
- **Polarity Convention:** $V_{Th}$ polarity = polarity of $V_{OC}$ measured with the same voltmeter leads.
- **Standard Tolerances in Lab:** Resistors ±5%, voltmeter ±0.5%, ammeter ±1% — these tolerances justify small deviations between theoretical and measured values.
- **Always Quote Units:** V for voltage, A for current, Ω for resistance, W for power.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of impedance, admittance, and power factor in RLC series/parallel circuit and study the effect of reactive components on power factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL307 - CIRCUITS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of impedance, admittance, and power factor in RLC series/parallel circuit and study the effect of reactive components on power factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In an alternating current (AC) circuit containing a Resistor (R), Inductor (L) and Capacitor (C), three foundational parameters characterize the circuit's opposition to current flow and its phase behavior:

- **Impedance (Z):** The total sinusoidal steady-state opposition offered by a passive two-terminal network to the flow of alternating current, expressed as a complex quantity having both magnitude and phase. The SI unit is **Ohm (Ω)**.

- **Admittance (Y):** The mathematical reciprocal of impedance, representing the ease with which a circuit allows current to flow. It is a complex quantity, and its SI unit is **Siemens (S)** or **mho (℧)**.

- **Power Factor (cos φ or pf):** The cosine of the angle by which the current leads or lags the applied voltage in an AC circuit. It is the ratio of **True Power (W)** to **Apparent Power (VA)**, indicating how effectively electrical power is converted into useful work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A circuit is classified as **resistive (pf = 1)**, **inductive (lagging pf)**, or **capacitive (leading pf)** based on the sign of the net reactance. Power factor correction (PFC) using capacitors is a direct industrial application studied under this experiment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water-Pipe Model

Imagine a closed water-pump loop carrying water (current) driven by a piston pump (AC source):

- **Resistor (R):** A narrow pipe section. It restricts water flow but does not store any energy. Energy is dissipated as heat (real power). The water (current) is in step with the pump push (voltage).
- **Inductor (L):** A heavy flywheel connected to the pipe. It resists *changes* in flow. When the pump pushes, the flywheel takes time to spin up, so the water flow **lags** behind the push. Energy is alternately stored in the magnetic field and returned to the source.
- **Capacitor (C):** A flexible diaphragm tank. It fills up before water can pass through freely. When the pump pushes, the tank first gets filled, then releases water, so the flow **leads** the push. Energy is alternately stored in the electric field and returned.
- **Impedance (Z):** The *total* opposition from narrow pipe + flywheel + diaphragm combined.
- **Power Factor:** How "in sync" the actual water delivery is with the actual pump effort. A perfect sync = pf of 1; a poorly timed delivery = low pf.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reactive components (L and C) do not consume net energy** — they only *store* and *return* it. However, they increase the magnitude of current drawn from the source, which is why reactive components *degrade* the power factor and waste transmission capacity.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Highlighted)

- **Frequency (f):** **50 Hz** is the Indian standard supply frequency.
- **Angular frequency (ω):** $\omega = 2\pi f$ in **rad/s**.
- **Inductive Reactance (X_L):** $\omega L$ in **Ω**.
- **Capacitive Reactance (X_C):** $1 / \omega C$ in **Ω**.
- **Magnitude of Impedance (vert Z vert):** $\sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}$ in **Ω**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 RLC Series Circuit — Phasor Reasoning

In a series RLC circuit, the same current **I** flows through all three elements, while the voltage across each element has a distinct phase relationship with the current.

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

1. The voltage across the resistor $V_R = IR$ is **in-phase** with the current (real axis).
2. The voltage across the inductor $V_L = I X_L$ **leads** the current by $90^\circ$ (positive imaginary axis).
3. The voltage across the capacitor $V_C = I X_C$ **lags** the current by $90^\circ$ (negative imaginary axis).
4. The total supply voltage $V$ is the **phasor sum** of $V_R$, $V_L$, and $V_C$.
5. Defining the **net reactance** $X = X_L - X_C$, the complex impedance is:

$$Z = R + jX_L - jX_C = R + j(X_L - X_C)$$

6. Three cases arise:
   - **If $X_L > X_C$:** Circuit behaves **inductively** (lagging pf).
   - **If $X_L < X_C$:** Circuit behaves **capacitively** (leading pf).
   - **If $X_L = X_C$:** **Resonance** — circuit is purely resistive, $Z = R$, pf = 1 (Unity).

### 2.2 RLC Parallel Circuit — Phasor Reasoning

In a parallel RLC circuit, the same voltage **V** appears across each branch, while branch currents are independent.

1. Current through resistor: $I_R = V/R$ — **in-phase** with voltage.
2. Current through inductor: $I_L = V / X_L$ — **lags** voltage by $90^\circ$ (negative imaginary).
3. Current through capacitor: $I_C = V / X_C$ — **leads** voltage by $90^\circ$ (positive imaginary).
4. Total current: $I = I_R - j I_L + j I_C = V [ (1/R) + j(\omega C - 1/\omega L) ]$.
5. Complex admittance:

$$Y = G + j B = \frac{1}{R} + j\left(\omega C - \frac{1}{\omega L}\right)$$

6. **Resonance condition:** $\omega_0 = 1 / \sqrt{LC}$ — the susceptance is zero and admittance is minimum (= $1/R$), so the line current is minimum.

### 2.3 Power Factor — Physical Meaning

The instantaneous power $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$ in an AC circuit with sinusoidal excitation contains:

- A **DC (average) component** = True Power $P = V I \cos\phi$ (in Watts).
- A **double-frequency oscillating component** = Reactive Power $Q = V I \sin\phi$ (in VAR).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apparent Power** $S = V I$ (in VA) is the product of RMS values. The ratio $P / S = \cos\phi$ is the **power factor**. Industrial consumers are penalized for **low (lagging) pf** because it increases $I^2 R$ transmission losses without delivering more real power.

### 2.4 Effect of Reactive Components on Power Factor

| Action on Circuit | Resulting Power Factor Behavior | Real-World Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Add pure Inductor (L) | pf decreases, becomes **lagging** | Motors, transformers cause lagging pf |
| Add pure Capacitor (C) | pf increases toward 1, becomes **leading** | Capacitor banks installed for power factor correction |
| Add both L and C tuned to resonance | pf = **unity (1.0)** | Used in tuning circuits, filters |
| R only | pf = 1 (unity) | Purely resistive loads (heaters, incandescent lamps) |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inductive Reactance | $X_L = 2\pi f L = \omega L$ | $\Omega$ | Increases linearly with $f$ |
| 2 | Capacitive Reactance | $X_C = 1 / (2\pi f C) = 1 / (\omega C)$ | $\Omega$ | Decreases with $f$ |
| 3 | Series Impedance (Magnitude) | $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}$ | $\Omega$ | Phasor form: $Z = R + j(X_L - X_C)$ |
| 4 | Series Impedance (Phase) | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_L - X_C}{R}\right)$ | degrees or rad | Sign of $\phi$ → inductive (+) or capacitive (-) |
| 5 | Parallel Admittance (Magnitude) | $\vert Y \vert = \sqrt{G^2 + (B_C - B_L)^2}$ | S (Siemens) | $G = 1/R$, $B_L = 1/X_L$, $B_C = 1/X_C$ |
| 6 | Parallel Impedance (Magnitude) | $\vert Z \vert = 1 / \vert Y \vert$ | $\Omega$ | Useful for converting admittance to impedance |
| 7 | Power Factor | $\cos\phi = R / \vert Z \vert = P / S$ | dimensionless | $\phi = \tan^{-1}(X/R)$ |
| 8 | True Power | $P = V I \cos\phi = I^2 R$ | W (Watts) | Resistive power consumed |
| 9 | Reactive Power | $Q = V I \sin\phi = I^2 X$ | VAR | Power stored and returned by L, C |
| 10 | Apparent Power | $S = V I = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ | VA | Geometric sum of P and Q |
| 11 | Resonant Frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Hz | $X_L = X_C$ at this frequency |
| 12 | Quality Factor (Series) | $Q_{fac} = (1/R)\sqrt{L/C}$ | dimensionless | Sharpness of resonance |
| 13 | Power Factor Correction Capacitor | $C = P (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2) / (2\pi f V^2)$ | F | $C$ required to raise pf from $\cos\phi_1$ to $\cos\phi_2$ |
| 14 | Line Current | $I = V / \vert Z \vert$ | A | Ohm's law in AC phasor form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Board Tip:** Always state whether the computed power factor is **lagging** (inductive) or **leading** (capacitive). A bare number like "0.7" without a qualifier will cost marks.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Power Factor Correction (PFC):** Heavy inductive loads (induction motors, arc furnaces) draw lagging reactive current. Shunt capacitor banks are switched in to compensate, raising the pf close to unity and reducing electricity bills.
- **Tuned RLC Filters:** In power systems, series/parallel RLC circuits are used as harmonic filters to block or pass specific frequency bands.
- **Oscillator Circuits:** LC networks determine the oscillation frequency in radio transmitters, signal generators, and crystal-locked timing circuits.
- **Wattmeter Readings:** In the lab, a wattmeter reads true power $P$, a voltmeter reads $V$, and an ammeter reads $I$, so $\cos\phi = P / (V I)$ is verified experimentally.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Series RLC Impedance from Phasor Sum

**Starting point:** Three voltages $V_R$, $V_L$, $V_C$ add phasorially.

**Step 1 — Write the branch voltages using a common reference current $I$:**

$$V_R = I \cdot R, \quad V_L = I \cdot j \omega L = I \cdot j X_L, \quad V_C = I \cdot \frac{1}{j \omega C} = -I \cdot j X_C$$

**Step 2 — Apply KVL around the series loop:**

$$V = V_R + V_L + V_C = I[R + j X_L - j X_C] = I \cdot Z$$

**Step 3 — Extract the complex impedance:**

$$Z = R + j (X_L - X_C)$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude and Phase:**

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}, \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_L - X_C}{R}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Express in polar form:**

$$Z = \vert Z \vert \angle \phi$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A positive $\phi$ means current **lags** voltage (inductive), negative $\phi$ means current **leads** voltage (capacitive). When $X_L = X_C$, $\phi = 0$ and $Z = R$ → **resonance**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Parallel RLC Admittance

**Starting point:** Voltage $V$ common across all branches.

**Step 1 — Branch currents using a common reference voltage $V$:**

$$I_R = \frac{V}{R}, \quad I_L = \frac{V}{j \omega L} = -j \frac{V}{X_L}, \quad I_C = j \omega C V = j \frac{V}{X_C}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the top node:**

$$I = I_R + I_L + I_C = V \left[ \frac{1}{R} - \frac{j}{X_L} + \frac{j}{X_C} \right] = V \cdot Y$$

**Step 3 — Identify the complex admittance:**

$$Y = \frac{1}{R} + j \left( \omega C - \frac{1}{\omega L} \right) = G + j B$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude and Phase of admittance:**

$$\vert Y \vert = \sqrt{G^2 + B^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{R}\right)^2 + \left(\omega C - \frac{1}{\omega L}\right)^2}$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega C - 1/\omega L}{1/R}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Convert to impedance if needed:**

$$Z = \frac{1}{Y}, \quad \vert Z \vert = \frac{1}{\vert Y \vert}$$

### 3.3 Power Triangle and Power Factor Derivation

**Step 1 — Express complex power:**

$$S = V \cdot I^* = V I (\cos\phi + j \sin\phi) = P + j Q$$

**Step 2 — Identify the components:**

$$P = V I \cos\phi, \quad Q = V I \sin\phi$$

**Step 3 — Form the right-angled power triangle:**

$$\tan\phi = \frac{Q}{P} = \frac{X}{R}, \quad S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$$

**Step 4 — Power factor expression:**

$$\cos\phi = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{R}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{G}{\vert Y \vert}$$

### 3.4 Solved Numerical — RLC Series Circuit

> **Given:** $R = 30 \,\Omega$, $L = 100 \,\text{mH}$, $C = 50 \,\mu F$, $V_s = 230 \,\text{V (rms)}$, $f = 50 \,\text{Hz}$.

**Step 1 — Compute angular frequency:**

$$\omega = 2 \pi f = 2 \pi \times 50 = 314.159 \,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute inductive reactance:**

$$X_L = \omega L = 314.159 \times 0.1 = 31.416 \,\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Compute capacitive reactance:**

$$X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C} = \frac{1}{314.159 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}} = 63.662 \,\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Net reactance (capacitive dominates):**

$$X = X_L - X_C = 31.416 - 63.662 = -32.246 \,\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude of impedance:**

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} = \sqrt{30^2 + (-32.246)^2} = \sqrt{900 + 1039.8} = \sqrt{1939.8} = 44.05 \,\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Phase angle:**

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-32.246}{30}\right) = -47.06^\circ$$

**Step 7 — Power factor (leading, since $\phi < 0$):**

$$\cos\phi = \cos(47.06^\circ) = 0.681 \text{ (leading)}$$

**Step 8 — RMS current:**

$$I = \frac{V_s}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{230}{44.05} = 5.22 \,\text{A}$$

**Step 9 — True power consumed:**

$$P = I^2 R = (5.22)^2 \times 30 = 27.24 \times 30 = 817.3 \,\text{W}$$

**Step 10 — Reactive power:**

$$Q = I^2 X = (5.22)^2 \times (-32.246) = -878.4 \,\text{VAR (capacitive)}$$

**Step 11 — Apparent power:**

$$S = V_s I = 230 \times 5.22 = 1200.6 \,\text{VA}$$

**Verification:** $\sqrt{P^2 + Q^2} = \sqrt{817.3^2 + 878.4^2} = \sqrt{667973 + 771587} = \sqrt{1439560} = 1200.0 \,\text{VA}$ ✓

### 3.5 Solved Numerical — RLC Parallel Circuit

> **Given:** $R = 100 \,\Omega$, $L = 0.5 \,\text{H}$, $C = 10 \,\mu F$, $V = 200 \,\text{V (rms)}$, $f = 50 \,\text{Hz}$.

**Step 1 — Angular frequency:** $\omega = 314.159 \,\text{rad/s}$

**Step 2 — Conductance and Susceptances:**

$$G = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{100} = 0.0100 \,\text{S}$$

$$B_L = \frac{1}{\omega L} = \frac{1}{314.159 \times 0.5} = 0.006366 \,\text{S}$$

$$B_C = \omega C = 314.159 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 0.003142 \,\text{S}$$

**Step 3 — Net Susceptance (inductive dominates):**

$$B = B_C - B_L = 0.003142 - 0.006366 = -0.003224 \,\text{S}$$

**Step 4 — Admittance magnitude:**

$$\vert Y \vert = \sqrt{G^2 + B^2} = \sqrt{0.01^2 + 0.003224^2} = \sqrt{0.000100 + 0.0000104} = 0.01051 \,\text{S}$$

**Step 5 — Impedance magnitude:**

$$\vert Z \vert = \frac{1}{\vert Y \vert} = \frac{1}{0.01051} = 95.16 \,\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Phase angle and power factor:**

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{B}{G}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-0.003224}{0.01}\right) = -17.88^\circ$$

$$\cos\phi = 0.952 \text{ (lagging, since } B < 0 \text{, i.e., inductive)}$$

**Step 7 — Line current:**

$$I = V \cdot \vert Y \vert = 200 \times 0.01051 = 2.10 \,\text{A}$$

### 3.6 Power Factor Correction Numerical

> **Given:** A 3-phase load draws $P = 50 \,\text{kW}$ at $V_{LL} = 415 \,\text{V}$, $f = 50 \,\text{Hz}$, with existing pf = 0.70 lagging. Find the capacitance per phase to improve pf to 0.95 lagging.

**Step 1 — Initial phase angle:**

$$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.70) = 45.57^\circ, \quad \tan\phi_1 = 1.020$$

**Step 2 — Target phase angle:**

$$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19^\circ, \quad \tan\phi_2 = 0.329$$

**Step 3 — Reactive power to be supplied by capacitor bank (3-phase):**

$$Q_c = P (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2) = 50000 \times (1.020 - 0.329) = 34550 \,\text{VAR}$$

**Step 4 — Per-phase reactive power:**

$$Q_{c,ph} = \frac{Q_c}{3} = 11516.7 \,\text{VAR}$$

**Step 5 — Per-phase capacitance (star-connected bank, phase voltage $V_{ph} = 415 / \sqrt{3} = 239.6 \,\text{V}$):**

$$C = \frac{Q_{c,ph}}{2\pi f V_{ph}^2} = \frac{11516.7}{2\pi \times 50 \times (239.6)^2} = \frac{11516.7}{1804097} = 6.38 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{F} \approx 6380 \,\mu F$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — RLC Series Circuit

```mermaid
graph LR
  srcA[AC Voltage Source Vs] --> resA[Resistor R]
  resA --> indA[Inductor L with XL = omega L]
  indA --> capA[Capacitor C with XC = 1 over omega C]
  capA --> srcA
  resA -. VR = I times R .-> pRes[Phasor VR along Real axis]
  indA -. VL = I times XL leads by 90 .-> pInd[Phasor VL along Positive Imaginary axis]
  capA -. VC = I times XC lags by 90 .-> pCap[Phasor VC along Negative Imaginary axis]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — RLC Parallel Circuit

```mermaid
graph TD
  srcB[AC Voltage Source V] --> n1Top[Top Node N1]
  srcB --> n2Bot[Bottom Node N2]
  n1Top --> r1[Resistor R]
  r1 --> n2Bot
  n1Top --> l1[Inductor L]
  l1 --> n2Bot
  n1Top --> c1[Capacitor C]
  c1 --> n2Bot
  r1 -. IR in phase with V .-> iR[Phasor IR on Real axis]
  l1 -. IL lags V by 90 .-> iL[Phasor IL on Negative Imaginary axis]
  c1 -. IC leads V by 90 .-> iC[Phasor IC on Positive Imaginary axis]
```

### 4.3 Phasor Diagram — Series RLC (Inductive Case, $X_L > X_C$)

```mermaid
graph TD
  origin[Origin O] --> vr[VR along Real axis, magnitude IR]
  origin --> vl[VL along Positive Imaginary axis, magnitude IXL]
  origin --> vc[VC along Negative Imaginary axis, magnitude IXC]
  origin --> vnet[VNET along Real Positive Quadrant, magnitude V]
  origin --> iph[Current I along Real axis reference]
  vc -. phasor subtraction reduces net VL .-> vnet
```

### 4.4 Power Triangle Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
  pAxis[Real Power P along X axis] --> sH[Hypotenuse Apparent Power S]
  qAxis[Reactive Power Q along Y axis] --> sH
  sH -. cos phi equals P over S .-> pf[Power Factor cos phi]
  sH -. sin phi equals Q over S .-> pf
  pf --> tag{phi sign?}
  tag --> lag[Inductive Lagging pf when Q positive]
  tag --> lead[Capacitive Leading pf when Q negative]
  tag --> unity[Unity pf when phi equals zero]
```

### 4.5 Power Factor Correction — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
  load[Industrial Inductive Load Motor] -->|draws lagging reactive QL| bus[Common AC Bus]
  cap[Capacitor Bank Shunt Connected] -->|supplies leading Qc| bus
  bus -->|net reactive Qnet = QL minus Qc| source[Utility Grid]
  load -->|real power P| bus
  bus -. pf improves from cos phi1 to cos phi2 .-> result[Reduced Line Current and Lower I squared R Losses]
```

### 4.6 Experimental Setup Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
  acsrc[Variac AC Supply 0 to 230 V] --> ammeter[Ammeter A in series]
  ammeter --> r1exp[R]
  r1exp --> l1exp[L]
  l1exp --> c1exp[C]
  c1exp --> back[Return to Variac]
  acsrc --> voltmeter[Voltmeter V across source]
  acsrc -. across load .-> wattmeter[Wattmeter W reading P]
  back --> wattmeter
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
