## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
Define the terms **(a) Impedance**, **(b) Admittance**, and **(c) Power factor** as applied to a sinusoidal AC circuit. State the SI units.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Impedance (Z):** The total opposition offered by an AC circuit to the flow of alternating current, expressed as a complex quantity $Z = R + jX$, where $R$ is resistance and $X$ is net reactance. **SI unit: Ohm (Ω).**

**(b) Admittance (Y):** The reciprocal of impedance, $Y = 1/Z = G + jB$, where $G$ is conductance and $B$ is susceptance. It indicates the ease with which the circuit admits current. **SI unit: Siemens (S)** or **mho (℧)**.

**(c) Power Factor (cos φ):** The ratio of true power (in W) to apparent power (in VA), equal to $\cos\phi$ where $\phi$ is the phase angle between voltage and current. It is **dimensionless** with a value between 0 and 1. A leading pf indicates capacitive behavior; a lagging pf indicates inductive behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners award 1 mark per definition with the correct SI unit. Missing the unit alone costs 1 mark per sub-part.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
A series RLC circuit with $R = 40 \,\Omega$, $X_L = 70 \,\Omega$, and $X_C = 30 \,\Omega$ is connected across a 200 V, 50 Hz supply. Calculate the **(a) impedance magnitude**, **(b) current drawn**, and **(c) power factor**.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Net reactance:** $X = X_L - X_C = 70 - 30 = 40 \,\Omega$

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} = \sqrt{40^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{1600 + 1600} = \sqrt{3200} = 56.57 \,\Omega$$

**(b) RMS current:** $I = V / \vert Z \vert = 200 / 56.57 = 3.536 \,\text{A}$

**(c) Power factor:** $\cos\phi = R / \vert Z \vert = 40 / 56.57 = 0.707$ **lagging** (since $X_L > X_C$, circuit is inductive).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the impedance of a series RLC circuit. Draw its phasor diagram and discuss the three possible cases based on the relative magnitudes of $X_L$ and $X_C$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A series RLC circuit with $R = 20 \,\Omega$, $L = 80 \,\text{mH}$, and $C = 60 \,\mu F$ is connected across a $230 \,\text{V}$, $50 \,\text{Hz}$ supply. Calculate the **(i)** impedance, **(ii)** current, **(iii)** true power, **(iv)** reactive power, **(v)** apparent power, and **(vi)** power factor. Comment on whether the pf is leading or lagging. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Consider a series circuit with $R$, $L$, $C$ carrying the same current $I \angle 0^\circ$ (taken as reference).

Voltages across each element:

$$V_R = I R \angle 0^\circ, \quad V_L = I X_L \angle 90^\circ, \quad V_C = I X_C \angle -90^\circ$$

By KVL: $V = V_R + V_L + V_C = I (R + j X_L - j X_C) = I Z$

$$\boxed{Z = R + j (X_L - X_C)}$$

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}, \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_L - X_C}{R}\right)$$

**Three cases:**

| Case | Condition | Phase angle | Nature | Power factor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I | $X_L > X_C$ | $\phi > 0$ | Inductive | Lagging |
| II | $X_L < X_C$ | $\phi < 0$ | Capacitive | Leading |
| III | $X_L = X_C$ | $\phi = 0$ | Purely resistive (Resonance) | Unity |

The phasor diagram (drawn with $I$ along the reference real axis) shows $V_R$ along $I$, $V_L$ perpendicular upward, $V_C$ perpendicular downward, and the net $V$ tilted by angle $\phi$.

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

Given: $R = 20 \,\Omega$, $L = 80 \,\text{mH} = 0.08 \,\text{H}$, $C = 60 \,\mu F = 60 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{F}$, $V = 230 \,\text{V}$, $f = 50 \,\text{Hz}$.

**Step 1:** $\omega = 2 \pi \times 50 = 314.159 \,\text{rad/s}$

**Step 2:** $X_L = \omega L = 314.159 \times 0.08 = 25.13 \,\Omega$

**Step 3:** $X_C = 1 / (\omega C) = 1 / (314.159 \times 60 \times 10^{-6}) = 53.05 \,\Omega$

**Step 4:** $X = X_L - X_C = 25.13 - 53.05 = -27.92 \,\Omega$ (capacitive)

**Step 5 (i) Impedance:**

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{20^2 + (-27.92)^2} = \sqrt{400 + 779.5} = \sqrt{1179.5} = 34.34 \,\Omega$$

**Step 6 (ii) Current:**

$$I = 230 / 34.34 = 6.697 \,\text{A}$$

**Step 7 (iii) True Power:**

$$P = I^2 R = (6.697)^2 \times 20 = 44.85 \times 20 = 897.0 \,\text{W}$$

**Step 8 (iv) Reactive Power:**

$$Q = I^2 X = (6.697)^2 \times (-27.92) = 44.85 \times (-27.92) = -1252.2 \,\text{VAR}$$

(Negative sign → capacitive reactive power, i.e., leading.)

**Step 9 (v) Apparent Power:**

$$S = V I = 230 \times 6.697 = 1540.3 \,\text{VA}$$

**Step 10 (vi) Power factor:**

$$\cos\phi = P / S = 897.0 / 1540.3 = 0.582 \text{ (leading)}$$

Comment: Since $X_C > X_L$, the circuit is **capacitive** and the pf of 0.582 is **leading**.

---

#### **Question B** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the aid of a phasor diagram, derive the expression for admittance of a parallel RLC circuit and hence obtain the condition for resonance. Define the dynamic resistance at resonance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parallel RLC circuit consists of $R = 200 \,\Omega$, $L = 0.2 \,\text{H}$, and $C = 5 \,\mu F$, connected to a 100 V, 50 Hz AC supply. Determine the **(i)** admittance, **(ii)** line current, **(iii)** power factor, and **(iv)** the value of capacitance that must be connected in parallel to bring the pf to unity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

A parallel RLC circuit has a common voltage $V \angle 0^\circ$ across all branches.

Branch currents:

$$I_R = V/R \angle 0^\circ, \quad I_L = V/(j\omega L) = -jV/(\omega L), \quad I_C = j\omega C V$$

By KCL: $I = I_R + I_L + I_C = V [1/R + j(\omega C - 1/(\omega L))] = V Y$

$$\boxed{Y = \frac{1}{R} + j\left(\omega C - \frac{1}{\omega L}\right) = G + jB}$$

**Magnitude and phase:**

$$\vert Y \vert = \sqrt{\frac{1}{R^2} + \left(\omega C - \frac{1}{\omega L}\right)^2}, \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\left[R\left(\omega C - \frac{1}{\omega L}\right)\right]$$

**Resonance condition:** Susceptance $B = 0$, i.e., $\omega C = 1/(\omega L)$, giving:

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}, \quad f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}}$$

At resonance, $Y = 1/R$ (minimum), so the impedance is maximum and purely resistive. This maximum impedance $Z_{\max} = R$ is called the **dynamic resistance** of the parallel resonant circuit.

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

Given: $R = 200 \,\Omega$, $L = 0.2 \,\text{H}$, $C = 5 \,\mu F = 5 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{F}$, $V = 100 \,\text{V}$, $f = 50 \,\text{Hz}$.

**Step 1:** $\omega = 314.159 \,\text{rad/s}$

**Step 2:** $G = 1/R = 1/200 = 0.005 \,\text{S}$

$B_L = 1/(\omega L) = 1/(314.159 \times 0.2) = 0.01592 \,\text{S}$

$B_C = \omega C = 314.159 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 0.001571 \,\text{S}$

Net susceptance: $B = B_C - B_L = 0.001571 - 0.01592 = -0.01435 \,\text{S}$

**Step 3 (i) Admittance magnitude:**

$$\vert Y \vert = \sqrt{0.005^2 + (-0.01435)^2} = \sqrt{0.000025 + 0.0002059} = \sqrt{0.0002309} = 0.01520 \,\text{S}$$

**Step 4 (ii) Line current:**

$$I = V \vert Y \vert = 100 \times 0.01520 = 1.520 \,\text{A}$$

**Step 5 (iii) Power factor:**

$$\cos\phi = G / \vert Y \vert = 0.005 / 0.01520 = 0.329 \text{ (lagging, since } B < 0 \text{)}$$

**Step 6 (iv) Capacitance for unity pf:**

For unity pf, the net susceptance must be zero:

$$\omega C_{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{\omega L} \implies C_{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{\omega^2 L} = \frac{1}{(314.159)^2 \times 0.2} = \frac{1}{19739.2} = 5.066 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{F} \approx 50.66 \,\mu F$$

Equivalently, the **additional** capacitor to be connected in parallel:

$$C_{\text{add}} = C_{\text{new}} - C = 50.66 - 5 = 45.66 \,\mu F$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in this experiment/exam topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the leading/lagging tag:** A bare answer "$\cos\phi = 0.7$" loses 1 mark. Always write "**0.7 lagging**" or "**0.7 leading**" depending on the sign of net reactance.
> 2. **Sign error in $X = X_L - X_C$:** In series circuits, $X_L - X_C$ decides lagging/leading. For parallel, the susceptance difference $B_C - B_L$ decides the sign of phase. Mixing these up is the most common mistake.
> 3. **Using $f$ in Hz inside $\omega$:** $\omega$ needs $f$ in **Hz** only when you compute $\omega = 2\pi f$ — a unit slip gives wildly wrong answers.
> 4. **Confusing $X_C$ and $1/X_C$ in parallel:** In parallel circuits, the capacitive **susceptance** is $B_C = \omega C = 1/X_C$. Do not mistakenly use $X_C$ as the branch impedance; the current is $V/X_C$, not $V \cdot X_C$.
> 5. **Reporting wattmeter reading as total power:** A single wattmeter reads the **real** power. The apparent power $S = V I$ requires both voltmeter and ammeter RMS values. Dividing $P$ by $V I$ gives pf.
> 6. **Ignoring instrument loading:** In lab reports, mention that voltmeters draw finite current and ammeters have non-zero resistance, which slightly alters measured pf from theoretical values.
> 7. **PFC capacitor calculation sign:** Use the formula $C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)/(2\pi f V^2)$ — subtracting in the **correct** order ($\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2$ where $\phi_1$ is the worse pf). Reversing it gives a *negative* capacitance, an obvious error.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Impedance** $Z = R + jX$ where $X = X_L - X_C$. Magnitude $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$, phase $\phi = \tan^{-1}(X/R)$.
- [ ] **Admittance** $Y = G + jB$ where $G = 1/R$, $B = \omega C - 1/(\omega L)$. $Y = 1/Z$ always.
- [ ] **Power factor** $\cos\phi = R / \vert Z \vert = P/S$. It is **lagging** if net reactance is inductive, **leading** if capacitive.
- [ ] **Resonance frequency** $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$. At resonance, $\vert Z \vert$ is minimum in **series**, maximum in **parallel**.
- [ ] **Quality factor** $Q_{fac} = (1/R)\sqrt{L/C}$ governs the sharpness of the resonance peak.
- [ ] **Three powers:** $P = VI\cos\phi$ (W), $Q = VI\sin\phi$ (VAR), $S = VI$ (VA). $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$.
- [ ] **Power factor correction** uses **shunt capacitors** to supply leading VARs that cancel lagging VARs of inductive loads.
- [ ] **Lab measurement** recipe: read $V$ (voltmeter), $I$ (ammeter), $P$ (wattmeter) → compute $\cos\phi = P/(VI)$ and compare with theoretical $\cos\phi = R/\vert Z \vert$.
- [ ] **Always** report pf with **leading** or **lagging** qualifier — never as a bare number.
- [ ] For the **parallel RLC** circuit, the **branch currents** are computed individually, then summed **phasorially** (not algebraically).
- [ ] For the **series RLC** circuit, the **branch voltages** are computed individually, then summed **phasorially** to get the supply voltage.
- [ ] Indian supply standard: **230 V, 50 Hz** for single-phase, **415 V, 50 Hz** for three-phase systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of two port network parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL307 - CIRCUITS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Measurement of two port network parameters # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Two-Port Network** is an electrical network enclosed in a black box that contains two distinct pairs of terminals (ports) through which electrical signals can enter and leave. The **input port (Port 1)** is characterized by the voltage $V_1$ and current $I_1$ entering the network, while the **output port (Port 2)** is characterized by the voltage $V_2$ and current $I_2$ leaving the network. The internal composition of the network may consist of resistors, inductors, capacitors, dependent sources, and/or transistors, but the behavior at the external terminals is fully described by a set of measurable constants called **two-port network parameters** (or *network functions*). Mathematically, the network is described as a four-terminal, two-port device, where: $$[V] = [Z][I] \quad \text{or} \quad [I] = [Y][V] \quad \text{or} \quad \text{hybrid, transmission forms}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCEEL307 – Module 2):** > Students are expected to experimentally determine the **Z-parameters (Open-Circuit Impedance)**, **Y-parameters (Short-Circuit Admittance)**, **h-parameters (Hybrid)**, and **ABCD (Transmission) parameters** of a passive/active two-port network using a signal source, multimeters, and appropriate termination (open/short) at the inaccessible port. ## 1.2 The Four Variables of a Two-Port For any linear bilateral two-port network, there are exactly **four terminal variables**: - $V_1$ — Voltage across Port 1 terminals - $I_1$ — Current entering Port 1 - $V_2$ — Voltage across Port 2 terminals - $I_2$ — Current entering Port 2 These four variables are related by **two independent linear equations** in any of six possible ways, giving rise to the **six standard parameter sets**: **Z, Y, h, g, ABCD, and A'B'C'D'** (inverse transmission). ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Black-Box Translator Booth":** > Imagine a translation booth at an international conference. The speaker (Port 1) speaks in Language A and expects output in Language B from the speaker on the other side (Port 2). You, as the engineer, do not know what is inside the booth (resistors? transistors? ICs?). You only know the **input/output relationship** — how much output sound (voltage) you get for a given input (current/voltage). > > The **two-port parameters** are essentially the "**character certificate**" of the black box. They tell you, *under standardized test conditions* (input port open or short), exactly how the box will behave when connected to a real load. A simple example: a **low-pass RC filter** is a two-port network. If you apply a signal at the input and measure the output, the *ratio* of output to input depends on frequency. That ratio is one of the network's parameters. ## 1.4 Why Measure Two-Port Parameters? 1. **Black-box characterization** of complex active devices (transistors, op-amps, filters). 2. **Cascade analysis** — when networks are connected in series, the ABCD parameters multiply, making design modular. 3. **Stability and matching** in RF and microwave engineering. 4. **Fault diagnosis** — deviation of measured parameters from datasheet values indicates a faulty component. **Standard Test Conditions used in measurements:** - **Open Circuit (OC):** $I = 0$ at the port → used for Z and ABCD parameters. - **Short Circuit (SC):** $V = 0$ at the port → used for Y parameters. - **Mixed conditions:** for h and g parameters. The default standard reference impedance is **$Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$** in RF contexts, but at low frequencies in the lab, we use physical open or short circuits directly. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Parameter Sets — A Unified View Since two equations suffice to describe a linear two-port, choosing any two of the four variables $\{V_1, I_1, V_2, I_2\}$ as **dependent variables** and the other two as **independent variables** yields a $2\times 2$ parameter matrix. There are $\binom{4}{2} = 6$ such choices, giving the six parameter sets. ## 2.2 Z-Parameters (Open-Circuit Impedance Parameters) The voltage at each port is expressed as a linear function of the currents at both ports. $$V_1 = Z_{11}\,I_1 + Z_{12}\,I_2$$ $$V_2 = Z_{21}\,I_1 + Z_{22}\,I_2$$ In matrix form: $$\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Measurement Conditions (WHY these specific tests?):** - To find $Z_{11}$ and $Z_{21}$: Open-circuit the output port ($I_2 = 0$). Apply a known $I_1$ and measure $V_1$ and $V_2$. - To find $Z_{22}$ and $Z_{12}$: Open-circuit the input port ($I_1 = 0$). Apply a known $I_2$ and measure $V_1$ and $V_2$. **Why is this the easiest measurement in a student lab?** Open circuits are simple to implement — you just leave the terminal disconnected. A practical current source can be approximated by a high voltage source in series with a large resistor, OR you can apply a known voltage and measure current. ## 2.3 Y-Parameters (Short-Circuit Admittance Parameters) The current at each port is expressed as a linear function of the voltages at both ports. $$I_1 = Y_{11}\,V_1 + Y_{12}\,V_2$$ $$I_2 = Y_{21}\,V_1 + Y_{22}\,V_2$$ **Measurement Conditions:** - $Y_{11} = I_1/V_1 \vert_{V_2=0}$ — Short output, drive Port 1, measure input current and voltage. - $Y_{21} = I_2/V_1 \vert_{V_2=0}$ — Same setup, measure short-circuit output current. - $Y_{22} = I_2/V_2 \vert_{V_1=0}$ — Short input, drive Port 2, measure output current and voltage. - $Y_{12} = I_1/V_2 \vert_{V_1=0}$ — Same setup, measure input current. > [!WARNING] > **Practical Pitfall — Short-circuit measurements can be dangerous in active networks.** If the network contains dependent sources, shorting a port may draw large currents. Always check the network topology before applying a short. ## 2.4 h-Parameters (Hybrid Parameters) The h-parameters are particularly important for **transistor modeling** (BJT/FET small-signal analysis). They mix a voltage and a current at the input, and a current and a voltage at the output. $$V_1 = h_{11}\,I_1 + h_{12}\,V_2$$ $$I_2 = h_{21}\,I_1 + h_{22}\,V_2$$ **Measurement Conditions:** - $h_{11}$ and $h_{21}$: Short-circuit the output ($V_2 = 0$). - $h_{22}$ and $h_{12}$: Open-circuit the input ($I_1 = 0$). ## 2.5 ABCD (Transmission) Parameters Used in **cascade / chain analysis**. When two-port networks are connected in cascade, the overall ABCD matrix is the product of the individual matrices. $$V_1 = A\,V_2 - B\,I_2$$ $$I_1 = C\,V_2 - D\,I_2$$ > [!NOTE] > The **minus signs** arise from the convention that $I_2$ is defined as flowing *into* the network at Port 2, which is the same direction as the current *entering* the next cascaded stage. **Measurement Conditions:** - $A = V_1/V_2 \vert_{I_2=0}$ — Open output, measure $V_1$ and $V_2$. - $B = -V_1/I_2 \vert_{V_2=0}$ — Short output, apply $I_2$, measure $V_1$. - $C = I_1/V_2 \vert_{I_2=0}$ — Open output, measure $I_1$ and $V_2$. - $D = -I_1/I_2 \vert_{V_2=0}$ — Short output, apply $I_2$, measure $I_1$. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — All Parameters in One Place > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below is the **single most important reference** for Module 2 lab viva and exams. Memorize the conditions and units. | Parameter Set | Equations | Independent Variables | Test Condition (Step 1) | Test Condition (Step 2) | Units | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Z-parameters** | $V_1 = Z_{11}I_1 + Z_{12}I_2$
$V_2 = Z_{21}I_1 + Z_{22}I_2$ | $I_1,\ I_2$ | OC at Port 2 $\Rightarrow I_2=0$ | OC at Port 1 $\Rightarrow I_1=0$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | **Y-parameters** | $I_1 = Y_{11}V_1 + Y_{12}V_2$
$I_2 = Y_{21}V_1 + Y_{22}V_2$ | $V_1,\ V_2$ | SC at Port 2 $\Rightarrow V_2=0$ | SC at Port 1 $\Rightarrow V_1=0$ | Siemens (S) | | **h-parameters** | $V_1 = h_{11}I_1 + h_{12}V_2$
$I_2 = h_{21}I_1 + h_{22}V_2$ | $I_1,\ V_2$ | SC at Port 2 $\Rightarrow V_2=0$ | OC at Port 1 $\Rightarrow I_1=0$ | Mixed: $\Omega$, dimensionless, S, $\Omega^{-1}$ | | **ABCD parameters** | $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$
$I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2$ | $V_2,\ I_2$ | OC at Port 2 $\Rightarrow I_2=0$ | SC at Port 2 $\Rightarrow V_2=0$ | All dimensionless | | **g-parameters** | $I_1 = g_{11}V_1 + g_{12}I_2$
$V_2 = g_{21}V_1 + g_{22}I_2$ | $V_1,\ I_2$ | OC at Port 1 $\Rightarrow I_1=0$ | SC at Port 1 $\Rightarrow V_1=0$ | Mixed | **Key Reciprocal Relationships (VERY HIGH YIELD for KTU):** $$Z_{12} = Z_{21} \quad \text{and} \quad Y_{12} = Y_{21} \quad \text{(for reciprocal networks)}$$ $$AD - BC = 1 \quad \text{(reciprocity condition for ABCD)}$$ $$h_{11}h_{22} - h_{12}h_{21} = 1 \quad \text{(reciprocity condition for h)}$$ ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Parameter Most Used | Reason | |---|---|---| | Transistor (BJT/FET) small-signal model | **h-parameters** | Easy to measure at high frequencies with a short on output | | Power transmission lines | **ABCD parameters** | Cascaded sections multiply simply | | RF / Microwave engineering | **S-parameters** (not in KTU) | Direct measurement with VNA | | Filter design | **Z or Y parameters** | Direct relation to impedance/admittance | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Reciprocity Condition for Z-Parameters Consider a linear, passive, bilateral two-port network composed of resistors. By **Tellegen's theorem** (or simply by energy considerations), such a network is **reciprocal** — meaning if you swap source and load, the transfer function remains the same. **Step 1:** Express the network in terms of a generic T-network (or any passive topology). For any passive linear network, the impedance matrix must be symmetric. **Step 2:** The reciprocity condition states that for a passive network: $$Z_{12} = Z_{21}$$ **Step 3:** Proof by example. Consider a simple T-network with $Z_a$ in series at top, $Z_b$ in series at bottom, $Z_c$ as the shunt arm. By inspection: $$Z_{11} = Z_a + Z_c$$ $$Z_{22} = Z_b + Z_c$$ $$Z_{12} = Z_{21} = Z_c$$ **Step 4:** The off-diagonal terms are *equal* and represent the *common shunt element*. This is the geometric/intuitive reason for reciprocity in passive networks. ## 3.2 Derivation: Conversion between Z and Y Parameters Given the Z-parameter equation $[V] = [Z][I]$, we can write the inverse relation $[I] = [Z]^{-1}[V]$. By definition, $[Y] = [Z]^{-1}$. **Step 1:** For a $2\times 2$ matrix, the inverse is: $$[Z]^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det(Z)} \begin{bmatrix} Z_{22} & -Z_{12} \\ -Z_{21} & Z_{11} \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2:** The determinant is: $$\Delta Z = \det(Z) = Z_{11}Z_{22} - Z_{12}Z_{21}$$ **Step 3:** Therefore: $$Y_{11} = \frac{Z_{22}}{\Delta Z}, \quad Y_{12} = \frac{-Z_{12}}{\Delta Z}, \quad Y_{21} = \frac{-Z_{21}}{\Delta Z}, \quad Y_{22} = \frac{Z_{11}}{\Delta Z}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Conversion between Z and ABCD Parameters Start from the Z-parameter equations: $$V_1 = Z_{11}I_1 + Z_{12}I_2 \qquad (1)$$ $$V_2 = Z_{21}I_1 + Z_{22}I_2 \qquad (2)$$ **Step 1:** Solve equation (2) for $I_1$: $$I_1 = \frac{V_2 - Z_{22}I_2}{Z_{21}}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute into equation (1): $$V_1 = Z_{11}\left(\frac{V_2 - Z_{22}I_2}{Z_{21}}\right) + Z_{12}I_2$$ **Step 3:** Expand and group terms in $V_2$ and $I_2$: $$V_1 = \left(\frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}}\right)V_2 + \left(\frac{Z_{11}Z_{22} - Z_{12}Z_{21}}{Z_{21}}\right)(-I_2)$$ **Step 4:** Compare with $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$: $$A = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}}, \quad B = \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}}$$ **Step 5:** Similarly, by comparing $I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2$: $$C = \frac{1}{Z_{21}}, \quad D = \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}}$$ ## 3.4 Conversion: h-Parameters to Z-Parameters (Exam Favourite) From the h-parameter equations: $$V_1 = h_{11}I_1 + h_{12}V_2$$ $$I_2 = h_{21}I_1 + h_{22}V_2$$ Solve the second equation for $V_2$: $$V_2 = \frac{I_2 - h_{21}I_1}{h_{22}}$$ Substitute into the first: $$V_1 = h_{11}I_1 + h_{12}\left(\frac{I_2 - h_{21}I_1}{h_{22}}\right)$$ $$V_1 = \left(h_{11} - \frac{h_{12}h_{21}}{h_{22}}\right)I_1 + \left(\frac{h_{12}}{h_{22}}\right)I_2$$ Therefore: $$Z_{11} = h_{11} - \frac{h_{12}h_{21}}{h_{22}}, \quad Z_{12} = \frac{h_{12}}{h_{22}}$$ Similarly (by further manipulation): $$Z_{21} = -\frac{h_{21}}{h_{22}}, \quad Z_{22} = \frac{1}{h_{22}}$$ ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Model Lab Calculation) > [!NOTE] > **Example:** Consider a T-network with $Z_a = 2\ \Omega$, $Z_b = 4\ \Omega$, $Z_c = 6\ \Omega$. Compute all four Z-parameters. **Step 1:** Apply KVL to the input loop with Port 2 open-circuited ($I_2 = 0$): $$V_1 = (Z_a + Z_c) I_1 = (2 + 6) I_1 = 8 I_1$$ $$Z_{11} = \left.\frac{V_1}{I_1}\right|_{I_2=0} = 8\ \Omega$$ **Step 2:** With $I_2 = 0$, the output voltage is the voltage across $Z_c$ only: $$V_2 = Z_c \cdot I_1 = 6 I_1$$ $$Z_{21} = \left.\frac{V_2}{I_1}\right|_{I_2=0} = 6\ \Omega$$ **Step 3:** Apply KVL with Port 1 open-circuited ($I_1 = 0$): $$V_2 = (Z_b + Z_c) I_2 = (4 + 6) I_2 = 10 I_2$$ $$Z_{22} = \left.\frac{V_2}{I_2}\right|_{I_1=0} = 10\ \Omega$$ **Step 4:** With $I_1 = 0$, the input voltage is across $Z_c$: $$V_1 = Z_c \cdot I_2 = 6 I_2$$ $$Z_{12} = \left.\frac{V_1}{I_2}\right|_{I_1=0} = 6\ \Omega$$ **Step 5:** Verify reciprocity: $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 6\ \Omega$ ✓ ## 3.6 Step-by-Step Lab Measurement Procedure (Z-Parameters) This is the actual procedure a student follows in the lab. **Step 1 — Setup:** Connect the two-port network on a breadboard. Label Port 1 (input) and Port 2 (output) terminals clearly. **Step 2 — Source:** Connect a **function generator** (sine wave, typically **1 kHz, 5 V peak**) to Port 1 through a **series resistor** $R_s$ (say, $1\ \text{k}\Omega$). The series resistor converts the voltage source into a *near-ideal current source* for measuring $Z_{11}$ if $R_s \gg Z_{11}$. **Step 3 — Measure $Z_{11}$ and $Z_{21}$:** Leave Port 2 terminals **open** (i.e., no load connected). Using a **dual-trace oscilloscope** or two **AC voltmeters**, measure: - $V_1$ (input voltage) - $V_2$ (output voltage) - $I_1$ calculated as $(V_s - V_1)/R_s$ where $V_s$ is the source voltage Then: $$Z_{11} = \frac{V_1}{I_1}, \quad Z_{21} = \frac{V_2}{I_1}$$ **Step 4 — Measure $Z_{22}$ and $Z_{12}$:** Now, leave Port 1 **open** and apply the source to Port 2. Measure $V_1$, $V_2$, and $I_2$. Then: $$Z_{22} = \frac{V_2}{I_2}, \quad Z_{12} = \frac{V_1}{I_2}$$ **Step 5 — Repeat for Y, h, ABCD:** Use the same methodology but change terminations (open → short) per the formula table. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Frequency Selection:** The measurements are valid only at the test frequency. For reactive networks, parameters will vary with frequency. Always record the **frequency** along with the parameter values in the lab report. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Generic Two-Port Network Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Port 1 Terminal A] --> B((Network
Black Box
Z, Y, h, ABCD)) C[Port 1 Terminal B
Common/Return] --> B B --> D[Port 2 Terminal C] B --> E[Port 2 Terminal D
Common/Return] F[Source Vs] -.1 kHz Sine.-> A G[Measurement: V1, I1] --- A H[Measurement: V2, I2] --- D ``` ## 4.2 Test Setup for Z-Parameter Measurement (Open-Circuit Output) ```mermaid flowchart TD SRC[Function Generator
1 kHz, 5 Vpp] --> RS[Series Resistor Rs
1 kΩ] RS --> P1A[Port 1 Terminal A] P1A --> NET[Two-Port Network
Under Test] NET --> P1B[Port 1 Terminal B] P1B --> GND1[Ground] NET --> P2A[Port 2 Terminal C] P2A --> OPEN[OPEN CIRCUIT
No Load - I2 = 0] NET --> P2B[Port 2 Terminal D] P2B --> GND2[Ground] V1[V1 Measurement
across Port 1] --- P1A V1 --- P1B V2[V2 Measurement
across Port 2] --- P2A V2 --- P2B ``` ## 4.3 Test Setup for Y-Parameter Measurement (Short-Circuit Output) ```mermaid flowchart TD SRC2[AC Source
1 kHz, 5 Vpp] --> P1A2[Port 1 Terminal A] P1A2 --> NET2[Two-Port Network] NET2 --> P1B2[Port 1 Terminal B] P1B2 --> GND3[Ground] NET2 --> P2A2[Port 2 Terminal C] P2A2 --> SHORT[SHORT CIRCUIT
V2 = 0] SHORT --> P2B2[Port 2 Terminal D] P2B2 --> GND4[Ground] I1M[I1 measured via
ammeter in series] --- P1A2 I2M[I2 measured via
ammeter at Port 2] --- P2A2 ``` ## 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Which Parameter Set Should I Use? ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{What do you
want to measure?} Q1 -->|Voltage ratios
with current input| Z1[Use Z-parameters
Open circuit test] Q1 -->|Current ratios
with voltage input| Y1[Use Y-parameters
Short circuit test] Q1 -->|Transistor
characterization| H1[Use h-parameters
Mixed open/short] Q1 -->|Cascaded networks
transmission line| ABCD1[Use ABCD
parameters] Q1 -->|Filter design| ZY1[Use Z or Y] Z1 --> FORM[Refer to Formula Table] Y1 --> FORM H1 --> FORM ABCD1 --> FORM ``` ## 4.5 Cascade Connection (Why ABCD is Special) ```mermaid flowchart LR N1[Network 1
ABCD Matrix M1] --> N2[Network 2
ABCD Matrix M2] N2 --> N3[Network 3
ABCD Matrix M3] N3 --> RL[Load RL] M1 & M2 & M3 --> MATRIX[Overall Matrix M = M1 x M2 x M3] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define Z-parameters of a two-port network. Why is the open-circuit test used for their measurement?** **Model Answer:** Z-parameters (or open-circuit impedance parameters) of a two-port network are defined by the equations: $$V_1 = Z_{11}I_1 + Z_{12}I_2$$ $$V_2 = Z_{21}I_1 + Z_{22}I_2$$ The four Z-parameters are defined as: - $Z_{11} = V_1/I_1 \vert_{I_2=0}$ — driving-point impedance at Port 1 - $Z_{21} = V_2/I_1 \vert_{I_2=0}$ — forward transfer impedance - $Z_{12} = V_1/I_2 \vert_{I_1=0}$ — reverse transfer impedance - $Z_{22} = V_2/I_2 \vert_{I_1=0}$ — driving-point impedance at Port 2 **Why open-circuit test?** Because the definition of $Z_{11}$ and $Z_{21}$ requires $I_2 = 0$, which is achieved by leaving Port 2 open. Similarly, $Z_{12}$ and $Z_{22}$ require $I_1 = 0$, achieved by open-circuiting Port 1. Open circuit is the simplest termination to physically realize in a lab. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **State the conditions under which h-parameters and ABCD parameters of a two-port network are measured.** **Model Answer:** **h-parameters (Hybrid Parameters):** Defined by the equations $V_1 = h_{11}I_1 + h_{12}V_2$ and $I_2 = h_{21}I_1 + h_{22}V_2$. - To find $h_{11}$ and $h_{21}$: **Short-circuit the output port** ($V_2 = 0$). - To find $h_{22}$ and $h_{12}$: **Open-circuit the input port** ($I_1 = 0$). **ABCD (Transmission) Parameters:** Defined by $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$ and $I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2$. - To find $A$ and $C$: **Open-circuit the output port** ($I_2 = 0$). - To find $B$ and $D$: **Short-circuit the output port** ($V_2 = 0$). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part A):** Students often confuse the conditions for $A$ vs $C$ (both are measured with the output port open, but $A$ is a voltage ratio and $C$ is a current ratio). Do not mix them up — partial credit is lost on the very first sub-clause. --- ## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** **(a)** For the two-port T-network shown in the figure, derive the Z-parameters in terms of the branch impedances $Z_a$, $Z_b$, $Z_c$. (7 marks) **(b)** Given a T-network with $Z_a = 3\ \Omega$, $Z_b = 5\ \Omega$, $Z_c = 7\ \Omega$ operating at $\omega = 10^4$ rad/s, calculate the Y-parameters and ABCD parameters of the network. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Derivation of Z-Parameters for T-Network **Step 1:** Consider a T-network with $Z_a$ in the upper series arm, $Z_b$ in the lower series arm, and $Z_c$ as the shunt (vertical) arm. **Step 2:** Apply KVL to the input mesh. Let $I_c$ be the current through $Z_c$. By KCL at the central node: $$I_c = I_1 - I_2$$ **Step 3:** Write the loop equation for the input side: $$V_1 = Z_a I_1 + Z_c I_c = Z_a I_1 + Z_c(I_1 - I_2)$$ $$V_1 = (Z_a + Z_c) I_1 - Z_c I_2$$ **Step 4:** Write the loop equation for the output side: $$V_2 = Z_b I_2 + Z_c I_c = Z_b I_2 + Z_c(I_1 - I_2)$$ $$V_2 = Z_c I_1 + (Z_b + Z_c) I_2$$ **Step 5:** Compare with the Z-parameter form $V_1 = Z_{11}I_1 + Z_{12}I_2$ and $V_2 = Z_{21}I_1 + Z_{22}I_2$: $$\boxed{Z_{11} = Z_a + Z_c, \quad Z_{12} = -Z_c, \quad Z_{21} = -Z_c, \quad Z_{22} = Z_b + Z_c}$$ > [!NOTE] > The **minus signs** arise because the defined current $I_2$ enters the network at Port 2, which is opposite to the assumed direction in the T-network's series arm. Some textbooks absorb this sign into the definition by redefining $I_2$ as flowing *out* of the network. Always check the convention used in your KTU textbook. #### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation **Given:** $Z_a = 3\ \Omega$, $Z_b = 5\ \Omega$, $Z_c = 7\ \Omega$. **Step 1:** Compute Z-parameters from Part (a): $$Z_{11} = 3 + 7 = 10\ \Omega$$ $$Z_{22} = 5 + 7 = 12\ \Omega$$ $$Z_{12} = Z_{21} = -7\ \Omega$$ **Step 2:** Compute the determinant: $$\Delta Z = Z_{11}Z_{22} - Z_{12}Z_{21} = (10)(12) - (-7)(-7) = 120 - 49 = 71\ \Omega^2$$ **Step 3:** Compute Y-parameters using the conversion formulas: $$Y_{11} = \frac{Z_{22}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{12}{71} = 0.169\ \text{S}$$ $$Y_{12} = \frac{-Z_{12}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{7}{71} = 0.0986\ \text{S}$$ $$Y_{21} = \frac{-Z_{21}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{7}{71} = 0.0986\ \text{S}$$ $$Y_{22} = \frac{Z_{11}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{10}{71} = 0.1408\ \text{S}$$ **Step 4:** Compute ABCD parameters: $$A = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{10}{-7} = -1.4286$$ $$B = \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}} = \frac{71}{-7} = -10.143\ \Omega$$ $$C = \frac{1}{Z_{21}} = \frac{1}{-7} = -0.1429\ \text{S}$$ $$D = \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{12}{-7} = -1.7143$$ **Step 5:** Verify reciprocity condition $AD - BC = 1$: $$AD - BC = (-1.4286)(-1.7143) - (-10.143)(-0.1429)$$ $$= 2.4490 - 1.4494 = 0.9996 \approx 1\ \checkmark$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When computing ABCD parameters from Z-parameters, the sign convention for $I_2$ matters. If your KTU textbook uses $I_2$ flowing *out* of the network, the formulas are: > $$A = Z_{11}/Z_{21}, \quad B = -\Delta Z/Z_{21}, \quad C = -1/Z_{21}, \quad D = -Z_{22}/Z_{21}$$ > Getting the sign wrong here costs **3–4 marks** out of 7. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** **(a)** Derive the expressions for converting h-parameters of a two-port network to Y-parameters. (7 marks) **(b)** For a BJT in common-emitter configuration, the measured h-parameters at $I_C = 1$ mA are: $h_{ie} = 1500\ \Omega$, $h_{re} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$, $h_{fe} = 80$, $h_{oe} = 25\ \mu\text{S}$. Calculate the Z-parameters of the transistor. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Conversion from h-Parameters to Y-Parameters **Step 1:** Write the h-parameter equations: $$V_1 = h_{11}I_1 + h_{12}V_2 \qquad (1)$$ $$I_2 = h_{21}I_1 + h_{22}V_2 \qquad (2)$$ **Step 2:** Solve equation (2) for $I_1$: $$I_1 = \frac{I_2 - h_{22}V_2}{h_{21}}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute into equation (1): $$V_1 = h_{11}\left(\frac{I_2 - h_{22}V_2}{h_{21}}\right) + h_{12}V_2$$ $$V_1 = \left(\frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}}\right)I_2 + \left(h_{12} - \frac{h_{11}h_{22}}{h_{21}}\right)V_2$$ **Step 4:** We want to express $I_1$ and $I_2$ in terms of $V_1$ and $V_2$. From equation (2): $$I_2 = h_{21}I_1 + h_{22}V_2$$ We need another relation. From (1), solve for $I_1$: $$I_1 = \frac{V_1 - h_{12}V_2}{h_{11}}$$ Substitute into (2): $$I_2 = h_{21}\left(\frac{V_1 - h_{12}V_2}{h_{11}}\right) + h_{22}V_2$$ $$I_2 = \left(\frac{h_{21}}{h_{11}}\right)V_1 + \left(h_{22} - \frac{h_{12}h_{21}}{h_{11}}\right)V_2$$ **Step 5:** Compare with $I_1 = Y_{11}V_1 + Y_{12}V_2$ and $I_2 = Y_{21}V_1 + Y_{22}V_2$: $$Y_{11} = \frac{1}{h_{11}}, \quad Y_{12} = -\frac{h_{12}}{h_{11}}$$ $$Y_{21} = \frac{h_{21}}{h_{11}}, \quad Y_{22} = h_{22} - \frac{h_{12}h_{21}}{h_{11}} = \frac{\Delta h}{h_{11}}$$ where $\Delta h = h_{11}h_{22} - h_{12}h_{21}$. #### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation of Z-Parameters from h-Parameters **Given:** $h_{11} = h_{ie} = 1500\ \Omega$, $h_{12} = h_{re} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$, $h_{21} = h_{fe} = 80$, $h_{22} = h_{oe} = 25 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{S}$. **Step 1:** Compute the determinant $\Delta h$: $$\Delta h = h_{11}h_{22} - h_{12}h_{21}$$ $$= (1500)(25 \times 10^{-6}) - (1.5 \times 10^{-4})(80)$$ $$= 0.0375 - 0.0120 = 0.0255$$ **Step 2:** Apply the h-to-Z conversion formulas derived in Section 3.4: $$Z_{11} = \frac{\Delta h}{h_{22}} = \frac{0.0255}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = 1020\ \Omega$$ $$Z_{12} = \frac{h_{12}}{h_{22}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = 6\ \Omega$$ $$Z_{21} = -\frac{h_{21}}{h_{22}} = -\frac{80}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = -3.2 \times 10^{6}\ \Omega$$ $$Z_{22} = \frac{1}{h_{22}} = \frac{1}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = 40{,}000\ \Omega = 40\ \text{k}\Omega$$ **Step 3:** Interpretation: - $Z_{11} = 1020\ \Omega$: The input impedance looking into the base is around 1 kΩ. - $Z_{21} = -3.2\ \text{M}\Omega$: Very large — typical of a common-emitter BJT. - The large magnitude of $Z_{21}$ indicates high forward transfer gain. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes in BJT h-Parameter Problems:** > 1. Confusing $h_{ie}$ with $h_{ib}$ or $h_{ic}$ (the subscripts $e, b, c$ denote common-emitter, common-base, common-collector configurations). > 2. Sign errors in $Z_{21}$ — it must be negative for a CE configuration. > 3. Unit confusion: $h_{oe}$ is in Siemens (or mho, or $\Omega^{-1}$), not in ohms. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Must-Know" List Before Walking Into the KTU Lab Exam:** - [x] A **two-port network** has 4 terminal variables: $V_1, I_1, V_2, I_2$. - [x] **Z-parameters** use $I_1, I_2$ as independent variables → measured by **open-circuiting** the inactive port. - [x] **Y-parameters** use $V_1, V_2$ as independent variables → measured by **short-circuiting** the inactive port. - [x] **h-parameters** are hybrid → short-circuit for $h_{11}, h_{21}$; open-circuit for $h_{12}, h_{22}$. - [x] **ABCD (transmission) parameters** are dimensionless (except $B$ in $\Omega$ and $C$ in S) → used for cascaded networks. - [x] **Reciprocity condition** (passive network): $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$, $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$, $AD - BC = 1$, $h_{12} = -h_{21} \cdot \Delta h / h_{11}$. - [x] **Symmetry condition** (additional, for networks symmetric about the vertical axis): $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ and $A = D$. - [x] Always specify the **test frequency** when reporting parameters — they are frequency-dependent. - [x] In lab, a **series resistor** with the source approximates a current source for Z-parameter measurement. - [x] The **direction of $I_2$** (into or out of the network) affects the sign in ABCD parameter formulas — check the KTU textbook convention. - [x] **Cascade formula** for ABCD: $M_{total} = M_1 \times M_2 \times M_3 \times \ldots$ - [x] **Hybrid-$\pi$ model of BJT** uses h-parameters at low frequencies and Y-parameters (or $\pi$-model parameters $r_\pi, g_m, r_o$) at high frequencies. - [x] For the **T-network**: $Z_{11} = Z_a + Z_c$, $Z_{22} = Z_b + Z_c$, $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = \pm Z_c$ (sign depends on $I_2$ convention). - [x] For the **$\pi$-network**: Y-parameters are directly the branch admittances.

topics.title: Step response of RLC circuit (suggested to use DSO)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL307 - CIRCUITS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Step response of RLC circuit (suggested to use DSO) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) A **Series RLC Circuit** is a second-order electrical network comprising a Resistor ($R$), an Inductor ($L$), and a Capacitor ($C$) connected in series with a voltage source. The **Step Response** is defined as the time-domain behavior of the circuit's output (typically the capacitor voltage $v_c(t)$ or the circuit current $i(t)$) when subjected to a **unit step input** $V_s \cdot u(t)$, where $u(t)$ is the Heaviside step function. This is formally expressed as $V_{in}(t) = V_s$ for $t \geq 0$, and $V_{in}(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$. The response is governed by a **second-order linear differential equation** with constant coefficients, making it the canonical experiment to study **damping phenomena** in electrical systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** The experiment **must** be performed using a **Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO)** to capture and display the transient waveforms. The function generator acts as the step source. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine pushing a child on a swing: - **Underdamped** = The swing oscillates back and forth many times before stopping (like a guitar string vibration). - **Critically Damped** = The swing returns to rest in the **shortest possible time without oscillating** (perfect shock absorbers in a car). - **Overdamped** = The swing moves back to rest very slowly, like dragging it through thick mud. In an RLC circuit, the **resistor $R$** acts as the "friction" (energy dissipator), the **capacitor $C$** acts as the "spring" (stores electric energy in an electric field), and the **inductor $L$** acts as the "mass" (stores energy in a magnetic field and opposes change in current). ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Natural Frequency:** $\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ (measured in **rad/s**) - **Damping Coefficient:** $\alpha = \frac{R}{2L}$ (measured in **Np/s** or **1/s**) - **Quality Factor:** $Q = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$ (dimensionless) > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** All three damping cases (over, critical, under) must be physically observed in the lab and verified against theoretical calculations for **full marks** in the KTU Lab Record and University Practical Exam (UE). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How" When a step voltage $V_s$ is applied to a series RLC loop at $t=0$, Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) yields the **circuit equation**: $$V_s = v_R(t) + v_L(t) + v_C(t)$$ Substituting the element relations $v_R = iR$, $v_L = L\frac{di}{dt}$, and $i = C\frac{dv_C}{dt}$: $$V_s = LC\frac{d^2v_C}{dt^2} + RC\frac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C(t)$$ This is a **second-order linear ODE**. The behavior of the response depends entirely on the **roots of the characteristic equation**: $$s^2 + \frac{R}{L}s + \frac{1}{LC} = 0$$ The roots are given by: $$s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2} = -\frac{R}{2L} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 - \frac{1}{LC}}$$ ## 2.2 The Three Damping Cases | Damping Condition | Relationship | Discriminant | Time Domain Response Shape | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Overdamped** | $\alpha > \omega_0$ | $\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2 > 0$ | Two real, distinct negative roots. Slow, non-oscillatory rise to $V_s$. | | **Critically Damped** | $\alpha = \omega_0$ | $\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2 = 0$ | Repeated real negative root. Fastest non-oscillatory response. | | **Underdamped** | $\alpha < \omega_0$ | $\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2 < 0$ | Complex conjugate roots. Oscillatory decay (ringing). | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **EXAM NOTE:** The following table is a **must-memorize** cheat sheet. Every KTU lab exam question (theory or viva) for this experiment requires at least one of these formulas. | Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Physical Meaning | Standard Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Characteristic Equation | $s^2 + 2\alpha s + \omega_0^2 = 0$ | Defines the dynamics of the system | Dimensionless | | Neper Frequency (Damping) | $\alpha = \frac{R}{2L}$ | Rate of exponential decay | Np/s | | Resonant Frequency | $\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | Undamped natural frequency | rad/s | | Damped Frequency | $\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$ | Actual ringing frequency observed | rad/s | | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \frac{\alpha}{\omega_0} = \frac{R}{2}\sqrt{\frac{C}{L}}$ | Normalized damping measure | Dimensionless | | Quality Factor | $Q = \frac{1}{2\zeta} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$ | Sharpness of resonance | Dimensionless | | Capacitor Voltage (Underdamped) | $v_C(t) = V_s\left[1 - e^{-\alpha t}\left(\cos\omega_d t + \frac{\alpha}{\omega_d}\sin\omega_d t\right)\right]$ | Complete step response for $\alpha < \omega_0$ | Volts (V) | | Capacitor Voltage (Overdamped) | $v_C(t) = V_s\left[1 - \frac{s_2 e^{s_1 t} - s_1 e^{s_2 t}}{s_2 - s_1}\right]$ | Non-oscillatory response | Volts (V) | | Capacitor Voltage (Critically Damped) | $v_C(t) = V_s\left[1 - e^{-\alpha t}(1 + \alpha t)\right]$ | Fastest non-oscillatory response | Volts (V) | | Peak Overshoot | $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | Maximum deviation above $V_s$ | Per Unit (p.u.) | | Settling Time (2% criterion) | $t_s = \frac{4}{\zeta\omega_0} = \frac{8L}{R}$ | Time to settle within $\pm 2\%$ of $V_s$ | Seconds (s) | | Rise Time (10%–90%) | $t_r \approx \frac{1.8}{\omega_d}$ | Time to go from 10% to 90% of $V_s$ | Seconds (s) | > [!WARNING] > In the formula table above, the absolute value notation `|s|` has been replaced with `\vert s \vert` to avoid breaking markdown table parsing. Use this convention in all your lab records and exam scripts. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive Suspension Systems:** A mechanical analogue of a critically damped RLC system ensures passenger comfort without excessive bouncing. - **Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS):** RLC filter design directly uses step-response analysis to prevent voltage overshoot that could damage microprocessors. - **Communication Filters:** Band-pass filters in radios are designed using $Q$ and $\omega_0$ relationships derived from RLC step response. - **Medical Devices (Defibrillators):** The discharge waveform across the patient is a controlled RLC step response, requiring precise critical/underdamped tuning for therapeutic effect. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Deriving the Step Response for the Underdamped Case We begin with the series RLC circuit driven by a DC step source $V_s$ at $t=0$. The switch closes at $t=0$ and the initial conditions are: - Initial capacitor voltage: $v_C(0) = 0$ V (assuming uncharged) - Initial inductor current: $i_L(0) = 0$ A (assuming no prior current) ### Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) For $t \geq 0$, summing voltages around the loop: $$V_s = v_R(t) + v_L(t) + v_C(t)$$ ### Step 2: Substitute Element V-I Relations The current through the series circuit is the same for all elements: $$i(t) = C\frac{dv_C}{dt}$$ Therefore: - Voltage across resistor: $v_R = iR = RC\frac{dv_C}{dt}$ - Voltage across inductor: $v_L = L\frac{di}{dt} = LC\frac{d^2v_C}{dt^2}$ ### Step 3: Formulate the Differential Equation $$V_s = LC\frac{d^2v_C}{dt^2} + RC\frac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C(t)$$ ### Step 4: Convert to Standard Second-Order Form Divide through by $LC$: $$\frac{d^2v_C}{dt^2} + \frac{R}{L}\frac{dv_C}{dt} + \frac{1}{LC}v_C(t) = \frac{V_s}{LC}$$ Compare with the canonical form: $\frac{d^2y}{dt^2} + 2\alpha \frac{dy}{dt} + \omega_0^2 y = K$ This yields: - $2\alpha = \frac{R}{L} \implies \alpha = \frac{R}{2L}$ - $\omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{LC} \implies \omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ ### Step 5: Solve the Homogeneous Equation The characteristic equation is $s^2 + 2\alpha s + \omega_0^2 = 0$, giving roots: $$s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}$$ For the **underdamped case** ($\alpha < \omega_0$), we define the damped natural frequency: $$\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$$ The roots become **complex conjugates**: $s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm j\omega_d$ The homogeneous solution is: $$v_{C,h}(t) = e^{-\alpha t}\left[A_1\cos(\omega_d t) + A_2\sin(\omega_d t)\right]$$ ### Step 6: Find the Particular (Forced) Solution Since the input is a constant DC step $V_s$, the steady-state (particular) solution is the capacitor fully charged: $$v_{C,p}(t) = V_s$$ ### Step 7: Total Solution $$v_C(t) = V_s + e^{-\alpha t}\left[A_1\cos(\omega_d t) + A_2\sin(\omega_d t)\right]$$ ### Step 8: Apply Initial Conditions to Find Constants **Condition 1:** $v_C(0) = 0$ (uncharged capacitor): $$0 = V_s + A_1 \implies A_1 = -V_s$$ **Condition 2:** $\frac{dv_C}{dt}\Big|_{t=0} = \frac{i(0)}{C} = 0$ (zero initial current): Differentiating $v_C(t)$: $$\frac{dv_C}{dt} = -\alpha e^{-\alpha t}\left[A_1\cos(\omega_d t) + A_2\sin(\omega_d t)\right] + e^{-\alpha t}\left[-A_1\omega_d\sin(\omega_d t) + A_2\omega_d\cos(\omega_d t)\right]$$ At $t=0$: $$0 = -\alpha A_1 + A_2\omega_d \implies A_2 = \frac{\alpha A_1}{\omega_d} = -\frac{\alpha V_s}{\omega_d}$$ ### Step 9: Final Underdamped Step Response Equation $$\boxed{v_C(t) = V_s - V_s e^{-\alpha t}\left[\cos(\omega_d t) + \frac{\alpha}{\omega_d}\sin(\omega_d t)\right]}$$ This can be compactly written using the damping ratio $\zeta = \alpha/\omega_0$: $$v_C(t) = V_s\left[1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_0 t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\sin\left(\omega_d t + \phi\right)\right]$$ where $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}{\zeta}\right)$. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Damping Cases for $v_C(t)$ | Parameter | Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$) | Critically Damped ($\zeta = 1$) | Underdamped ($0 < \zeta < 1$) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Roots | $s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}$ | $s_{1,2} = -\alpha$ (repeated) | $s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm j\omega_d$ | | Response Type | Sum of two real exponentials | $(1 + \alpha t)e^{-\alpha t}$ | Decaying sinusoid | | Oscillations | **None** | **None** | Present (ringing) | | Rise Time | Slowest | Fastest (no overshoot) | Fast, but with overshoot | | Settling Time | Longest | Moderate | Moderate (for low $\zeta$) | | DSO Observation | Smooth monotonic curve | Smooth monotonic curve | Oscillating decaying curve | ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation) **Problem:** A series RLC circuit has $R = 50\ \Omega$, $L = 10\ \text{mH}$, $C = 1\ \mu\text{F}$. A step voltage of $V_s = 10$ V is applied at $t = 0$. Determine the type of damping and write the complete expression for $v_C(t)$. **Solution:** **Step A: Calculate $\alpha$ and $\omega_0$:** $$\alpha = \frac{R}{2L} = \frac{50}{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{50}{0.02} = 2500\ \text{Np/s}$$ $$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10^{-8}}} = 10^4\ \text{rad/s}$$ **Step B: Identify Damping Type:** Since $\alpha = 2500 < \omega_0 = 10000$, the system is **underdamped**. **Step C: Calculate $\omega_d$:** $$\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2} = \sqrt{(10^4)^2 - (2500)^2}$$ $$= \sqrt{10^8 - 6.25 \times 10^6} = \sqrt{93.75 \times 10^6}$$ $$\omega_d \approx 9682.46\ \text{rad/s}$$ **Step D: Write the Final Response:** $$v_C(t) = 10 - 10\, e^{-2500\, t}\left[\cos(9682.46\, t) + \frac{2500}{9682.46}\sin(9682.46\, t)\right]$$ $$v_C(t) = 10\left[1 - e^{-2500t}\left(\cos(9682.46t) + 0.2582\sin(9682.46t)\right)\right]\ \text{V}$$ **Step E: Calculate Peak Overshoot $M_p$:** $$\zeta = \frac{\alpha}{\omega_0} = \frac{2500}{10000} = 0.25$$ $$M_p = e^{-\pi \times 0.25 / \sqrt{1 - 0.0625}} = e^{-\pi \times 0.25 / 0.9682} = e^{-0.8113} \approx 0.4444$$ Peak voltage = $V_s(1 + M_p) = 10 \times 1.4444 = 14.44$ V. > [!NOTE] > **Lab Tip:** This means if you set $R = 50\ \Omega$, $L = 10$ mH, $C = 1\ \mu\text{F}$ on your breadboard and apply a 10 V step, you should see the capacitor voltage oscillate and reach a peak near 14.4 V before settling to 10 V. The DSO will display this waveform directly on Channel 1. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Circuit Schematic (Series RLC with Step Input) ```mermaid graph LR A[Function Generator
SQUARE WAVE 1 kHz] -->|V_in| B(Switch closes at t=0) B --> C((R = Resistor)) C --> D((L = Inductor)) D --> E((C = Capacitor)) E --> F[Ground GND] B -.-> G[DSO CH1
Monitors V_in] E -.-> H[DSO CH2
Monitors V_c t] C -.-> I[DSO CH2 Reference] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Experiment Workflow) ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Start: Power ON DSO and FG]) --> setup[Set Function Generator to
SQUARE WAVE 1 kHz, 5 Vp-p] setup --> circuit[Build Series RLC on Breadboard
R = 100 ohm, L = 10 mH, C = 0.1 uF] circuit --> probe[Connect DSO Probes
CH1 to FG output, CH2 across Capacitor] probe --> run[Trigger DSO on rising edge
Set TIME/DIV and VOLTS/DIV] run --> observe1{Check Waveform} observe1 -->|Oscillating| underdamped[UNDERDAMPED Response
Note peak overshoot, period] observe1 -->|Non-oscillating fast| critdamped[CRITICALLY DAMPED Response
Adjust R to R_crit] observe1 -->|Non-oscillating slow| overdamped[OVERDAMPED Response
Increase R] underdamped --> measureR[Measure Time Period Td from DSO] critdamped --> measureR overdamped --> measureR measureR --> calc[Calculate alpha, omega_0, omega_d
and compare with theory] calc --> verify[Verify DSO measurement with theory
Percent error less than 10%] verify --> record[Record in Lab Record with snapshot] record --> end([End of Experiment]) ``` ## 4.3 Expected DSO Waveform Patterns (Textual Representation) | Damping Type | DSO Display Description (V_c vs Time) | | :--- | :--- | | **Underdamped** | Rises above $V_s$, oscillates up and down with decreasing amplitude, settles to $V_s$. Shape: `∿∿∿∿` decaying ripple. | | **Critically Damped** | Rises to $V_s$ as fast as possible without overshooting, no oscillation. Shape: smooth S-curve to steady state. | | **Overdamped** | Slowly rises to $V_s$ with no oscillation, long settling time. Shape: very sluggish exponential-like rise. | ## 4.4 Component List and Equipment Required (KTU 2024 Standard) | Equipment | Specification | Quantity | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Resistor | $R = 100\ \Omega$, $1$ k$\Omega$ (variable decade box preferred) | 1 each | | Inductor | $L = 10$ mH, $33$ mH | 1 each | | Capacitor | $C = 0.1\ \mu$F, $0.01\ \mu$F | 1 each | | Function Generator | 1 Hz to 1 MHz, square wave output | 1 | | Digital Storage Oscilloscope | 2-channel, $\geq 20$ MHz bandwidth | 1 | | Breadboard & Connecting Wires | Standard | 1 set | | DC Power Supply (optional) | $0$–$15$ V variable (if FG is not available for step) | 1 | > [!NOTE] > **Lab Procedure Tip:** Using a **square wave** (instead of a true DC step) effectively creates a periodic step input. The DSO captures both the rising edge (charging) and falling edge (discharging) transients, giving you **all three damping cases** on a single screen by varying $R$. This is the **suggested KTU method** to save lab time. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, PCEEL307]** **Define the term "damping ratio" $\zeta$ for a series RLC circuit. What are its values for overdamped, critically damped, and underdamped responses?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **(1 Mark)** The damping ratio $\zeta$ is defined as the ratio of the actual damping coefficient $\alpha$ to the natural frequency $\omega_0$: $$\zeta = \frac{\alpha}{\omega_0} = \frac{R}{2}\sqrt{\frac{C}{L}}$$ - **(1 Mark)** It is a **dimensionless** parameter that characterizes how rapidly the oscillations in a second-order system decay. - **(1 Mark)** The three cases are: - Overdamped: $\zeta > 1$ - Critically damped: $\zeta = 1$ - Underdamped: $0 < \zeta < 1$ ### **Question A2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PCEEL307]** **Why is a Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO) preferred over a CRO for observing the step response of an RLC circuit?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **(1 Mark)** A DSO can **store and freeze** transient waveforms (single-shot events) on its digital display, whereas a CRO requires repetitive triggering to display non-periodic signals. - **(1 Mark)** A step response is a **single-shot, non-repetitive transient** in the strict sense, making DSO the ideal instrument. - **(1 Mark)** Additional DSO advantages: built-in **cursors for precise time and voltage measurement** (e.g., measuring $\omega_d$ and peak overshoot $M_p$), USB data export for lab report, and automatic parameter measurement. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full University Exam Question (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **(a) Derive the expression for the step response of capacitor voltage in a series RLC circuit for the underdamped case. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(Step 1 — KVL Application — 1 Mark):** Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law to the series loop with step input $V_s$: $$V_s = Ri(t) + L\frac{di(t)}{dt} + v_C(t)$$ **(Step 2 — Convert to $v_C$ ODE — 2 Marks):** Substituting $i(t) = C\frac{dv_C}{dt}$: $$LC\frac{d^2v_C}{dt^2} + RC\frac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C(t) = V_s$$ Dividing by $LC$: $$\frac{d^2v_C}{dt^2} + \frac{R}{L}\frac{dv_C}{dt} + \frac{1}{LC}v_C(t) = \frac{V_s}{LC}$$ **(Step 3 — Define Parameters — 1 Mark):** Comparing with standard form gives: - $\alpha = \frac{R}{2L}$ (Neper frequency) - $\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ (natural frequency) **(Step 4 — Solve Homogeneous Equation — 2 Marks):** The characteristic equation $s^2 + 2\alpha s + \omega_0^2 = 0$ has roots $s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm j\omega_d$ where $\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$. The homogeneous solution is: $$v_{C,h}(t) = e^{-\alpha t}\left[A_1\cos(\omega_d t) + A_2\sin(\omega_d t)\right]$$ **(Step 5 — Apply Initial Conditions — 1 Mark):** With $v_C(0) = 0$ and $\frac{dv_C(0)}{dt} = 0$, we obtain $A_1 = -V_s$ and $A_2 = -\frac{\alpha V_s}{\omega_d}$. **Final Result:** $$\boxed{v_C(t) = V_s\left[1 - e^{-\alpha t}\left(\cos\omega_d t + \frac{\alpha}{\omega_d}\sin\omega_d t\right)\right]\ \text{V}}$$ --- **(b) For a series RLC circuit with $R = 20\ \Omega$, $L = 5$ mH, and $C = 0.5\ \mu$F, a step voltage of 12 V is applied. Determine: (i) Damping type, (ii) Damped frequency $f_d$, (iii) Peak overshoot $M_p$, and (iv) Settling time $t_s$. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Damping Type — 2 Marks:** $$\alpha = \frac{R}{2L} = \frac{20}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{20}{0.01} = 2000\ \text{Np/s}$$ $$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-9}}}$$ $$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 20000\ \text{rad/s}$$ Since $\alpha = 2000 < \omega_0 = 20000$, the system is **underdamped**. **(ii) Damped Frequency — 2 Marks:** $$\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2} = \sqrt{(20000)^2 - (2000)^2} = \sqrt{4 \times 10^8 - 4 \times 10^6}$$ $$\omega_d = \sqrt{3.96 \times 10^8} = 19899.75\ \text{rad/s}$$ $$f_d = \frac{\omega_d}{2\pi} = \frac{19899.75}{2\pi} \approx 3167\ \text{Hz} \approx 3.17\ \text{kHz}$$ **(iii) Peak Overshoot — 1.5 Marks:** $$\zeta = \frac{\alpha}{\omega_0} = \frac{2000}{20000} = 0.1$$ $$M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = e^{-\pi(0.1)/\sqrt{0.99}} = e^{-0.3158} \approx 0.7292\ \text{or}\ 72.92\%$$ **(iv) Settling Time — 1.5 Marks:** $$t_s = \frac{4}{\zeta\omega_0} = \frac{4}{0.1 \times 20000} = \frac{4}{2000} = 2\ \text{ms}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a) Explain with a neat sketch how the step response of a series RLC circuit is obtained using a DSO. List the components required. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution Outline:** - **Circuit Diagram (2 Marks):** Series connection of $R$, $L$, $C$ with a function generator (square wave source). DSO CH1 across source, CH2 across capacitor. - **Procedure (3 Marks):** (1) Set FG to square wave $\sim$1 kHz, 5 Vp-p. (2) Connect probes to DSO. (3) Adjust TIME/DIV and VOLTS/DIV. (4) Trigger DSO and observe $v_C(t)$ waveform. (5) Vary $R$ decade box to obtain overdamped, critically damped, and underdamped responses. - **Component List (2 Marks):** Resistor decade box, Inductor, Capacitor, Function Generator, DSO, Breadboard, Connecting Wires, BNC-to-probe cables. **(b) A series RLC circuit with $R = 10\ \Omega$, $L = 100$ mH, $C = 10\ \mu$F is excited by a 20 V step. Find the condition for critical damping and the value of $R$ required for it. Also calculate the rise time of the critically damped response. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Condition for critical damping (3 Marks):** $\alpha = \omega_0$, i.e., $\frac{R}{2L} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$. Squaring both sides: $$\frac{R^2}{4L^2} = \frac{1}{LC} \implies R^2 = \frac{4L}{C} \implies R_{crit} = 2\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$ **Calculating $R_{crit}$ (2 Marks):** $$R_{crit} = 2\sqrt{\frac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{-6}}} = 2\sqrt{10^4} = 2 \times 100 = 200\ \Omega$$ **Rise Time for Critical Damping (2 Marks):** The rise time for a critically damped second-order system is given by: $$t_r = \frac{3.36}{\omega_0} = 3.36 \sqrt{LC}$$ $$t_r = 3.36 \times \sqrt{100 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.36 \times \sqrt{10^{-6}} = 3.36 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 3.36\ \text{ms}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit Confusion (–2 Marks):** Writing $\omega_0$ in Hz instead of rad/s, or $\alpha$ without specifying Np/s. Always mention units explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting the Sign in Roots (–2 Marks):** Students often write $s_{1,2} = \frac{R}{2L} \pm \dots$ (positive sign) instead of the correct $-\frac{R}{2L} \pm \dots$. The roots must be **negative** for a stable system. > 3. **Initial Conditions (–3 Marks):** Forgetting that $v_C(0) = 0$ and $i_L(0) = 0$ in the uncharged case. Writing $A_1 = V_s$ instead of $-V_s$ will lead to a completely wrong waveform. > 4. **Confusing $V_s$ and $V_{in}$:** The step input is $V_s \cdot u(t)$. The output $v_C(t)$ approaches $V_s$ at steady state, not infinity. > 5. **DSO Reading Errors in Lab:** Not setting the probe calibration to **1X** (or 10X consistently) and reading the wrong time/div scale. > 6. **Confusing Series vs Parallel RLC:** In a **series** RLC, the same current flows. The total impedance is $Z = R + j(\omega L - 1/\omega C)$. In **parallel** RLC, the same voltage appears across all branches — the time constant formula changes! --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 PCEEL307 Module 2 Experiment 2** - [x] **Step Response** = output of an RLC circuit when a sudden step DC voltage is applied at $t=0$. - [x] The governing equation is a **2nd-order linear ODE**: $LC\ddot{v}_C + RC\dot{v}_C + v_C = V_s$. - [x] **Two key parameters:** $\alpha = \frac{R}{2L}$ (damping) and $\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ (natural frequency). - [x] **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = \alpha/\omega_0 = \frac{R}{2}\sqrt{\frac{C}{L}}$. - [x] **Three cases:** Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$), Critically Damped ($\zeta = 1$), Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$). - [x] **Critical resistance formula:** $R_{crit} = 2\sqrt{L/C}$ — memorize this! - [x] **Underdamped response:** $v_C(t) = V_s\left[1 - e^{-\alpha t}\left(\cos\omega_d t + \frac{\alpha}{\omega_d}\sin\omega_d t\right)\right]$. - [x] **Damped frequency:** $\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$ — this is what you measure on the DSO. - [x] **Peak overshoot:** $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ — occurs only in underdamped case. - [x] **Settling time (2%):** $t_s = 4/(\zeta\omega_0) = 8L/R$. - [x] **Instrument:** Always use a **DSO** (not CRO) for transient capture. Use **square wave** input for periodic re-triggering. - [x] **Lab formula verification:** Calculate $f_d = \omega_d/2\pi$ from theory, then **measure** the ringing period $T_d$ on DSO and verify $f_d = 1/T_d$. - [x] **Viva question to expect:** "What happens to the response as $R \to 0$?" → Pure LC oscillation, undamped sinusoidal ringing at $\omega_0$. - [x] **Viva question to expect:** "What is the physical significance of $Q$ factor?" → Higher $Q$ = sharper resonance = slower decay of oscillations.

topics.title: 3-phase power measurement using one-wattmeter and two-wattmeter methods and determination of reactive/apparent power drawn
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL307 - CIRCUITS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** 3-phase power measurement using one-wattmeter and two-wattmeter methods and determination of reactive/apparent power drawn

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-phase power measurement** is the process of determining the total active (real) power, reactive power, and apparent power consumed by a three-phase AC load using appropriate wattmeter connection schemes. The two principal methods employed in the PCEEL307 lab are the **One-Wattmeter Method** (valid for balanced loads only) and the **Two-Wattmeter Method** (valid for both balanced and unbalanced loads).

In a three-phase system, the **total instantaneous power** is constant (non-pulsating) — a property unique to polyphase systems with sinusoidal excitation. Mathematically:

$$P_{3\phi} = v_R i_R + v_Y i_Y + v_B i_B$$

The instruments (wattmeters) measure **average real power**, while reactive and apparent powers are derived algebraically from the wattmeter readings.

### 1.2 Classification of Three-Phase Power

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The student is expected to clearly distinguish between the three power types in a 3-phase system.

| Power Type | Symbol | Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Real (Active / True) Power | $P$ | **Watts (W) / kW** | Power actually consumed (converted to heat, light, mechanical work) |
| Reactive Power | $Q$ | **VAR / kVAR** | Power oscillating between source and load due to inductive/capacitive elements |
| Apparent Power | $S$ | **VA / kVA** | Vector sum magnitude; product of RMS voltage and current |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Beer Mug" Intuition

Imagine you are at a restaurant and the waiter brings a **frosty beer mug** filled to the brim with a mixture of beer and foam:

- **The liquid beer you can actually drink** = **Real Power (P)**. This is the "useful" component — the part of electrical energy that does real work (turns motors, heats resistors, lights bulbs).
- **The white foam on top** = **Reactive Power (Q)**. It occupies space in the mug, requires energy to create, but you cannot drink it. Reactive power is needed to maintain magnetic/electric fields in inductors and capacitors but performs no net work.
- **The total volume of the mug (beer + foam)** = **Apparent Power (S)**. This is what the power company has to "generate" and transmit — they must size their infrastructure for the entire mug, not just the liquid part.

> [!TIP]
> **Power Factor (pf)** = $P / S$ = (drinkable beer) / (total mug volume). A poor power factor (lots of foam) means the utility must supply more current for the same useful work, increasing $I^2R$ losses in transmission lines.

### 1.4 Why Different Wattmeter Methods Exist?

In a **single-phase** circuit, one wattmeter suffices because the power equation is simple. In a **three-phase** system, we have three voltages and three currents, but a wattmeter has only two terminals (a current coil and a voltage/potential coil). The challenge is to combine the readings of 1, 2, or 3 wattmeters to obtain the total power.

- **Single wattmeter**: Works only when the load is **balanced** AND accessible at the neutral point (for star loads).
- **Two wattmeters**: The **industry-standard method** — works for both balanced and unbalanced 3-wire systems (no neutral required). This is the **Blondel's Theorem** applied to 3-phase circuits.
- **Three wattmeters**: Used for 4-wire systems with neutral, especially with unbalanced loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blondel's Theorem (KTU Favourite):** *"The power in a polyphase system can be measured using $N$ wattmeters, where $N$ is the number of phases, with their current coils connected in each line and their voltage coils connected between that line and a common reference point."* For a 3-phase, 3-wire system, this common point can be a fictitious (virtual) neutral, leading to the two-wattmeter method.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The One-Wattmeter Method (Balanced Loads)

### Theory
In a balanced three-phase system, all three phases are identical in magnitude and displaced by 120° in time. Therefore, the power in each phase is equal. If we can measure the power in **one phase** and multiply by **three**, we obtain the total.

### Conditions of Validity
1. The load must be **perfectly balanced** (identical magnitude and phase angle in all three phases).
2. For a **Star (Y) connected load**, the neutral point must be accessible so that the wattmeter's voltage coil can be connected between the line and the neutral.
3. The supply voltages must be **sinusoidal and balanced**.

### Connection Topology
- **Current coil** of the wattmeter is inserted in **one line** (say R-phase line).
- **Voltage (pressure) coil** is connected between that same line (R) and the **neutral point N**.

### Reading Interpretation
Let $V_{ph}$ = phase voltage, $I_{ph}$ = phase current, $\phi$ = phase angle between $V_{ph}$ and $I_{ph}$.

$$W_1 = V_{ph} \cdot I_{ph} \cdot \cos(\phi)$$

$$P_{total} = 3 \cdot W_1 = 3 V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos(\phi)$$

For Y-load: $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{ph}$ and $I_L = I_{ph}$, so:

$$P_{total} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos(\phi)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** If the load is Delta-connected OR if the neutral is inaccessible (3-wire system), the one-wattmeter method **fails**. This is why the two-wattmeter method is preferred in industry.

---

## 2.2 The Two-Wattmeter Method (Blondel's Method)

This is the **most important method** for KTU examinations. It works for **any 3-phase, 3-wire system** (balanced or unbalanced) — provided the system has no neutral wire.

### Connection Topology
- **Wattmeter 1 (W₁):**
  - Current coil → in **Line R**
  - Voltage coil → between **Line R and Line Y** (measures $V_{RY}$)
- **Wattmeter 2 (W₂):**
  - Current coil → in **Line B**
  - Voltage coil → between **Line B and Line Y** (measures $V_{BY}$)
- The **Y-line is the common reference** for both voltage coils (acts as a virtual/artificial neutral created by the two voltage coils meeting at a common point).

### Reading Equations
Assume phase sequence is **R-Y-B (positive sequence)**, balanced load with phase angle $\phi$ (lagging):

$$W_1 = V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos(30^\circ - \phi)$$

$$W_2 = V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos(30^\circ + \phi)$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the single most important reference for the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).** Memorize every entry.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Real Power | $P = W_1 + W_2$ | W / kW | Valid for any load |
| 2 | Total Reactive Power | $Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot \vert W_1 - W_2 \vert$ | VAR / kVAR | Magnitude; sign indicates leading/lagging |
| 3 | Power Factor Angle | $\tan(\phi) = \sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 + W_2}$ | radians/degrees | Sign of $(W_1 - W_2)$ determines lag/lead |
| 4 | Apparent Power | $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ | VA / kVA | Always positive |
| 5 | Power Factor | $\text{pf} = \cos(\phi) = \dfrac{P}{S}$ | dimensionless | $\vert \cos(\phi) \vert \le 1$ |
| 6 | Power Factor (Direct) | $\text{pf} = \dfrac{W_1 + W_2}{2 W_1}$ | dimensionless | Alternative shortcut (when phase angle unknown) |
| 7 | One-Wattmeter Power | $P = 3 W_1$ | W | Only for balanced loads |
| 8 | One-Wattmeter Power (Line) | $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos(\phi)$ | W | Universal balanced 3-$\phi$ formula |
| 9 | Phase Voltage (Y) | $V_{ph} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ | V | $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{ph}$ |
| 10 | Phase Current (Delta) | $I_{ph} = \dfrac{I_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ | A | $I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{ph}$ |
| 11 | Reactive (per wattmeter) | $Q = \sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2)$ | VAR | Signed: positive for lag, negative for lead |

---

## 2.4 Power Triangle Relationships

> [!TIP]
> The **power triangle** is a right-angled triangle where $P$ (base) and $Q$ (perpendicular) are the two legs, and $S$ (hypotenuse) is the apparent power. The angle between $P$ and $S$ is the phase angle $\phi$.

| Geometric Relation | Trigonometric Relation |
|---|---|
| $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$ | $\sin(\phi) = Q/S$, $\cos(\phi) = P/S$, $\tan(\phi) = Q/P$ |
| $P = S \cos(\phi)$ | $Q = S \sin(\phi)$ |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Why Two-Wattmeter Method is Used |
|---|---|
| **Industrial Motor Testing** | Induction motors are 3-phase, 3-wire; the two-wattmeter method is the standard test-bench technique to measure input power. |
| **Power Quality Audits** | Industrial loads (welding machines, arc furnaces) are often unbalanced; two-wattmeter method captures true power. |
| **Energy Metering in Substations** | Three-phase energy meters are essentially two-wattmeter circuits with mechanical/electronic summation. |
| **Transformer Testing** | Open-circuit and short-circuit tests on 3-phase transformers use this method. |
| **Reactive Power Compensation** | Determining whether a plant needs leading or lagging VAR compensation (capacitor banks). |

---

## 2.6 Special Cases in Two-Wattmeter Method (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Load Condition | $\phi$ (deg) | $W_1$ Reading | $W_2$ Reading | Observation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pure Resistive | $0^\circ$ | $W_1 = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L I_L$ | $W_2 = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L I_L$ | $W_1 = W_2 > 0$ |
| pf = 0.5 lagging | $60^\circ$ | $W_1 = V_L I_L$ | $W_2 = 0$ | One wattmeter reads zero |
| pf < 0.5 lagging | $\phi > 60^\circ$ | $W_1 > 0$ | $W_2 < 0$ | **W₂ reads negative** — reverse its pressure coil leads to take reading |
| Pure Inductive | $90^\circ$ | $W_1 = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L I_L$ | $W_2 = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L I_L$ | Readings equal in magnitude, opposite in sign |
| Pure Capacitive | $-90^\circ$ | $W_1 = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L I_L$ | $W_2 = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L I_L$ | Roles reverse |
