## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define an instrument transformer. Why is it preferred over a shunt or multiplier for high-current and high-voltage AC measurement?**
*(Module 2 — CO2 — Bloom Level: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
An instrument transformer is a precision electromagnetic device used in conjunction with measuring instruments to extend their range in AC circuits. There are two types: **Current Transformer (CT)** for current measurement and **Potential Transformer (PT)** for voltage measurement. They are preferred over shunts and multipliers because (i) they provide **galvanic isolation** between the high-voltage power circuit and the instrument, ensuring operator safety; (ii) they handle very high power levels (kA and kV) with negligible power loss in the measuring circuit; (iii) the secondary outputs are standardized to **5 A and 110 V**, allowing the same instrument to be used over a wide range; and (iv) shunts dissipate large $I^2R$ losses at high currents and multipliers become impractically large and unstable at high voltages. Instrument transformers, in contrast, are efficient, accurate, and safe for continuous operation in substations and industrial panels.

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2. State any three differences between a Current Transformer and a Potential Transformer.**
*(Module 2 — CO2 — Bloom Level: Remember)*

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Current Transformer (CT) | Potential Transformer (PT) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Primary is connected in **series** with the line. | Primary is connected in **shunt (parallel)** across the line. |
| 2 | Steps down **current**; secondary rated at **5 A**. | Steps down **voltage**; secondary rated at **110 V**. |
| 3 | Secondary must **never be open-circuited** — open secondary causes dangerously high voltage and core saturation. | Secondary can be open-circuited safely (it behaves like a no-load transformer). |
| 4 | Primary turns are very few (1 to 5); secondary turns are large. | Both primary and secondary have many turns. |
| 5 | Operates near **short-circuit** condition. | Operates near **open-circuit (no-load)** condition. |
| 6 | Burden is expressed in **ohms (Ω)**. | Burden is expressed in **volt-amperes (VA)**. |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention in ratio error:** Many students write $\epsilon_r = (K_i - K_n)/K_n$ instead of $(K_n - K_i)/K_i$. This leads to a 2-mark deduction. Memorize the **standard KTU textbook definition** verbatim.
> 2. **Confusing the turn ratio direction:** For a CT, $K_t = N_s / N_p$ (large); for a PT, $K_t = N_p / N_s$ (large). Mixing these up will make every numerical wrong. Note that in a PT, primary is HIGH voltage with MANY turns.
> 3. **Ignoring the "open secondary" hazard in CT:** When asked "what happens if CT secondary is opened?", students often say "no current flows" — this is dangerous misinformation. The correct answer involves flux runaway, saturation, and lethal over-voltage.
> 4. **Not converting phase angle from radians to minutes:** A common 1-mark loss in the phase-error sub-question.
> 5. **Reporting burden in wrong units:** Burden for a CT is in $\Omega$, for a PT it is in VA. Examiners will deduct marks for this.

---

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module Internal Choice, Q11A]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat phasor diagram, derive the expression for the **ratio error of a Current Transformer**. Discuss the factors that affect the ratio error and explain how it can be minimized. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A CT with a nominal ratio of **400 A / 5 A** has a primary winding of 1 turn and a secondary winding of 80 turns. When the primary carries 300 A, the secondary ammeter reads 3.72 A. Calculate:
- (i) The actual transformation ratio $K_i$
- (ii) The ratio error $\epsilon_r$
- (iii) The phase angle error if the magnetizing current is 1.5 A and the loss angle is $30°$
*(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation of Ratio Error of a CT:**

The exact ampere-turn equation of a CT is:

$$N_p \vec{I_p} = N_s \vec{I_s} + N_p \vec{I_m}$$

Rearranging for primary current:

$$\vec{I_p} = \frac{N_s}{N_p} \vec{I_s} + \vec{I_m} = K_t \vec{I_s} + \vec{I_m}$$

The **actual transformation ratio** is the magnitude of the phasor ratio of $I_p$ to $I_s$:

$$K_i = \frac{\vert I_p \vert}{\vert I_s \vert} = \left\vert K_t + \frac{I_m}{I_s} \right\vert$$

For small magnetizing current relative to $K_t I_s$:

$$K_i \approx K_t + \frac{I_m}{I_s} \cos(\beta - \alpha)$$

The **nominal ratio** is:

$$K_n = \frac{I_{p,rated}}{I_{s,rated}}$$

The **ratio error** is:

$$\epsilon_r = \frac{K_n - K_i}{K_i} \times 100\%$$

Substituting and noting that for small errors $K_i \approx K_n$ in the denominator:

$$\epsilon_r \approx -\frac{I_m \cos(\beta - \alpha)}{N_s I_s} \times 100\%$$

**Factors affecting ratio error:**
- Magnitude of magnetizing current $I_m$ (depends on core material, flux density, and burden)
- Magnitude of secondary current $I_s$ (lower $I_s$ increases error)
- Burden impedance (higher burden → higher error)
- Frequency of operation
- Temperature of windings

**Minimization techniques:**
- Use high-permeability core materials (CRGO, nanocrystalline, permalloy)
- Keep the flux density low (use larger core cross-section)
- Minimize the burden impedance
- Use a turns correction (extra fractional turn on secondary) to compensate
- Maintain the CT within its rated frequency

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given: Nominal ratio $K_n = 400/5 = 80$. Primary turns $N_p = 1$. Secondary turns $N_s = 80$. Primary current $I_p = 300$ A. Secondary ammeter reading $I_s = 3.72$ A. Magnetizing current $I_m = 1.5$ A. Loss angle $\alpha = 30°$.

**(i) Actual transformation ratio:**

$$K_i = \frac{I_p}{I_s} = \frac{300}{3.72} = 80.6452$$

**(ii) Ratio error:**

$$\epsilon_r = \frac{K_n - K_i}{K_i} \times 100 = \frac{80 - 80.6452}{80.6452} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{-0.6452}{80.6452} \times 100 = -0.8000\%$$

So the ratio error is approximately **−0.80%**.

**(iii) Phase angle error:**

The phase angle error in radians is given by:

$$\theta \approx \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{I_m \sin(\beta - \alpha)}{N_s I_s}\right)$$

Taking $\beta \approx 90°$ for a typical transformer (magnetizing current lags flux by $90°$ approximately):

$$\beta - \alpha = 90° - 30° = 60°$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1.5 \times \sin 60°}{80 \times 3.72}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1.5 \times 0.8660}{297.6}\right)$$

$$= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1.299}{297.6}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.004365) = 0.2500°$$

Converting to **minutes of arc**:

$$\theta = 0.2500 \times 60 = 15 \text{ minutes}$$

Hence the phase angle error is **15 minutes** (approximately).

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module Internal Choice, Q11B]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Potential Transformer (PT)**. Derive the expression for its **ratio error**. Mention the standard secondary voltage ratings. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A PT with a turns ratio of **200 : 1** is used to measure the voltage of an 11 kV line. The secondary voltmeter reads **54.8 V**. The phase angle between the primary voltage phasor and the reversed secondary phasor is measured to be $18$ minutes. Calculate:
- (i) The actual voltage ratio $K_v$
- (ii) The ratio error in percent
- (iii) The percentage error in the measurement of power if the load has a power factor of **0.8 lagging**
*(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Construction, Working, and Ratio Error of a PT:**

A Potential Transformer (PT) is a precision step-down transformer used for measuring high AC voltages safely. **Constructionally**, it consists of:
- A **high-permeability silicon steel core** (shell-type or core-type), designed to minimize core losses and magnetizing current.
- A **primary winding** with a large number of turns of fine-gauge insulated copper wire, designed to withstand the full line voltage (with appropriate insulation and taping).
- A **secondary winding** with a smaller number of turns, designed to deliver a standardized low voltage (110 V line-to-line or 110/√3 V phase-to-neutral).
- Both windings are assembled on the core, impregnated with insulating varnish, and housed in a robust oil-filled or cast-resin tank for high-voltage applications.
- A **fuse** is connected in series with the primary for short-circuit protection, and the secondary is grounded at one terminal for safety.

**Working:** The primary is connected across the high-voltage line. The secondary is connected to a high-impedance voltmeter (or the voltage coils of wattmeters/energy meters). The transformer steps the voltage down by the turns ratio. Standard secondary voltage ratings are **110 V (line-to-line)** for three-phase systems and **110/√3 V ≈ 63.5 V (phase-to-neutral)** for systems with phase voltages.

**Derivation of ratio error:**

From the equivalent circuit of the PT referred to the primary, the exact voltage equation is:

$$\vec{V_p} = \vec{V_s}' + (R_1 + R_2')\vec{I_p} + j(X_1 + X_2')\vec{I_p} - jX_m \vec{I_m} + \vec{V_{correction}}$$

For small voltage drops, the **actual voltage ratio** is:

$$K_v = \frac{V_p}{V_s} = K_t \left[ 1 + \frac{(R_1 + R_2')\cos\phi + (X_1 + X_2')\sin\phi}{V_s'} \right]$$

The **ratio error** is:

$$\epsilon_r = \frac{K_n - K_v}{K_v} \times 100\% \approx -\frac{(R_1 + R_2')\cos\phi + (X_1 + X_2')\sin\phi}{V_s'} \times 100\%$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given: Turns ratio $N_p : N_s = 200 : 1$, so $K_t = 200$. Line (primary) voltage nominal $V_{p,rated} = 11000$ V. Secondary voltmeter reading $V_s = 54.8$ V. Phase angle error $\delta = 18$ minutes. Load power factor $\cos\phi = 0.8$ lagging, so $\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.8) = 36.87°$.

**(i) Actual voltage ratio:**

$$K_v = \frac{V_{p,actual}}{V_s}$$

The *nominal* primary voltage is 11000 V (the actual primary is what we want to find). The actual voltage ratio based on the turns ratio is $K_t = 200$ approximately, but the actual ratio is computed using the indicated primary voltage (i.e., nominal):

$$K_v = \frac{K_n \times V_s}{V_s} \times \frac{V_{p,indicated}}{V_{p,actual}} = \frac{200 \times 54.8}{V_{p,actual}}$$

However, the typical KTU approach: we first compute the **indicated primary voltage** (what the instrument reports):

$$V_{p,indicated} = K_n \times V_s = 200 \times 54.8 = 10960 \text{ V}$$

The actual primary voltage is the line voltage, but for the ratio error calculation, we compare nominal vs. actual ratio based on readings. Using the conventional KTU approach for a PT with no separate actual primary given, we take:

$$K_v = \frac{V_{p,line}}{V_s} = \frac{11000}{54.8} = 200.730 \text{ (assuming line is exactly 11 kV)}$$

For a lab experiment, however, both $V_p$ and $V_s$ are measured simultaneously, so $K_v = V_p / V_s$ directly.

**(ii) Ratio error:**

$$\epsilon_r = \frac{K_n - K_v}{K_v} \times 100 = \frac{200 - 200.730}{200.730} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{-0.730}{200.730} \times 100 = -0.3636\% \approx -0.36\%$$

**(iii) Percentage error in power measurement:**

True power factor angle: $\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.8) = 36.87°$
Phase angle error: $\delta = 18$ minutes $= 18/60 = 0.30° = 0.005236$ rad

The wattmeter reads $\cos(\phi \pm \delta)$ instead of $\cos(\phi)$.

For a lagging load and a typical PT phase lead, the apparent power factor is:

$$\cos(\phi + \delta) = \cos(36.87° + 0.30°) = \cos(37.17°)$$

Computing:
$$\cos(36.87°) = 0.8000$$
$$\cos(37.17°) \approx 0.7970$$

The percentage error in power is:

$$\%\text{error} = \frac{\cos(\phi + \delta) - \cos\phi}{\cos\phi} \times 100 = \frac{0.7970 - 0.8000}{0.8000} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{-0.0030}{0.8000} \times 100 = -0.375\%$$

Hence the wattmeter will under-read the true power by approximately **0.375%**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2**

- **Instrument Transformer** = a precision transformer used to extend the range of AC measuring instruments; provides isolation, standardization, and safety.
- **CT (Current Transformer):** Primary in **series**, secondary standardized at **5 A** (or 1 A). Steps down current. Turns ratio $K_t = N_s / N_p$ is *large*.
- **PT (Potential Transformer):** Primary in **parallel (shunt)**, secondary standardized at **110 V** (or 110/√3 V). Steps down voltage. Turns ratio $K_t = N_p / N_s$ is *large*.
- **CT secondary must NEVER be open-circuited** — this causes flux runaway, core saturation, overheating, and lethal over-voltage. Always short the secondary through a burden or ammeter.
- **PT secondary can be open-circuited safely** — it behaves like a lightly loaded power transformer.
- **Ratio error formula (CT):** $\epsilon_r = (K_n - K_i)/K_i \times 100\%$ — typically negative for CTs.
- **Ratio error formula (PT):** $\epsilon_r = (K_n - K_v)/K_v \times 100\%$ — sign depends on burden power factor.
- **Phase angle error** is the angular displacement in minutes between primary phasor and reversed secondary phasor — critical for power/energy measurement because $\cos(\phi \pm \delta) \neq \cos\phi$.
- **Burden** is the total impedance/load connected to the secondary. CT burden in $\Omega$, PT burden in VA.
- **Burden of a CT** = total impedance of the secondary loop. Excessive burden increases ratio error and causes saturation.
- **Burden of a PT** = total VA consumed by the voltmeter and voltage coils. Higher burden → larger ratio error.
- **Core flux density in a CT is very low** (≈ 0.1 T) because the secondary is near-shorted; in a PT it is normal (≈ 1.2–1.5 T).
- **Standard accuracy classes** for metering CTs/PTs: 0.2, 0.5, 1, 3. Lower number = higher accuracy.
- **Protection CTs** (class 5P, 10P, PS) have different accuracy requirements than metering CTs.
- **Indicated Primary Quantity** = Nominal Ratio × Secondary Reading.
- **Phase angle conversion:** 1° = 60 minutes, 1 radian = 3438 minutes.
- **Sign convention in KTU exams:** Always use the textbook definition $\epsilon_r = (K_n - K_{actual})/K_{actual} \times 100\%$. Memorize this verbatim.
- **Lab experiment essentials:** (1) Measure primary and secondary simultaneously; (2) vary burden or load current in steps; (3) plot $\epsilon_r$ vs. burden or vs. $I_p$; (4) compute phase angle from wattmeter readings; (5) ground the secondary for safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Takeaway:** Instrument transformers are the *silent enablers* of every high-voltage measurement. The KTU lab on this topic tests your ability to (a) wire a CT and PT correctly, (b) measure primary/secondary quantities simultaneously, (c) compute ratio error and phase error from raw data, and (d) interpret the results in terms of accuracy class and burden compliance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of 1-phase Energy meter at various power factors and phantom loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL307 - CIRCUITS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of 1-phase Energy meter at various power factors and phantom loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Energy Meter Calibration** is the experimental procedure of comparing the registration (energy recorded) of a single-phase induction-type watt-hour meter against a known reference standard under precisely controlled test conditions, to determine its **percentage error** and verify compliance with accuracy class limits prescribed by **IS 722 / IEC 62053-21**.

**Phantom Loading** (also called *phantom loading* or *virtual loading*) is a specialized laboratory testing technique in which the **current coil** and **pressure (voltage) coil** of the energy meter are energized from **two independent, electrically isolated sources**. The phase angle between the test current and the test voltage is deliberately adjusted (typically 0°, 60°, or 0° leading) to simulate **various power factors (U.P.F, 0.5 lagging, 0.8 lagging)** — *without* actually drawing the corresponding real power from the mains supply.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCEEL307 – Module 2):**
> The experiment evaluates **CO1 (Apply measurement principles)** and **CO2 (Analyze instrument errors)**. Students must demonstrate competence in *circuit wiring, time-instrumentation, error computation, and tabulation* — all of which carry weightage in the continuous evaluation (CE) and end-semester lab examination.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **weighing scale** that is supposed to measure 50 kg. To *calibrate* it, you do not necessarily need to load it with 50 kg of real iron. Instead, you cleverly apply a **known calibrated force** through a mechanical lever system to make the scale behave *as if* 50 kg were placed on it. The scale's response is observed, and the difference between "what should be" and "what is" is the **error**.

In the electrical world, a 1-phase energy meter rated for, say, **5 A, 230 V** would normally consume a real power of $5 \times 230 = 1150 \text{ W} = 1.15 \text{ kW}$. Running such a load for a long time is wasteful, expensive, and thermally stressful. **Phantom loading** cleverly supplies the **full voltage** to the pressure coil (say, 230 V at no load) and the **full current** to the current coil (say, 5 A) — but from separate, phase-shifted sources. The meter experiences the *correct magnetic flux conditions* of a real load, but the **net true power drawn from the supply is only the small no-load copper + iron losses**, often as low as **10–20 W** instead of 1150 W.

> [!NOTE]
> **The key insight:** The meter does not "know" the current and voltage are from different sources. It only senses the *magnitudes* and the *phase angle* between them. If we set the phase angle to $\cos^{-1}(0.5) = 60°$, the meter *believes* it is measuring at 0.5 power factor lag.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Meter constant ($K$ or $K_h$):** Revolutions per kilowatt-hour (rev/kWh) — usually stamped on the meter nameplate.
- **Standard reference energy:** Determined using a precision **wattmeter + stopwatch** combination (or electronic reference standard).
- **Rated secondary standards of Indian reference meters:** **±0.2 %** accuracy.
- **Class 1.0 energy meter accuracy limit:** **±2 % error at unity power factor** and **±2.5 % at 0.5 power factor lag** (as per IS 722).
- **Standard test points (KTU lab convention):** U.P.F (cos φ = 1.0), 0.8 P.F lag, 0.5 P.F lag.
- **Standard load durations:** Typically 50–100 revolutions of the aluminum disc are counted, and the time is measured.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of a Single-Phase Induction Energy Meter

The energy meter operates on the **Ferraris principle** (induction type), using the interaction of two alternating fluxes to produce a torque proportional to **power**:

1. **Pressure coil (PC):** Highly inductive (large number of turns on a small cross-section), connected across the supply. It produces a flux $\Phi_p$ **lagging the supply voltage $V$ by nearly 90°** (because of its high inductance-to-resistance ratio).

2. **Current coil (CC):** Few turns of thick wire, connected in series with the load. The flux $\Phi_c$ produced is **in phase with the load current $I$**.

3. **Driving torque ($T_d$):** The interaction of these two fluxes with the **aluminum disc** induces eddy currents, which interacting with the fluxes produce a torque proportional to **VI cos φ** (i.e., real power):
$$T_d \;\propto\; V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$$

4. **Braking torque ($T_b$):** A permanent magnet (C-shaped) produces a flux $\Phi_m$ that induces eddy currents in the disc, creating a retarding torque proportional to **disc speed $N$**:
$$T_b \;\propto\; N$$

5. **Steady-state condition** ($T_d = T_b$) gives a disc speed proportional to power:
$$N \;\propto\; V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$$

6. **Total revolutions in time $t$** is therefore proportional to **energy**:
$$\text{Revolutions} \;\propto\; \int_0^t VI\cos\phi\,dt \;=\; \text{Energy (kWh)}$$

### 2.2 The Phantom Loading Technique — Why and How

#### 2.2.1 Why Phantom Loading is Needed

| Issue with Direct Loading | Phantom Loading Solution |
|---|---|
| High power consumption (e.g., 230 V × 5 A = 1150 W continuously) | Net real power drawn is only the *no-load loss* of the instrument (≈ 5–15 W) |
| Requires bulky rheostats / lamp banks | Uses low-loss, compact variacs and low-voltage current sources |
| Heat dissipation in lab is uncomfortable | Lab remains cool and safe |
| Power factor adjustment requires inductive + resistive load combinations | Achieved by simply shifting the phase of a separate low-voltage source using a phase-shifting transformer |

#### 2.2.2 How Phantom Loading Works (Logical Steps)

1. **Isolate the meter's voltage and current paths** by removing the actual series load.
2. **Energize the pressure coil (PC)** at *rated voltage* (e.g., 230 V, 50 Hz) from the **mains supply** through a variac.
3. **Energize the current coil (CC)** at *low voltage but rated current* (e.g., 5 A at 6–10 V) from a **separate low-voltage transformer** (called the *phantom load transformer* or *current supply transformer*).
4. **Introduce a phase-shifting network** (either a **phase-shifting transformer** or a **RC network**) between the two sources so that the phase angle between $V$ (across PC) and $I$ (through CC) can be set to the desired power factor.
5. The meter "sees" a flux configuration identical to a real load, but the **actual kW drawn from the supply** is just the PC loss + CC loss, which is minimal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Every electricity board's energy meter testing lab, BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) certification labs, and meter manufacturing quality control divisions use phantom loading as the *standard* test method. Without it, calibration of 1000+ meters per day would be economically and environmentally unviable.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Real power drawn by load (in real loading) | $P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | W (or kW) | $V$ = line voltage, $I$ = line current |
| 2 | Energy recorded by meter in $N$ revolutions | $E_m = \dfrac{N}{K_h}$ | kWh | $K_h$ = meter constant (rev/kWh) |
| 3 | True / Reference energy (using standard wattmeter) | $E_t = \dfrac{P_w \times t}{1000 \times 3600}$ | kWh | $P_w$ in W, $t$ in seconds |
| 4 | Equivalent true time for $N$ revolutions | $T_t = \dfrac{3600 \times N \times 1000}{K_h \times P_w} = \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^6 \times N}{K_h \times P_w}$ | s | $P_w$ in W |
| 5 | Measured (observed) time for $N$ revolutions | $T_m$ | s | Stopwatch reading |
| 6 | **Percentage Error** (KTU standard formula) | $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{T_t - T_m}{T_m} \times 100$ | % | Positive = meter runs **fast**; Negative = meter runs **slow** |
| 7 | Alternative error formula (using energy) | $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{E_t - E_m}{E_m} \times 100$ | % | Equivalent to the time-based form |
| 8 | Alternative error formula (using meter constant) | $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{K_h \cdot E_t - N}{N} \times 100$ | % | Used when $K_h$ is the *nameplate* value |
| 9 | Phantom load current | $I_{ph} = \dfrac{P_{true,simulated}}{V_{PC}}$ (referenced through step-down x'mer) | A | $V_{PC}$ is the secondary of the low-voltage transformer |
| 10 | Disc speed (theoretical) | $N_{rev/min} = \dfrac{P \times 60}{1000 \times K_h}$ | rev/min | $P$ in kW |
| 11 | Power factor (phantom) | $\cos\phi = \cos(\theta_V - \theta_I)$ | dimensionless | $\theta_V - \theta_I$ is the *phase shift* introduced |
| 12 | Class accuracy (Class 1.0) | $\vert \%\text{Error} \vert \le 2\%$ at UPF; $\le 2.5\%$ at 0.5 PF lag | % | IS 722 / IEC 62053-21 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Board Pitfall:** Many students mistakenly compute $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{T_m - T_t}{T_t} \times 100$. The **correct reference** is the **measured (observed) time $T_m$** in the denominator. Always use the formula in **row 6** above as taught in the KTU model lab manual.

### 2.4 The Three Test Conditions (KTU 2024 Lab Standard)

| S.No | Test Condition | Cos φ Setting | Phase Shift Network | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Unity Power Factor** | $\cos\phi = 1.0$ | No phase shift (or trim for exact UPF) | Verify base accuracy |
| 2 | **0.8 Power Factor Lag** | $\cos\phi = 0.8$ | Phase shift = $\cos^{-1}(0.8) = 36.87°$ lag | Verify accuracy under typical industrial PF |
| 3 | **0.5 Power Factor Lag** | $\cos\phi = 0.5$ | Phase shift = $\cos^{-1}(0.5) = 60°$ lag | Worst-case accuracy check (most stressed) |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Disc Speed Equation

The **driving torque** on the aluminum disc, under steady sinusoidal operation, is:
$$T_d = K_1 \cdot \Phi_p \cdot \Phi_c \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

where $\alpha$ is the angle between $\Phi_p$ and $\Phi_c$.

Since $\Phi_p \propto V$ (and lags $V$ by 90° due to the inductive pressure coil) and $\Phi_c \propto I$ (and is in phase with $I$), the angle between the two fluxes is $(90° - \phi)$, where $\phi$ is the load power factor angle. Therefore:
$$\sin(\alpha) = \sin(90° - \phi) = \cos\phi$$

This yields:
$$T_d = K_2 \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi \;=\; K_2 \cdot P$$

The **braking torque** from the permanent magnet, with the disc rotating at angular speed $\omega_d$:
$$T_b = K_3 \cdot \omega_d$$

At steady state, $T_d = T_b$:
$$K_2 \cdot P = K_3 \cdot \omega_d \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_d = \dfrac{K_2}{K_3} \cdot P$$

Converting angular speed to revolutions per unit time and expressing $P$ in kW with $K_h$ (rev/kWh):
$$\text{Revolutions per hour} = K_h \cdot P \quad \text{(in kW)}$$

For a finite interval $t$ (in hours), the total revolutions:
$$N = K_h \cdot P \cdot t$$

Solving for time $t$ (in seconds) to complete $N$ revolutions at constant power $P$ (in W):
$$t = \dfrac{N \cdot 3600 \cdot 1000}{K_h \cdot P} \;=\; \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^6 \cdot N}{K_h \cdot P}$$

This is the **true time** $T_t$ that *should* be measured by the stopwatch if the meter were ideal.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Percentage Error Formula

Let the *actual measured time* be $T_m$ (in seconds) for the same $N$ revolutions. Since the meter may run *faster or slower* than ideal, $T_m$ will differ from $T_t$.

By definition:
$$T_t \;\propto\; \dfrac{1}{P_{\text{true}}} \quad \text{and} \quad T_m \;\propto\; \dfrac{1}{P_{\text{indicated}}}$$

The **error in the meter's indicated power** is:
$$\Delta P = P_{\text{indicated}} - P_{\text{true}}$$

The fractional (per-unit) error:
$$\dfrac{\Delta P}{P_{\text{true}}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{indicated}} - P_{\text{true}}}{P_{\text{true}}} = \dfrac{T_t - T_m}{T_m}$$

Multiplying by 100 gives the **percentage error**:
$$\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{T_t - T_m}{T_m} \times 100$$

**Step-by-step interpretation:**
- If $T_m < T_t$ (disc took *less* time to complete $N$ revolutions) → meter is **fast** → **positive error**.
- If $T_m > T_t$ (disc took *more* time) → meter is **slow** → **negative error**.
- If $T_m = T_t$ → meter is **perfect** (0% error — extremely rare in practice).

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (UPF Case)

**Given:**
- Meter constant $K_h = 600 \text{ rev/kWh}$ (typical 1-phase induction meter)
- Test current $I = 5 \text{ A}$ at U.P.F
- Test voltage across PC $V = 230 \text{ V}$
- Number of revolutions counted $N = 50$
- Stopwatch reading for $N = 50$ revs: $T_m = 295 \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — True power** (UPF, so $\cos\phi = 1$):
$$P_t = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = 230 \times 5 \times 1 = 1150 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — True time** for 50 revolutions at rated $K_h = 600$:
$$T_t = \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^6 \times 50}{600 \times 1150} = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^8}{6.9 \times 10^5} \approx 260.87 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage error:**
$$\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{260.87 - 295}{295} \times 100 = \dfrac{-34.13}{295} \times 100 \approx -11.57\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The negative sign indicates the meter is running **slow** (i.e., the disc is taking *more* time than ideal to complete 50 revolutions). Such a meter **fails** the Class 1.0 accuracy criterion (which permits $\pm 2\%$) and would require **re-calibration or adjustment** of the brake magnet position or the lag adjustment.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (0.5 PF Lag Case)

**Given:**
- Same meter, $K_h = 600 \text{ rev/kWh}$
- Test current $I = 5 \text{ A}$, voltage $V = 230 \text{ V}$ (rated)
- $\cos\phi = 0.5$ lagging → $\phi = 60°$
- $N = 30$ revolutions counted
- $T_m = 412 \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — True power:**
$$P_t = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = 230 \times 5 \times 0.5 = 575 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — True time:**
$$T_t = \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^6 \times 30}{600 \times 575} = \dfrac{1.08 \times 10^8}{3.45 \times 10^5} \approx 313.04 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage error:**
$$\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{313.04 - 412}{412} \times 100 = \dfrac{-98.96}{412} \times 100 \approx -24.02\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At 0.5 PF lag, the **IS 722 permissible error is ±2.5%**. A reading of −24.02% means this meter is grossly inaccurate and is **not fit for billing**. In a real lab, after a fresh calibration, expect errors within **±2% to ±3%** — these are typical acceptable values for a healthy meter.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Why Error Increases at Lower Power Factor

| Factor | At UPF (cos φ = 1.0) | At 0.5 PF Lag (cos φ = 0.5) |
|---|---|---|
| Real power drawn | 100% of $V \cdot I$ | 50% of $V \cdot I$ |
| Driving torque magnitude | $K_2 \cdot V \cdot I$ (max) | $K_2 \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 0.5$ (half) |
| Disc speed | Faster | Slower |
| Friction torque (assumed constant) | Small fraction of $T_d$ | **Larger fraction** of $T_d$ — becomes significant |
| **Net effect on accuracy** | Closer to ideal | Error grows due to non-linear friction, PC coil phase errors, and eddy current loss compensation |
| IS 722 tolerance band | $\pm 2\%$ | $\pm 2.5\%$ (wider — acknowledges difficulty) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the tolerance band widen at low PF?** Because the *signal-to-noise ratio* worsens. The driving torque is smaller, while the friction and phase-angle errors (e.g., from the resistance of the pressure coil) remain roughly constant. Hence the *relative* error grows, and the standard allows a slightly larger band.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Phantom Loading Test Bench

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Mains[3 Phase or 1 Phase Mains 230V 50Hz] --> Var1[Variac for Pressure Coil]
    Mains --> PST[Phase Shifting Transformer]
    PST --> Var2[Variac for Current Source]
    Var1 --> PC[Energy Meter Pressure Coil 230V]
    Var2 --> CT[Step Down Current Transformer]
    CT --> CC[Energy Meter Current Coil 5A Low V]
    PC --> MeterDisc[Aluminum Disc and Brake Magnet Assembly]
    CC --> MeterDisc
    MeterDisc --> Counter[Mechanical Rev Counter with Start Stop Marks]
    Counter --> Stopwatch[Electronic Stopwatch Resolution 0.01 s]
    Var1 --> StdWattmeter[Standard Wattmeter Voltage Coil]
    PST --> StdWattmeter
    CT --> StdWattmeter
    StdWattmeter --> PowerReadout[Reference Power Reading P_w]
    Stopwatch --> T_m[Measured Time T_m]
    PowerReadout --> Compute[Computation Block True Time and Percent Error]
    T_m --> Compute
    MeterDisc --> K_h[Meter Constant from Nameplate rev per kWh]
    K_h --> Compute
    Compute --> Result[Percentage Error and Calibration Conclusion]
```

### 4.2 Circuit Schematic of Phantom Loading Connections (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SupplySection["Test Supply Section"]
        S1[Mains 230V 50Hz] -->|Phase| A1
        S1 -->|Neutral| A2
        A1 --> L1[Variac Primary]
        L1 --> L2[Variac Secondary 0 to 270V]
        L2 --> A3
    end
    subgraph VoltagePath["Pressure Coil Path"]
        A3 --> PCplus[Pressure Coil Top Terminal]
        A2 --> PCminus[Pressure Coil Bottom Terminal]
        PCplus --> PCcore[PC Magnetic Circuit]
        PCminus --> PCcore
        PCcore --> Flux1[Flux Phi_p lags V by 90 deg]
    end
    subgraph CurrentPath["Current Coil Path from Phase Shifter"]
        PST1[Phase Shifting Transformer Secondary] --> CT1[Current Transformer Step Down 10V 5A]
        CT1 --> CCplus[Current Coil Top Terminal]
        CCminus[Current Coil Bottom Terminal]
        CCplus --> CCcore[CC Magnetic Circuit]
        CCminus --> CCcore
        CCcore --> Flux2[Flux Phi_c in phase with I]
    end
    subgraph DiscAssembly["Disc and Brake Assembly"]
        Flux1 --> Disc[Aluminum Disc]
        Flux2 --> Disc
        Disc --> BrakeMagnet[Permanent Magnet Brake]
        BrakeMagnet --> T_brake[Braking Torque T_b proportional to N]
    end
    subgraph Measurement["Reference Measurement Section"]
        StdW[Standard Wattmeter] -.measures.-> PCplus
        StdW -.measures.-> CCplus
        StdW --> P_reading[Reference Power P_w in W]
        Disc --> Counter_Mark[Revolution Counter Mark]
        Counter_Mark --> Stopwatch_M[Stopwatch Records T_m]
        P_reading --> Calc[True Time and Error Computation]
        Stopwatch_M --> Calc
    end
```

### 4.3 Tabular Schematic: Terminal Connection Summary

| Energy Meter Terminal | Connected From | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase (in)** | Variac output (rated voltage, e.g., 230 V) | Energizes the pressure coil (PC) |
| **Neutral** | Mains neutral | Returns PC current |
| **Load (in) — top** | Low-voltage side of current transformer | Current coil (CC) input |
| **Load (out) — bottom** | Common return of low-voltage source | Current coil (CC) return |
| **CC + PC common** (inside meter) | Internally connected to neutral of pressure coil in most meters | Reference for both coils |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wiring Pitfall (KTU lab, very common error):** In a typical 1-phase household energy meter, the *current coil is in series* with the load, and the *pressure coil is across the supply*. Students sometimes wire the variac output directly to the **load terminal** instead of the **phase input** of the meter. This bypasses the current coil entirely and the meter disc will not rotate. Always verify by checking that the disc rotates smoothly when only the pressure coil is energized (light running test).

### 4.4 Calibration Procedure Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Inspect meter and note nameplate K_h] --> S2[Step 2 Connect pressure coil to rated voltage 230V through variac]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Light running test disc should rotate slowly with no CC current]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Set phase shifter to 0 degrees for UPF]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Energize CC at rated current 5A from low V source]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Read standard wattmeter P_w]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Count N revolutions typically 50 and record T_m with stopwatch]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Compute T_t using formula and percent error]
    S8 --> S9{Is error within 2 percent}
    S9 -->|Yes| S10[Record UPF reading in observation table]
    S9 -->|No| S11[Adjust brake magnet and repeat]
    S10 --> S12[Step 9 Set phase shifter to cos inverse 0.8 equals 36.87 deg lag]
    S11 --> S12
    S12 --> S13[Step 10 Repeat steps 5 to 8 for 0.8 PF lag]
    S13 --> S14[Step 11 Set phase shifter to cos inverse 0.5 equals 60 deg lag]
    S14 --> S15[Step 12 Repeat steps 5 to 8 for 0.5 PF lag]
    S15 --> S16[Step 13 Tabulate all readings compute average error and draw conclusions]
    S16 --> S17[Step 14 Compare with IS 722 accuracy class limits]
    S17 --> End[Calibration Complete]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
*What is phantom loading? Why is it preferred over direct loading for energy meter calibration?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Phantom loading is a laboratory technique in which the **pressure coil (PC)** and **current coil (CC)** of an energy meter are energized from **two independent, isolated sources** with an adjustable phase shift between them, simulating a load of any desired power factor. It is preferred over direct loading because the **actual real power consumed from the supply is only the small no-load losses of the meter** (typically a few watts), compared to the full rated load (e.g., 1.15 kW for a 5 A, 230 V meter). This makes it **economical, thermally safe, and space-efficient** for prolonged testing at multiple power factors.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
*Define meter constant. The nameplate of a single-phase energy meter reads 600 rev/kWh. How many revolutions should the disc complete in 5 minutes at a load of 1 kW at UPF?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Meter constant ($K_h$)** is the number of revolutions made by the aluminum disc of an energy meter per unit of energy consumed (1 kWh). It is a measure of the meter's speed.
- Energy consumed in 5 minutes at 1 kW UPF = $1 \text{ kW} \times \dfrac{5}{60} \text{ h} = \dfrac{1}{12} \text{ kWh} = 0.0833 \text{ kWh}$
- Number of revolutions = $K_h \times E = 600 \times 0.0833 = 50 \text{ revolutions}$

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **phantom loading** method of testing a single-phase induction-type energy meter. Explain how unity, 0.8 lagging, and 0.5 lagging power factors are simulated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a calibration test on a single-phase energy meter with $K_h = 450$ rev/kWh, the following readings were obtained at UPF, rated voltage and rated current (5 A, 230 V):

| Trial | Number of Revolutions ($N$) | Time Measured $T_m$ (s) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 40 | 318 |
| 2 | 40 | 320 |
| 3 | 40 | 322 |

Calculate the **percentage error** in each trial and the **mean percentage error**. Comment on the meter's accuracy as per IS 722 Class 1.0. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**Part (a) — Phantom Loading Description (7 marks):**

In the phantom loading arrangement:

1. The **pressure coil (PC)** of the energy meter is energized at **rated voltage (230 V, 50 Hz)** from the mains through a variac.
2. The **current coil (CC)** is energized at **rated current (5 A)** from a **separate low-voltage source** obtained through a step-down current transformer (typically 6–10 V secondary).
3. The phase angle between the PC voltage and CC current is controlled by a **phase-shifting transformer** (or an RC phase-shift network) inserted between the two sources.
4. The **standard wattmeter** measures the reference power $P_w = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ in the phantom circuit, where $\phi$ is the phase shift introduced.

**Power factor simulation:**
- **UPF (cos φ = 1):** Phase shifter set to 0° (voltage and current in phase).
- **0.8 PF lag:** Phase shifter set to $\cos^{-1}(0.8) = 36.87°$ lag.
- **0.5 PF lag:** Phase shifter set to $\cos^{-1}(0.5) = 60°$ lag.

> **Block diagram (textual):** Mains → Variac → PC terminals. Mains → Phase Shifting Transformer → Current Transformer → CC terminals. Standard Wattmeter measures $P_w$ between PC and CC reference. Energy meter disc revolutions are counted manually using a stopwatch.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — True power at UPF:**
$$P_t = 230 \times 5 \times 1 = 1150 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — True time for $N = 40$ revolutions** (using $K_h = 450$):
$$T_t = \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^6 \times 40}{450 \times 1150} = \dfrac{1.44 \times 10^8}{5.175 \times 10^5} \approx 278.26 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage error for each trial:**

Trial 1:
$$\%\text{Error}_1 = \dfrac{278.26 - 318}{318} \times 100 = \dfrac{-39.74}{318} \times 100 \approx -12.50\%$$

Trial 2:
$$\%\text{Error}_2 = \dfrac{278.26 - 320}{320} \times 100 = \dfrac{-41.74}{320} \times 100 \approx -13.04\%$$

Trial 3:
$$\%\text{Error}_3 = \dfrac{278.26 - 322}{322} \times 100 = \dfrac{-43.74}{322} \times 100 \approx -13.58\%$$

**Step 4 — Mean percentage error:**
$$\overline{\%\text{Error}} = \dfrac{(-12.50) + (-13.04) + (-13.58)}{3} = \dfrac{-39.12}{3} \approx -13.04\%$$

**Step 5 — IS 722 Class 1.0 Verdict:**
The IS 722 tolerance at UPF for a Class 1.0 meter is **±2%**. The mean error of **−13.04%** grossly exceeds this limit, and the meter **fails the calibration test**. The negative sign indicates that the meter is **running slow** (i.e., it under-registers the energy consumed). Corrective action required: **adjustment of the brake magnet position (to reduce braking torque)** and **re-testing**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice for Internal Optional, KTU 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **percentage error** of an induction-type energy meter in terms of the true time $T_t$ and the measured time $T_m$ for $N$ revolutions. Explain the physical significance of positive and negative error. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a phantom loading test on a 1-phase energy meter, the following readings were obtained at **0.5 PF lag** (rated 5 A, 230 V, $K_h = 600$ rev/kWh):

| Trial | $N$ | $T_m$ (s) | Standard Wattmeter $P_w$ (W) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 | 310 | 580 |
| 2 | 30 | 308 | 575 |
| 3 | 30 | 312 | 578 |

Compute the **percentage error** for each trial and the mean error. State whether the meter complies with IS 722 tolerance at 0.5 PF lag. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

The driving torque of the meter is proportional to the true power $P_t$:
$$T_d \propto P_t$$

The disc speed $N$ (rev/s) at steady state is proportional to $P_t$:
$$N = \dfrac{K_h \cdot P_t}{3600 \times 1000} \quad \text{[rev/s, with } P_t \text{ in W]}$$

The **true time** required to complete $N$ revolutions is:
$$T_t = \dfrac{N}{(\text{rev/s})} = \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^6 \cdot N}{K_h \cdot P_t}$$

The **measured time** $T_m$ may differ due to instrument errors. The fractional error is:
$$\delta = \dfrac{P_{\text{indicated}} - P_t}{P_t} = \dfrac{T_t - T_m}{T_m}$$

Multiplying by 100 gives the percentage error:
$$\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{T_t - T_m}{T_m} \times 100$$

**Physical significance:**
- **Positive error** ($T_t > T_m$): The disc completes $N$ revolutions in *less time* than ideal. The meter runs **fast** — over-registers energy — leading to **over-billing** of the consumer.
- **Negative error** ($T_t < T_m$): The disc takes *more time* than ideal. The meter runs **slow** — under-registers energy — leading to **revenue loss to the utility**.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

For each trial, compute $T_t$ first:
$$T_t = \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^6 \times 30}{600 \times P_w} = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^8}{600 \times P_w} = \dfrac{3.0 \times 10^5}{P_w}$$

**Trial 1:** $P_w = 580$ W → $T_t = \dfrac{3.0 \times 10^5}{580} \approx 517.24$ s
$$\%\text{Error}_1 = \dfrac{517.24 - 310}{310} \times 100 = \dfrac{207.24}{310} \times 100 \approx +66.85\%$$

**Trial 2:** $P_w = 575$ W → $T_t = \dfrac{3.0 \times 10^5}{575} \approx 521.74$ s
$$\%\text{Error}_2 = \dfrac{521.74 - 308}{308} \times 100 = \dfrac{213.74}{308} \times 100 \approx +69.40\%$$

**Trial 3:** $P_w = 578$ W → $T_t = \dfrac{3.0 \times 10^5}{578} \approx 519.03$ s
$$\%\text{Error}_3 = \dfrac{519.03 - 312}{312} \times 100 = \dfrac{207.03}{312} \times 100 \approx +66.36\%$$

**Mean error:**
$$\overline{\%\text{Error}} = \dfrac{66.85 + 69.40 + 66.36}{3} = \dfrac{202.61}{3} \approx +67.54\%$$

**IS 722 Verdict at 0.5 PF lag:** The tolerance is **±2.5%**. The mean error of **+67.54%** is **catastrophically high**, and the meter **fails the IS 722 Class 1.0 standard**. Positive sign means the meter is grossly **fast** — it would severely over-charge consumers and must be **withdrawn and re-calibrated** by adjusting the **lag coil (phase compensation)** and **brake magnet** before re-testing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the direction of error formula:** Several students write $\%\text{Error} = \frac{T_m - T_t}{T_t} \times 100$ (denominator $T_t$). The *correct* KTU convention is **denominator = $T_m$**. This single error flips the sign of your answer and is the **#1 reason for mark deduction** in calibration problems.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $K_h$ units:** $K_h$ is given in rev/kWh, but $P_w$ is in W and $t$ is in seconds. The factor $3.6 \times 10^6$ in the true-time formula is precisely to handle this unit conversion. Skipping it gives an error of ~2778×.
> 3. **Reporting only the magnitude of error:** IS 722 verdicts require the **sign** (fast or slow) — always state whether the meter runs fast (+) or slow (−).
> 4. **Not tabulating the trial-wise readings:** KTU valuation gives **1 mark** for a properly tabulated observation table. Skipping it costs an easy mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Meter constant $K_h$** (rev/kWh) is the *nameplate parameter*; never assume it — always read it from the meter.
- [ ] **Phantom loading** uses two *independent sources* — one for the pressure coil, one for the current coil — with a **phase-shifting transformer** for PF control.
- [ ] **True power in phantom loading** = $V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ where $\cos\phi$ is the *simulated* PF, but the **actual kW drawn from the mains** is only the small no-load loss of the meter.
- [ ] The **driving torque** is proportional to $VI\cos\phi$; the **braking torque** is proportional to disc speed $N$.
- [ ] The **true time formula** (in seconds) is $T_t = \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^6 \cdot N}{K_h \cdot P_w}$ — memorize this *exact* expression for KTU exams.
- [ ] The **percentage error** is $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{T_t - T_m}{T_m} \times 100$ — denominator is always $T_m$.
- [ ] **Positive error** → meter is **fast** → over-bills the consumer.
- [ ] **Negative error** → meter is **slow** → revenue loss to the utility.
- [ ] **IS 722 Class 1.0 limits:** $\pm 2\%$ at UPF, $\pm 2.5\%$ at 0.5 PF lag.
- [ ] The error **increases at lower PF** due to non-linear friction and PC phase errors becoming significant relative to the smaller driving torque.
- [ ] **Light running test** (PC energized, CC open) must be performed *before* the calibration — the disc should rotate slowly without creeping indefinitely.
- [ ] **Brake magnet** is the primary adjustment knob for fine-tuning the meter's accuracy.
- [ ] **Phase (lag) compensation coil** corrects PF-dependent errors.
- [ ] Always record at least **3 trials** per test point and report the **mean** error.
- [ ] The **standard wattmeter** in the phantom loading circuit reads the *reference true power* — never use the meter under test as the reference.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of 3-phase Energy meter using standard wattmeter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL307 - CIRCUITS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of 3-phase Energy meter using standard wattmeter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module Experiment 2: Calibration of 3-Phase Energy Meter Using Standard Wattmeter

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration of a 3-Phase Energy Meter** is the experimental procedure of comparing the energy recorded by the test energy meter (device under test) against a highly accurate **standard reference wattmeter (sub-standard/precision wattmeter)** over a defined period, under controlled balanced/unbalanced 3-phase load conditions, to determine the **meter error**, **percentage error**, and **meter constant correction factor** as per **IS 13010** and **IEC 62053** standards.

The energy meter is a **kWh meter** that integrates active power over time. Since an energy meter is essentially a watt-hour integrator, the most accurate way to calibrate it is to feed a known, stable power (measured by a standard wattmeter) for a measured time, and then compare the energy registered by the test meter with the energy computed from the wattmeter reading.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "Calibrating a Car's Fuel Gauge":**
> Imagine you fill your car with exactly **10 litres** of fuel, drive until the gauge reads "empty," and find the tank actually used **10.8 litres**. Your gauge is reading **8% low**. Calibration is the process of finding this **deviation** so that the manufacturer can apply a correction factor. In the same way, a 3-phase energy meter may read 100 kWh when the true energy consumed is 102 kWh — we find this **2% error** using a *trusted reference instrument* (the standard wattmeter).

### 1.3 Key Technical Vocabulary (Bold Constants/Standards)

- **True Energy (E_true)** = (W_std × t) / 3600, where W_std is the standard wattmeter reading in watts and *t* is time in seconds; result in **kWh**.
- **Energy recorded by test meter (E_meter)** = Number of disc revolutions × Meter constant (Rev/kWh).
- **Percentage Error** = [(E_meter − E_true) / E_true] × 100%
- **Meter Constant (K)** = Revolutions per kWh stated on the meter nameplate (typically **500 to 1200 rev/kWh** for induction-type meters).
- **Standard Wattmeter Accuracy Class**: typically **0.2** or **0.5** as per IS 1248.
- **Supply**: 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz balanced sinusoidal AC (KTU lab standard).
- **Reference Standard**: IS 13010 / IEC 62053-21 for AC static/induction energy meters.

### 1.4 Why Calibration? — The Underlying Physical Need

> [!NOTE]
> Energy meters in service accumulate errors due to:
> 1. **Aging of permanent magnets** in the braking system (alnico magnets lose ~1–2%/decade).
> 2. **Temperature drift** of copper coils (resistance changes by ~0.4%/°C).
> 3. **Friction compensation spring** fatigue.
> 4. **Waveform distortion** (harmonics affect induction disc torque non-linearly).
> 5. **Mechanical wear** of the register gear train.
>
> Hence periodic calibration is mandated under the **Indian Electricity Act 2003** for billing meters, every **5 years** for HT and every **8 years** for LT consumers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of the 3-Phase Induction Energy Meter

The 3-phase energy meter contains **two identical induction disc elements (driving units)** mounted on a common shaft, mechanically coupled to a single aluminium disc and a cyclometer register. Each element has:

1. **Voltage (pressure) coil** — highly inductive, in parallel with the supply (creates flux ∝ V).
2. **Current coil** — low resistance, in series with the load (creates flux ∝ I).
3. **Aluminium disc** — eddy currents induced produce a driving torque **T_d**.
4. **Permanent magnet (brake magnet)** — produces a **braking torque T_b ∝ ω** (speed).

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

- **Step 1:** The two driving elements are connected such that their torques **add algebraically** (one in each of the two phases of a 3-phase, 3-wire system, or a 3-phase, 4-wire system).
- **Step 2:** The driving torque is proportional to total 3-phase active power:
$$T_d = k_1 \cdot (P_1 + P_2 + P_3) = k_1 \cdot P_{total}$$
- **Step 3:** The permanent magnet brake torque is proportional to disc speed (ω):
$$T_b = k_2 \cdot \omega$$
- **Step 4:** At steady state, T_d = T_b, so disc speed is directly proportional to total power.
- **Step 5:** Number of revolutions in time *t* is proportional to energy:
$$N = \int_0^t \omega \, d\tau = \frac{k_1}{k_2} \int_0^t P_{total} \, d\tau = K \cdot E_{total}$$
where **K = meter constant (rev/kWh)**.

## 2.3 The Calibration Philosophy — Why Use a Wattmeter as Reference?

A **wattmeter** is a high-accuracy analog/digital instrument (accuracy class 0.2 or better) that **measures instantaneous active power** without integrating. By measuring a stable 3-phase load power (P_std) for a precise time interval (t seconds), the **true energy** is:

$$E_{true} = P_{std} \times t \quad \text{(in Watt-seconds)}$$

The energy meter's disc revolutions are counted over the same time interval. The energy meter's "claimed" energy is:

$$E_{meter} = \frac{N}{K} \quad \text{(kWh)}$$

The **percentage error** quantifies the deviation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a wattmeter and not a sub-standard energy meter?** A precision wattmeter measures power *instantaneously* without the integration drift of an energy meter. Combined with a precise stopwatch and a known stable load, the wattmeter-based calibration method gives **uncertainty better than ±0.5%**, which is the standard KTU lab acceptable tolerance for a Class 1 meter.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | True 3-Phase Power (Balanced) | $P_{std} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | W (watts) | V_L = line voltage, I_L = line current |
| 2 | True Energy from Wattmeter | $E_{true} = \dfrac{P_{std} \times t}{3600 \times 1000}$ | kWh | t in seconds; divide by 3600 to convert to hours, then by 1000 for kW |
| 3 | Energy from Test Meter | $E_{meter} = \dfrac{N}{K}$ | kWh | N = revolutions, K = meter constant (rev/kWh) |
| 4 | Absolute Error | $\Delta E = E_{meter} - E_{true}$ | kWh | Positive = meter over-registers (customer loses); negative = under-registers (utility loses) |
| 5 | Percentage Error | $\% \text{Error} = \dfrac{E_{meter} - E_{true}}{E_{true}} \times 100$ | % | Sign convention: **+ve → fast meter; −ve → slow meter** |
| 6 | Correction Factor | $CF = \dfrac{E_{true}}{E_{meter}}$ | dimensionless | Applied to meter reading for accurate billing |
| 7 | Disc Speed (steady) | $n = K \cdot P_{total}$ | rev/kWh × W | steady-state speed in rev/unit energy |
| 8 | Driving Torque | $T_d \propto V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | N·m | per element; summed for both elements in 3-phase meter |
| 9 | Braking Torque | $T_b = k_b \cdot \omega$ | N·m | proportional to angular velocity |
| 10 | Rated Meter Constant | $K = \dfrac{N_{rated}}{P_{rated} \times 1 \text{ hr}}$ | rev/kWh | read from nameplate |
| 11 | Acceptable Error Limit (Class 1) | $\pm 2.0\%$ at unity pf, $\pm 2.5\%$ at 0.5 pf lag | % | per IS 13010 for Class 1 induction meters |
| 12 | Acceptable Error Limit (Class 2) | $\pm 2.5\%$ at unity pf, $\pm 3.5\%$ at 0.5 pf lag | % | per IS 13010 for Class 2 induction meters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** The KTU lab viva almost always asks either (a) the formula for percentage error, (b) why we use a standard wattmeter instead of a sub-standard energy meter, or (c) what happens when percentage error exceeds the IS limit. Memorize row 5 above and the **±2% Class 1 limit** verbatim.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Calibration of 3-phase energy meters is **critical infrastructure** in:

1. **Electricity billing** — every commercial/industrial HT consumer's bill is based on a calibrated meter (loss to utility of 1% error × 1000 MUs = ₹5 crore/year for a state discom).
2. **Power quality auditing** — to detect whether a meter is recording energy correctly under non-linear (harmonic-rich) loads.
3. **Renewable energy settlement** — solar/wind net-metering requires Class 0.5S accuracy.
4. **ISO 9001 / NABL-accredited labs** — Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) Star-rated equipment must be traceably calibrated.
5. **Smart grid deployment** — to baseline compare legacy electromechanical meters against new smart meters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Driving Torque of a 3-Phase Energy Meter Element

### 3.1.1 Flux Equations

Let the voltage coil flux (lagging supply voltage V by ~90° for high-X design) be:
$$\phi_v = \phi_{vm} \sin(\omega t - 90°) = -\phi_{vm} \cos(\omega t)$$

Let the current coil flux (in phase with load current I, with phase angle φ relative to V) be:
$$\phi_i = \phi_{im} \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

### 3.1.2 Instantaneous Torque on the Disc

The instantaneous torque is proportional to the product of the two interacting fluxes (similar to a single-phase induction-type wattmeter derivation):
$$T(t) = k \cdot \phi_v(t) \cdot \phi_i(t)$$

Substituting:
$$T(t) = k \cdot [-\phi_{vm} \cos(\omega t)] \cdot [\phi_{im} \sin(\omega t - \phi)]$$

$$T(t) = -k \cdot \phi_{vm} \phi_{im} \cos(\omega t) \cdot \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

### 3.1.3 Average Torque Over a Cycle

Using the identity:
$$\cos A \cdot \sin B = \frac{1}{2}[\sin(A+B) - \sin(A-B)]$$

$$T(t) = -\frac{k \cdot \phi_{vm} \phi_{im}}{2} [\sin(2\omega t - \phi) - \sin(-\phi)]$$

The first term **sin(2ωt − φ)** averages to zero over one complete cycle. The second term is a constant:

$$T_{avg} = -\frac{k \cdot \phi_{vm} \phi_{im}}{2} \cdot [0 - \sin(-\phi)]$$

$$\boxed{T_{avg} = \frac{k \cdot \phi_{vm} \phi_{im}}{2} \sin\phi}$$

Since flux ∝ V and flux ∝ I, and the design ensures proportionality:
$$\phi_{vm} = c_1 \cdot V \quad \text{and} \quad \phi_{im} = c_2 \cdot I$$

Therefore:
$$T_{avg} = \frac{k \cdot c_1 c_2 \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \sin\phi}{2} = K_1 \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \sin\phi$$

For a properly designed watt-hour meter, the voltage coil flux is made to lag by **90° − α** (compensated), making the instantaneous torque proportional to **cos(φ − α) ≈ cos φ** for small α, giving the correct active-power response:

$$\boxed{T_{avg} = K_1 \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = K_1 \cdot P_{single-phase}}$$

### 3.1.4 Extension to 3-Phase (Two-Element Meter)

For a 3-phase, 3-wire system, the meter has two elements:

$$T_{total} = T_1 + T_2 = K_1 \cdot (P_1 + P_2) = K_1 \cdot P_{3-\phi, total}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** This derivation is the *theoretical* reason the induction-type energy meter correctly measures active energy: the average torque equals the active power. The brake torque then converts this power into a steady rotation whose total count equals energy. Calibration is needed because the proportionality constants (K_1, k_2) drift with age.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Step-by-Step Calibration Procedure & Calculation

Let us walk through a **complete numerical KTU lab problem**.

### 3.2.1 Given Data (typical KTU exam problem)

- Standard wattmeter reading: **P_std = 1500 W** (constant, steady-state)
- Time for N revolutions: **t = 60 seconds**
- Number of revolutions counted: **N = 25 rev**
- Meter constant (nameplate): **K = 500 rev/kWh**
- Test load: balanced 3-phase resistive (unity pf)

### 3.2.2 Step 1: Compute True Energy from the Standard Wattmeter

$$E_{true} = \frac{P_{std} \times t}{3600 \times 1000} \quad \text{(kWh)}$$

$$E_{true} = \frac{1500 \times 60}{3600 \times 1000} = \frac{90000}{3600000} = 0.025 \text{ kWh}$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2: Compute Energy Registered by the Test Meter

$$E_{meter} = \frac{N}{K} = \frac{25}{500} = 0.05 \text{ kWh}$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3: Compute Absolute Error

$$\Delta E = E_{meter} - E_{true} = 0.05 - 0.025 = +0.025 \text{ kWh}$$

### 3.2.5 Step 4: Compute Percentage Error

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{E_{meter} - E_{true}}{E_{true}} \times 100 = \frac{0.025}{0.025} \times 100 = +100\%$$

This is a **grossly fast meter** — outside any acceptable IS limit. In a real KTU lab, errors are typically within ±5%.

### 3.2.6 More Realistic Example

Suppose instead: P_std = 1500 W, t = 100 s, N = 21 rev, K = 500 rev/kWh.

$$E_{true} = \frac{1500 \times 100}{3600 \times 1000} = 0.04167 \text{ kWh}$$

$$E_{meter} = \frac{21}{500} = 0.042 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{0.042 - 0.04167}{0.04167} \times 100 = +0.79\%$$

This is a **fast meter by 0.79%** — **acceptable for Class 1 (±2%)** but **rejected for Class 0.5 (±0.5%)**. The KTU lab typically reports whether the meter passes the relevant IS class.

### 3.2.7 Final Decision Table (KTU Report Format)

| S.No. | Load (% of rated) | Time (s) | Revs (N) | E_true (kWh) | E_meter (kWh) | % Error | Class 1 Limit (±2%) | Class 2 Limit (±2.5%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 25% | 120 | 5 | … | … | … | Pass/Fail | Pass/Fail |
| 2 | 50% | 120 | 10 | … | … | … | Pass/Fail | Pass/Fail |
| 3 | 75% | 120 | 15 | … | … | … | Pass/Fail | Pass/Fail |
| 4 | 100% | 120 | 20 | … | … | … | Pass/Fail | Pass/Fail |
| 5 | 125% | 120 | 25 | … | … | … | Pass/Fail | Pass/Fail |

### 3.2.8 Why the Disc Spins at All — A Physical Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The disc is the only moving part. The **drag magnet** is what makes the disc speed proportional to power (and not, say, voltage squared). Without it, the disc would accelerate to destruction. With the drag magnet, **steady-state speed = driving torque / brake coefficient = K·P/k_b**. So number of revolutions in time t = (K/k_b)·P·t = (K/k_b)·Energy. Thus the **meter constant K = k_b/k_1** is purely a mechanical constant set by the permanent magnet strength and geometry. This is exactly the parameter that drifts with magnet aging — the fundamental reason calibration is needed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Calibration Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWSUP[Three Phase 415V 50Hz AC Supply]
        A1[R Phase]
        A2[Y Phase]
        A3[B Phase]
        A0[Neutral Optional]
    end

    subgraph STDSET[Standard Reference Instruments]
        B1[Standard Wattmeter 0.2 Class]
        B2[Stopwatch Synchronized to Disc Marker]
        B3[Voltmeter V_L Monitoring]
    end

    subgraph TESTMTR[Device Under Test 3Phase Energy Meter]
        C1[Current Coils in R and B Phases]
        C2[Voltage Coils Across RY and BY]
        C3[Aluminium Disc with Marker]
        C4[Permanent Brake Magnet]
        C5[Cyclometer Register kWh]
    end

    subgraph LOAD[Balanced Three Phase Resistive Load Bank]
        D1[Variable Resistive Load R 3 identical]
    end

    A1 --> C1
    A2 --> C2
    A3 --> C1
    A2 --> B1
    A1 --> B3
    A3 --> B3
    B1 -.measures.-> LOAD
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> A0
    C3 -->|optical count via photodiode| B2
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> C5
    C5 -->|displayed kWh| OBS[Observer / Student Record N revolutions and t seconds]
    B1 -->|W_std watts| OBS
    B2 -->|t seconds| OBS
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of Calibration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1 Connect circuit without supply] --> P2[Step 2 Apply 3 phase 415V 50Hz]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Set load to 25 percent of rated]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Energize test meter for 5 min warmup]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Note standard wattmeter W_std in watts]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Start stopwatch when disc marker crosses index]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Count N revolutions typically 5 to 50]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 Stop stopwatch at exact Nth revolution]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9 Record t in seconds and N]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10 Compute E_true W_std times t divided by 3600000 kWh]
    P10 --> P11[Step 11 Compute E_meter N divided by K kWh]
    P11 --> P12[Step 12 Compute percent error E_meter minus E_true divided by E_true times 100]
    P12 --> P13[Step 13 Compare with IS 13010 limits]
    P13 --> P14{Within Class 1 plus minus 2 percent}
    P14 -->|Yes| P15[Meter PASSES calibrate and seal]
    P14 -->|No| P16[Meter FAILS adjust brake magnet or repair]
    P15 --> P17[Repeat for 50 75 100 125 percent loads]
    P16 --> P17
    P17 --> P18[Plot percent error vs load current KTU viva graph]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Signal Flow — Energy Conversion Inside the Meter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VIN[3 Phase Supply V and I] --> FLX1[Voltage Coil Flux phi_v proportional V]
    VIN --> FLX2[Current Coil Flux phi_i proportional I]
    FLX1 --> TRQ[Driving Torque T_d proportional V times I times cos phi]
    FLX2 --> TRQ
    TRQ --> DSC[Aluminium Disc rotates at speed omega]
    DSC --> BRK[Brake Magnet Torque T_b proportional omega opposes motion]
    BRK --> EQ[Steady State T_d equals T_b so omega proportional P]
    EQ --> N[Revolutions N in time t proportional Energy]
    N --> REG[Register displays kWh]
    REG --> ETRUE[Compare with Standard Wattmeter E_true]
    ETRUE --> PERC[Compute Percentage Error]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Viva:** When asked "draw the block diagram," the examiner wants to see: (1) supply → test meter → load loop, (2) wattmeter in the *same* loop (so it measures the *same* power), (3) disc/rotation indicator going to observer, (4) computation block producing % error. Avoid the common error of putting the wattmeter on the *primary* side and the test meter on the *load* side — they must be in series on the *same* current path.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**Define calibration of an energy meter. Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Calibration of an energy meter is the **process of comparing its reading against a standard reference instrument** (a precision wattmeter of accuracy class 0.2/0.5) under controlled load conditions, to determine its **percentage error** and confirm whether it complies with **IS 13010 / IEC 62053** accuracy class limits.

It is necessary because:
- (1) Energy meters accumulate errors over years of service due to **aging of brake magnets** (alnico loses strength), **temperature drift** of coil resistances, **friction compensation spring** fatigue, and **waveform distortion** sensitivity.
- (2) A 1% error on a 1 MU/month industrial consumer = **₹50,000/month** revenue loss to the discom or overcharge to the consumer.
- (3) The **Indian Electricity Act 2003** mandates periodic calibration of billing meters (HT every 5 years, LT every 8 years).
- (4) Calibration provides a **traceable correction factor** to NABL-accredited standards.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write only "to check accuracy" — that is **1 mark**. You must elaborate on the *physical reasons* (aging, temperature, IS standard, legal mandate) to earn all 3 marks.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, May 2025]**
**What is percentage error in an energy meter? Write the formula and state the IS 13010 limit for a Class 1 meter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Percentage error of an energy meter is the **deviation of the energy registered by the test meter from the true energy** (as measured by the standard reference instrument), expressed as a percentage of the true energy:

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{E_{meter} - E_{true}}{E_{true}} \times 100$$

where:
- $E_{meter} = \dfrac{N}{K}$ (N = revolutions, K = meter constant)
- $E_{true} = \dfrac{P_{std} \times t}{3600 \times 1000}$ kWh

**Sign convention:**
- **Positive % error** → fast meter (over-registers → consumer is overcharged).
- **Negative % error** → slow meter (under-registers → utility loses revenue).

**IS 13010 limits for Class 1 meter:**
- At **unity power factor**: ±2.0%
- At **0.5 power factor lag**: ±2.5%

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing "error = E_true − E_meter" reverses the sign. The KTU standard is **E_meter − E_true in the numerator**, and a positive result means the meter is *fast* (running ahead).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, describe the experimental procedure to **calibrate a given 3-phase energy meter using a standard wattmeter** at different load conditions. List the apparatus required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a calibration test on a 3-phase energy meter, the following observations were recorded at unity power factor. The standard wattmeter reads **P = 1200 W** consistently. The meter constant is **K = 600 rev/kWh**. **(7 marks)**

| Load Current (A) | Time for 30 rev (s) |
|---|---|
| 5 | 250 |
| 10 | 125 |
| 15 | 84 |
| 20 | 63 |

Calculate the **percentage error at each load** and state whether the meter satisfies the **IS 13010 Class 1 (±2%)** requirement at unity power factor.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Procedure and Circuit (7 marks)

**Apparatus Required (2 marks):**
1. 3-phase energy meter (test meter, Class 1 or 2)
2. Standard precision wattmeter (0.2 class, 3-phase or equivalent single-phase × 2)
3. 3-phase variac (auto-transformer) — 0–415 V
4. Balanced 3-phase resistive load bank (rheostat type) — 3 identical units star/delta connected
5. Voltmeter (0–500 V AC, MI type)
6. Ammeter (0–20 A AC, MI type) — 3 Nos. (or one with selector switch)
7. Stopwatch (resolution 0.1 s)
8. Connecting wires, fuses, MCB
9. Photodiode revolution counter (optional, manual counting also accepted)

**Circuit Diagram Description (2 marks):**

The 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz supply is given through a 3-phase variac to the load bank. The **standard wattmeter(s)** are connected so as to measure total 3-phase power (in 3-phase 3-wire system, use the **two-wattmeter method** with two standard wattmeters; in 4-wire, use a 3-wattmeter arrangement or a single 3-element wattmeter). The **test energy meter** is connected in series on the line side (current coils) and in parallel on the line side (voltage coils) — i.e., the **same** current and voltage that the wattmeter measures. The load bank is variable to set different load currents (typically 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%, 125% of rated current).

**Experimental Procedure (3 marks):**

1. **Step 1 — Wiring:** Connect the circuit as per the diagram. Verify all connections, ensure fuses are intact, and the variac is at zero output.
2. **Step 2 — Initial Checks:** Set the load bank to minimum resistance (highest current rating, but disconnected). Set the variac to zero.
3. **Step 3 — Energize:** Switch on the 3-phase MCB. Slowly increase variac to rated voltage (415 V line-to-line). Verify voltmeter reading.
4. **Step 4 — Apply Load:** Adjust the load bank to the desired test current (e.g., 25% of rated = 5 A if rated is 20 A).
5. **Step 5 — Warmup:** Let the meter run for 5 minutes at load for thermal stabilization.
6. **Step 6 — Reading the Wattmeter:** Note the **standard wattmeter reading P_std in watts** (use the average of two wattmeters for 2-wattmeter method, since for unity pf both wattmeters read equal and P_total = W1 + W2).
7. **Step 7 — Counting Revolutions:** As the disc marker passes the index mark, **start the stopwatch simultaneously**. Count N revolutions (typically 20–50, as per lab convention). When the Nth marker passes, **stop the stopwatch**. Record t in seconds.
8. **Step 8 — Repeat:** Repeat steps 5–7 for at least 4–5 different load currents.
9. **Step 9 — Compute:** For each load, compute E_true, E_meter, and % error. Tabulate and plot % error vs load current.

**Precautions:**
- Keep load current steady throughout counting.
- The wattmeter should be read by the *same observer* who counts revolutions, to avoid parallax.
- Use the *same* N for all loads to minimize statistical error.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** K = 600 rev/kWh, P_std = 1200 W, N = 30 rev for each load.

**Formulae:**
$$E_{true} = \frac{P_{std} \times t}{3600 \times 1000} \text{ kWh}$$

$$E_{meter} = \frac{N}{K} = \frac{30}{600} = 0.05 \text{ kWh (constant for all loads)}$$

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{E_{meter} - E_{true}}{E_{true}} \times 100$$

**Load 1: I = 5 A, t = 250 s**

$$E_{true,1} = \frac{1200 \times 250}{3{,}600{,}000} = \frac{300{,}000}{3{,}600{,}000} = 0.08333 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\% \text{Error}_1 = \frac{0.05 - 0.08333}{0.08333} \times 100 = -40.0\%$$

**Load 2: I = 10 A, t = 125 s**

$$E_{true,2} = \frac{1200 \times 125}{3{,}600{,}000} = \frac{150{,}000}{3{,}600{,}000} = 0.04167 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\% \text{Error}_2 = \frac{0.05 - 0.04167}{0.04167} \times 100 = +20.0\%$$

**Load 3: I = 15 A, t = 84 s**

$$E_{true,3} = \frac{1200 \times 84}{3{,}600{,}000} = \frac{100{,}800}{3{,}600{,}000} = 0.02800 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\% \text{Error}_3 = \frac{0.05 - 0.028}{0.028} \times 100 = +78.57\%$$

**Load 4: I = 20 A, t = 63 s**

$$E_{true,4} = \frac{1200 \times 63}{3{,}600{,}000} = \frac{75{,}600}{3{,}600{,}000} = 0.02100 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\% \text{Error}_4 = \frac{0.05 - 0.021}{0.021} \times 100 = +138.10\%$$

**Conclusion:** The meter has **grossly non-linear error** — it is a **slow meter at light loads (5 A)** and a **fast meter at higher loads**. The errors range from **−40% to +138%**, **far exceeding the IS 13010 Class 1 limit of ±2%** at all loads. The meter is **NOT acceptable for Class 1 billing**. The cause is likely **wrong brake magnet strength** or **damaged friction compensation spring** — the meter must be re-conditioned or discarded.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning:** Do not forget to mention **all four load values** in tabular form. Marks are split: 1 mark per correct % error, 1 mark for conclusion, 1 mark for identifying "fast/slow" nature, 1 mark for IS limit reference. A common error is dividing by 1000 only once (or forgetting the /3600), leading to a factor-of-3600 mistake.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2025]**

**(a)** Explain with necessary theory the **principle of operation of a 3-phase induction-type energy meter**. Derive the expression for the **average driving torque** in terms of voltage, current, and power factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 3-wire energy meter has a meter constant of **400 rev/kWh**. It is calibrated at unity power factor with a standard wattmeter reading **P = 2000 W**. The disc makes **40 revolutions in 100 seconds**. Calculate: **(i)** the true energy, (ii) the energy recorded by the meter, (iii) the percentage error, and (iv) the correction factor. State whether the meter is **fast or slow** and whether it passes the **IS 13010 Class 1 (±2%)** specification. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Theory and Derivation (7 marks)

**Construction (2 marks):**

A 3-phase induction-type energy meter consists of:
- **Two driving elements (electromagnets)** — each with a voltage coil (highly inductive, connected across the line) and a current coil (low resistance, connected in series with the line).
- **A single aluminium disc** mounted on a common spindle, with both elements acting on it.
- **A permanent brake magnet** providing a retarding torque proportional to disc speed.
- **A cyclometer register** geared to the spindle displaying kWh.
- **A friction compensation spring/loop** to offset bearing friction at low loads.
- **A phase-shifting (lag) coil** for power-factor error compensation.

**Operation (2 marks):**

The voltage coils draw currents proportional to supply voltages and produce alternating fluxes that lag the voltages by ~90°. The current coils carry the load currents and produce fluxes in phase with them. Interaction of these fluxes induces eddy currents in the disc, producing a **driving torque T_d** in each element. The two torques are summed on the common disc. The **brake magnet torque T_b** opposes motion and is proportional to disc speed ω. At steady state, T_d = T_b, and the disc speed is proportional to total 3-phase power. The cyclometer integrates revolutions into kWh.

**Derivation of Average Torque (3 marks):**

(Detailed derivation already shown in Section 3.1 — reproduce the key boxed result here)

$$\boxed{T_{avg} = K_1 \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = K_1 \cdot P_{single-phase}}$$

For the 3-phase meter with two elements:
$$T_{total} = T_1 + T_2 = K_1 (P_1 + P_2) = K_1 \cdot P_{3-\phi, total}$$

The number of revolutions in time t:
$$N = \int_0^t \frac{T_{avg}}{k_b} d\tau = \frac{K_1}{k_b} \int_0^t P \, d\tau = K \cdot E_{total}$$

where $K = K_1/k_b$ is the **meter constant in rev/kWh**.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** K = 400 rev/kWh, P_std = 2000 W, N = 40 rev, t = 100 s.

**(i) True Energy:**

$$E_{true} = \frac{P_{std} \times t}{3600 \times 1000} = \frac{2000 \times 100}{3{,}600{,}000} = \frac{200{,}000}{3{,}600{,}000} = 0.05556 \text{ kWh}$$

**(ii) Energy Recorded by Test Meter:**

$$E_{meter} = \frac{N}{K} = \frac{40}{400} = 0.100 \text{ kWh}$$

**(iii) Percentage Error:**

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{E_{meter} - E_{true}}{E_{true}} \times 100 = \frac{0.100 - 0.05556}{0.05556} \times 100 = \frac{0.04444}{0.05556} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{Error} = +80.0\%$$

**(iv) Correction Factor:**

$$CF = \frac{E_{true}}{E_{meter}} = \frac{0.05556}{0.100} = 0.5556$$

**Interpretation:**
- The meter is a **fast meter** (positive % error = it over-registers).
- It records **80% more energy** than actually consumed.
- The correction factor of 0.5556 means the meter's reading must be multiplied by 0.5556 to obtain the true energy (i.e., the brake magnet is too weak, or the meter constant is too high).
- This meter **fails the IS 13010 Class 1 (±2%) specification by a huge margin** and must be re-calibrated, the brake magnet repositioned, or the meter discarded.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning:** The most common arithmetic mistake in part (b) is forgetting to divide by **3,600,000** (combining /3600 and /1000) — students often write E_true = 0.2 kWh or 200 kWh, both wrong by factors of 11 or 3600. The **final answer must be in kWh**, and the conversion factor is non-negotiable.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Must-Memorize Points**

- [x] **Definition:** Calibration = comparison with a standard reference to determine % error.
- [x] **Apparatus:** Standard wattmeter (0.2 class), 3-phase variac, balanced R-load bank, stopwatch, V/I meters, test energy meter.
- [x] **Core formula 1:** $E_{true} = \dfrac{P_{std} \times t}{3600 \times 1000}$ **kWh** (P in W, t in s).
- [x] **Core formula 2:** $E_{meter} = \dfrac{N}{K}$ **kWh** (N = revolutions, K = rev/kWh).
- [x] **Core formula 3:** $\% \text{Error} = \dfrac{E_{meter} - E_{true}}{E_{true}} \times 100$.
- [x] **Sign convention:** +ve = fast (over-register), −ve = slow (under-register).
- [x] **Correction factor:** $CF = E_{true}/E_{meter}$.
- [x] **IS 13010 Class 1 limit:** ±2% at unity pf, ±2.5% at 0.5 pf lag.
- [x] **IS 13010 Class 2 limit:** ±2.5% at unity pf, ±3.5% at 0.5 pf lag.
- [x] **Driving torque equation:** $T_d \propto V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$.
- [x] **Brake torque equation:** $T_b \propto \omega$ (proportional to disc speed).
- [x] **Steady-state speed:** $\omega \propto P_{total}$ (this is why revolutions ∝ energy).
- [x] **Two-element meter:** 3-φ, 3-wire uses two driving elements on a common disc; 3-φ, 4-wire uses three.
- [x] **Why a wattmeter and not a sub-standard energy meter?** Wattmeter has better accuracy and no integration drift.
- [x] **Why we vary the load?** To check linearity of meter error vs load (a Class 1 meter must be within ±2% at 5%, 10%, 50%, 100%, 125% of rated current).
- [x] **Sources of error in the meter itself:** aging brake magnet, temperature, friction spring, waveform distortion.
- [x] **Statutory mandate:** Indian Electricity Act 2003 — HT meter calibration every 5 years, LT every 8 years.
- [x] **KTU viva one-liner:** "The meter constant is the rev/kWh rating printed on the nameplate; it relates the number of disc revolutions to true energy consumed."
- [x] **Block-diagram order (KTU favorite):** Supply → Test Meter → Load (loop). Wattmeter is in the **same** loop.
