## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
Define an AC bridge. State the general balance condition and explain the significance of the null detector.

**Model Answer:**
An AC bridge is a null-type measuring instrument consisting of four impedance arms arranged in a closed loop, energized by an AC source and balanced using a null detector. **General balance condition:** $Z_1 Z_3 = Z_2 Z_4$. The null detector is a highly sensitive indicating device (headphone/oscilloscope) that detects zero current/voltage at the balance point. Its significance is that it provides a **null comparison** between known and unknown quantities, eliminating the need for the detector to be calibrated — only its sensitivity matters. This is why AC bridges are among the most accurate measurement methods.

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set A]**
Differentiate between Maxwell's bridge and Hay's bridge based on the configuration, frequency dependence, and Q-factor suitability.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Maxwell's Bridge | Hay's Bridge |
|:----------|:-----------------|:-------------|
| Standard arm | $R_3 \parallel C_3$ | $R_3$ in series with $C_3$ |
| Frequency dependence | Independent of $\omega$ | Strongly dependent on $\omega$ |
| Best for Q-factor | $Q$ between 1 and 10 | $Q$ greater than 10 |
| Balance equation for $L_x$ | $L_x = R_1 R_3 C_3$ | $L_x = \dfrac{R_1 R_3 C_3}{1 + \omega^2 R_3^2 C_3^2}$ |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** Derive the balance equations for **Maxwell's inductance bridge** and show that the expressions for $L_x$ and $R_x$ are independent of the source frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a Maxwell's bridge operating at 1 kHz, the following values are obtained at balance: $R_1 = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 22 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $C_3 = 0.05 \mu F$. Calculate the unknown $L_x$, $R_x$, and the Q-factor of the coil. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the complete step-by-step derivation. The key points the examiner expects:

1. State the arm layout clearly (Arm 1: $R_1$, Arm 2: $R_2$, Arm 3: $R_3 \parallel C_3$, Arm 4: $R_x + j\omega L_x$).
2. Compute $Z_3 = \dfrac{R_3}{1 + j\omega R_3 C_3}$.
3. Apply $Z_1 Z_3 = Z_2 Z_4$ and cross-multiply.
4. Expand using $(a+jb)(c+jd) = (ac-bd) + j(ad+bc)$.
5. Equate real and imaginary parts.

**Final balance equations:**

$$L_x = R_1 R_3 C_3 \qquad R_x = \frac{R_1 R_3}{R_2}$$

Since neither expression contains $\omega$, the balance is **frequency-independent**. This is the chief merit of Maxwell's bridge.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $R_1 = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 22 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_3 = 0.05 \mu F$, $f = 1 \text{ kHz}$.

**Step 1: Compute $L_x$**

$$L_x = R_1 R_3 C_3 = (4.7 \times 10^3)(22 \times 10^3)(0.05 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$L_x = 4.7 \times 22 \times 0.05 \times 10^{3+3-6} = 5.17 \times 10^0 = 5.17 \text{ H}$$

**Step 2: Compute $R_x$**

$$R_x = \frac{R_1 R_3}{R_2} = \frac{(4.7 \times 10^3)(22 \times 10^3)}{10 \times 10^3} = \frac{103400}{10} = 10340 \Omega = 10.34 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3: Compute $\omega$**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 1000 = 6283.19 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Compute Q-factor**

$$Q = \frac{\omega L_x}{R_x} = \frac{6283.19 \times 5.17}{10340} = \frac{32483.99}{10340} = 3.14$$

**Result:** $L_x = 5.17 \text{ H}$, $R_x = 10.34 \text{ k}\Omega$, $Q = 3.14$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students confuse $R_3 \parallel C_3$ with $R_3$ in series with $C_3$. **Remember:** Maxwell's uses the **parallel** combination; Hay's uses the **series** combination. Mixing these gives a 7-mark deduction immediately.
> - Always state the **frequency independence** explicitly as a separate point — examiners specifically test this conceptual understanding.
> - For numerical problems, **write the unit conversions explicitly** in scientific notation. Skipping this loses 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set C]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat phasor representation, derive the balance equations for the **Schering bridge** used to measure an unknown capacitance with loss. Define the dissipation factor and express it in terms of bridge components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a Schering bridge, the following values are obtained at balance: standard capacitor $C_2 = 100 \text{ pF}$, $R_3 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_4 = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, and the variable capacitor $C_4 = 250 \text{ pF}$. The source frequency is 1 kHz. Calculate the unknown $C_x$, its equivalent series resistance $r_x$, and the dissipation factor $\tan \delta$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

Refer to **Section 3.2** above for the complete step-by-step derivation. The key points:

1. State the arm layout (Arm 1: $C_x$ in series with $r_x$; Arm 2: $C_2$; Arm 3: $R_3$; Arm 4: $R_4 \parallel C_4$).
2. Compute $Z_1 = r_x + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_x}$ and $Z_4 = \dfrac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_4}$.
3. Apply the balance condition and cross-multiply.
4. Equate real and imaginary parts to obtain $C_x$ and $r_x$.

**Final balance equations:**

$$C_x = C_2 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_4} \qquad \tan \delta = \omega r_x C_x = \frac{1}{\omega R_4 C_4}$$

The **dissipation factor** is defined as the tangent of the loss angle of the capacitor, representing the ratio of resistive (loss) to reactive (capacitive) impedance:

$$\tan \delta = \frac{r_x}{1/(\omega C_x)} = \omega r_x C_x$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $C_2 = 100 \text{ pF} = 100 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$, $R_3 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega = 1000 \Omega$, $R_4 = 5 \text{ k}\Omega = 5000 \Omega$, $C_4 = 250 \text{ pF} = 250 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$, $f = 1 \text{ kHz}$.

**Step 1: Compute $C_x$**

$$C_x = C_2 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_4} = 100 \text{ pF} \times \frac{1000}{5000} = 100 \text{ pF} \times 0.2 = 20 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\omega$**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 1000 = 6283.19 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute the dissipation factor $\tan \delta$**

$$\tan \delta = \frac{1}{\omega R_4 C_4} = \frac{1}{6283.19 \times 5000 \times 250 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{6283.19 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 127.32$$

**Step 4: Compute $r_x$**

From $\tan \delta = \omega r_x C_x$:

$$r_x = \frac{\tan \delta}{\omega C_x} = \frac{127.32}{6283.19 \times 20 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{127.32}{1.257 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.013 \times 10^9 \Omega$$

$$r_x \approx 1013 \text{ M}\Omega$$

**Result:** $C_x = 20 \text{ pF}$, $\tan \delta = 127.32$ (high loss), $r_x \approx 1013 \text{ M}\Omega$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - The dissipation factor $\tan \delta$ is **dimensionless**. Always cross-check that the final answer has no units.
> - For the Schering bridge, students often forget to express $C_x$ in the **correct unit** (pF vs. F). The conversion must be explicit.
> - The high value of $\tan \delta$ in this example indicates a **poor-quality insulator** (low-grade dielectric). In real Schering bridge tests, $\tan \delta$ values are typically **0.001 to 0.1** for good insulators.
> - **Critical:** The Schering bridge is the **only** bridge for measuring the **dissipation factor** of insulating materials. Do not confuse it with De Sauty's bridge (which is only for lossless capacitors).

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this checklist — these are the must-know facts for KTU 2024 ESE.**

- [ ] **General balance equation:** $Z_1 Z_3 = Z_2 Z_4$ (product of opposite arm impedances are equal at null).
- [ ] **Two independent equations** arise from the real and imaginary parts — one for magnitude, one for phase.
- [ ] **Maxwell's bridge:** $L_x = R_1 R_3 C_3$, $R_x = R_1 R_3 / R_2$. **Frequency-independent.** Best for $1 \leq Q \leq 10$.
- [ ] **Hay's bridge:** Standard arm is $R_3$ in **series** with $C_3$. Best for $Q > 10$. Strongly **frequency-dependent**.
- [ ] **Anderson's bridge:** Five-arm modification of Maxwell's. Used for **low-resistance**, precise inductance measurement. **Frequency-independent**.
- [ ] **De Sauty's bridge:** $C_x = C_s \cdot R_3 / R_4$. **Only for lossless capacitors**.
- [ ] **Schering bridge:** Measures $C_x$ and $\tan \delta$. **Dissipation factor:** $\tan \delta = 1/(\omega R_4 C_4)$. Used in **insulation testing**.
- [ ] **Wien bridge:** $f = 1/(2\pi RC)$. Used as **frequency meter** and **audio oscillator**. No inductors needed.
- [ ] **Source frequency:** Standard is **1 kHz** (audio) for most lab bridges; **50 Hz** for power-frequency Schering bridges.
- [ ] **Null detector sensitivity:** Better than **10 µV** for precise balance.
- [ ] **Phasor diagram trick:** For a balanced bridge, the phasors of arm currents must sum to zero at the detector node.
- [ ] **Convergence:** A bridge is convergent only if small changes in variable arms lead to a unique balance point. Most lab bridges are convergent except some Maxwell-type variants.
- [ ] **Stray capacitance and inductance** of connecting leads are the **primary error sources** in lab AC bridges. Always use **short, twisted, shielded leads** and **Wagner's ground** for high-precision work.
- [ ] **Wagner's Earthing Device:** A common modification to AC bridges to eliminate the effect of stray capacitance to ground. Add this to your answer if the question asks about "improving accuracy."

---

### 5.4 KTU Common Mistakes to Avoid (Examiners' Red Flags)

> [!WARNING]
> **These mistakes cost 2–3 marks each in KTU 2024 ESE:**

1. **Confusing series and parallel arm configurations** in Maxwell vs. Hay bridge.
2. **Forgetting to specify the source frequency** when quoting balance equations (it does not always cancel out).
3. **Missing the dissipation factor definition** in Schering bridge answers — examiners specifically look for the formula $\tan \delta = \omega r_x C_x$.
4. **Ignoring the imaginary part** of the balance equation — leading to an incomplete answer with no second equation.
5. **Using Maxwell's bridge for high-Q coils** ($Q > 10$) — wrong selection. Use Hay's.
6. **Stating De Sauty's bridge works for lossy capacitors** — it does not. Use Schering.
7. **Not mentioning Wagner's earth device** when asked about "improving bridge accuracy" — examiners expect this.
8. **Numerical: forgetting to convert $\mu F$ to F and $k\Omega$ to $\Omega$** before substituting into the formula. A single point of unit error loses 1 mark.

---

*End of KTU Premium Notes — Module Experiment 2: Measurement of Capacitance, Inductance, and Frequency Using AC Bridges.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of characteristics of thermal sensors: thermistor, thermocouple, and RTD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL307 - CIRCUITS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of characteristics of thermal sensors: thermistor, thermocouple, and RTD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Thermal Sensor?

A **thermal sensor** (or temperature sensor / temperature transducer) is a device that converts a temperature input into a measurable, repeatable, and predictable electrical output signal. In the context of KTU's PCEEL307 lab, the three principal thermal sensors studied are the **Thermistor**, the **Thermocouple**, and the **Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PCEEL307):** A temperature transducer is a device that transforms thermal energy (heat) into an equivalent proportional electrical quantity (resistance or voltage) following a well-defined, reproducible transfer characteristic that allows quantitative temperature measurement.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three Translators"

Imagine a foreign-language translator sitting in a room where a person only speaks three different languages. The translator is a thermal sensor:

- **Thermocouple** → A *fast, two-wire translator* who only knows short phrases. He speaks quickly but with imperfect grammar; needs another translator (cold-junction compensation) to correct his accent.
- **RTD** → A *precise, high-fidelity translator* who speaks with perfect grammar but takes a long time to translate (slow response) and is expensive to hire.
- **Thermistor** → A *very loud, high-sensitivity translator* who exaggerates everything — small temperature changes produce huge output swings, but he has a very limited "vocabulary range" (small usable temperature range).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students are expected to experimentally determine the *static characteristics* of each thermal sensor: **Sensitivity (mV/°C or Ω/°C), Linearity (% non-linearity), Range, and Repeatability.** The lab record must include tabulated readings, plotted characteristic curves, and computed sensitivity values.

## 1.3 Operating Principles at a Glance

| Sensor | Underlying Physical Effect | Output Quantity | Key Standard |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Thermistor** | Change of semiconductor resistance with temperature | Resistance (Ω) | Steinhart-Hart model |
| **RTD** | Change of metal (Pt) resistance with temperature | Resistance (Ω) | **IEC 60751** (Pt100 / Pt1000) |
| **Thermocouple** | **Seebeck Effect** — dissimilar-metal junction EMF | Voltage (mV) | **IEC 60584** (K, J, T, E, R, S, B types) |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard metric to remember:** The Callendar-Van Dusen equation for a Pt100 RTD defines the nominal resistance at **0 °C as exactly 100 Ω**, with a fundamental interval of **38.5 Ω** between 0 °C and 100 °C.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermistor — Theory of Operation

A thermistor is a thermally sensitive resistor whose resistance varies **non-linearly and strongly** with temperature. Most practical thermistors are made from sintered metal oxides (Mn, Ni, Co, Fe oxides) and are classified as:

- **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient)** — Resistance *decreases* as temperature rises. Most common type.
- **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient)** — Resistance *increases* sharply near a switching temperature (used in motor protection / inrush current limiters).

The conduction mechanism in an NTC thermistor is **semiconductor hopping conduction** — as temperature increases, more charge carriers are thermally excited across the energy gap, increasing conductivity and decreasing resistance.

### 2.1.1 Thermistor Mathematical Models

**Model 1 — Simple Exponential (β-parameter) Equation:**

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \tfrac{1}{T} - \tfrac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

where:
- $R_T$ = resistance at absolute temperature $T$ (in Kelvin)
- $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (typically **298.15 K = 25 °C**)
- $\beta$ = material constant (typical **3000 K to 5000 K** for NTC)

**Model 2 — Steinhart-Hart Equation (more accurate):**

$$\frac{1}{T} = a + b \, \ln(R_T) + c \, (\ln(R_T))^3$$

where $a, b, c$ are empirically fitted Steinhart-Hart coefficients provided by the manufacturer.

**Model 3 — Linearized approximation (for small range ΔT around $T_0$):**

$$R_T \approx R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha \Delta T \right]$$

The **temperature coefficient of resistance (TCR)** for an NTC thermistor is **negative and large** (typically $-3\%/°C$ to $-6\%/°C$), compared to ~$+0.00385/°C$ for a Pt RTD. This makes thermistors roughly **10× more sensitive** than RTDs, but over a much smaller range.

## 2.2 The RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector) — Theory of Operation

An RTD operates on the principle that the electrical resistance of a pure metal increases with temperature due to enhanced **phonon scattering** of conducting electrons. The standard RTD uses **platinum (Pt)** because of its:

- Chemical inertness / stability
- Near-linear positive temperature coefficient
- Wide operating range (–200 °C to +850 °C)
- Excellent repeatability and interchangeability

### 2.2.1 RTD Callendar-Van Dusen Equation

For temperatures $T \ge 0 \, °C$:

$$R_T = R_0 \left( 1 + A T + B T^2 \right)$$

For temperatures $T < 0 \, °C$:

$$R_T = R_0 \left( 1 + A T + B T^2 + C (T - 100) T^3 \right)$$

The **IEC 60751** standard values for the coefficients are:

$$A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \, °C^{-1}$$
$$B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \, °C^{-2}$$
$$C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12} \, °C^{-4}$$

### 2.2.2 Pt100 vs Pt1000

- **Pt100** — $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$ — Standard, lower self-heating, longer lead tolerance.
- **Pt1000** — $R_0 = 1000 \, \Omega$ — Higher signal level, less susceptible to lead-wire resistance, used in 2-wire industrial sensors.

## 2.3 The Thermocouple — Theory of Operation

A thermocouple consists of **two dissimilar conductors joined at one end (the hot / measuring junction)**. When this junction is held at a different temperature from the other end (the **cold / reference junction**), a continuous EMF is generated, following the **Seebeck Effect** (discovered by Thomas Johann Seebeck in 1821).

The open-circuit thermoelectric EMF is given by the **integral of the relative Seebeck coefficient** along the temperature gradient:

$$E = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} S_A(T) - S_B(T) \, dT$$

where $S_A(T)$ and $S_B(T)$ are the absolute Seebeck coefficients of the two thermoelements.

### 2.3.1 The Law of Intermediate Metals and Cold-Junction Compensation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Law of Intermediate Metals:** Inserting a third metal into a thermocouple circuit does NOT change the net EMF, provided both new junctions are at the SAME temperature. This allows copper lead-wires and terminal blocks to be connected without error — *but only if both connections are isothermal*.

**Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC):** Real thermocouples require a known, stable reference temperature at the cold end. This is achieved by:
1. **Ice-bath reference** ($0 \, °C$) — laboratory standard, used in the KTU experiment.
2. **Electronic CJC** — using a precision silicon sensor at the terminal block + software polynomial correction.

### 2.3.2 Common Thermocouple Types

| Type | (+) Leg | (–) Leg | Range (°C) | Seebeck Coeff. (μV/°C) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **K** | Chromel (Ni-Cr) | Alumel (Ni-Al) | –200 to +1370 | ~40 |
| **J** | Iron | Constantan (Cu-Ni) | –40 to +750 | ~50 |
| **T** | Copper | Constantan | –200 to +350 | ~40 |
| **E** | Chromel | Constantan | –200 to +900 | ~60 |
| **R / S** | Pt-Rh | Pure Pt | 0 to +1450 | ~10 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU experiment tip:** Type-**K** thermocouples are the most common student-lab choice due to wide range, good sensitivity, and low cost. Chromel-Alumel chromel-alumel wires are color-coded (yellow outer sheath).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Constants |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $R_T = R_0 \, e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$ | NTC thermistor exponential model | $T$ in K, $\beta$ in K |
| 2 | $1/T = a + b \ln R_T + c (\ln R_T)^3$ | Steinhart-Hart 3-coeff. model | $a, b, c$ fitted |
| 3 | $R_T = R_0 (1 + A T + B T^2)$ | RTD (Pt) for $T \ge 0 \, °C$ | $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 4 | $R_T = R_0 (1 + A T + B T^2 + C(T-100)T^3)$ | RTD (Pt) for $T < 0 \, °C$ | $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}$ |
| 5 | $\alpha = \frac{R_{100} - R_0}{100 \cdot R_0}$ | TCR of RTD | $\alpha_{Pt} = 0.00385 \, /°C$ |
| 6 | $E = S \, \Delta T$ | Linearised thermocouple EMF | $S$ in $\mu V/°C$ |
| 7 | $S = \lim_{\Delta T \to 0} \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta T}$ | Seebeck coefficient definition | $\mu V/°C$ |
| 8 | $\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{\Delta \text{Output}}{\Delta \text{Temperature}}$ | Static sensitivity of any sensor | Output unit / °C |
| 9 | $\% \text{Non-linearity} = \frac{\max \vert y_{actual} - y_{ideal} \vert}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | Linearity error | Percentage |
| 10 | $P_{self} = I^2 R_T$ | Self-heating power in RTD / thermistor | Watts |
| 11 | $\Delta T_{self} = P_{self} \cdot \theta$ | Self-heating temperature rise | °C, $\theta$ = thermal resistance |
| 12 | $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$ | Kelvin-Celsius conversion | Constant: **273.15** |

## 2.5 Static Characteristics Expected from the Experiment

For each thermal sensor, the KTU lab viva and record expect the following:

- **Sensitivity (S)** — slope of the calibration curve.
- **Range** — minimum and maximum measurable temperatures.
- **Linearity / Non-linearity** — % deviation from the best-fit straight line.
- **Repeatability** — closeness of repeated readings under identical conditions.
- **Hysteresis** — difference in output when approached from low vs. high temperature.
- **Resolution** — smallest detectable temperature change.
- **Time constant (τ)** — 63.2% of final value after a step change.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Thermocouples dominate furnaces, kilns, and exhaust-gas monitoring (high-temp, fast response). RTDs dominate pharmaceutical, food, and laboratory-grade precision (–50 °C to +500 °C). Thermistors dominate medical (clinical thermometers), battery-pack management, and HVAC inlet-air sensors (limited range, very high sensitivity).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linearized Sensitivity of an NTC Thermistor

We start from the fundamental exponential model:

$$R_T = R_0 \, e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

The **instantaneous slope** $dR_T / dT$ gives the local sensitivity. Applying the chain rule:

$$\frac{dR_T}{dT} = R_0 \, e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)} \cdot \frac{d}{dT} \left[ \beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right) \right]$$

Evaluate the inner derivative:

$$\frac{d}{dT} \left[ \beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right) \right] = \beta \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{T^2} \right) = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}$$

Substitute back:

$$\frac{dR_T}{dT} = R_0 \, e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)} \cdot \left( -\frac{\beta}{T^2} \right) = -\frac{\beta}{T^2} \cdot R_T$$

Therefore the **thermistor sensitivity** at any temperature $T$ is:

$$S_T = \frac{dR_T}{dT} = -\frac{\beta \, R_T}{T^2}$$

The **TCR** (temperature coefficient of resistance) is then:

$$\alpha_T = \frac{1}{R_T} \cdot \frac{dR_T}{dT} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked numerical example (typical KTU value):** A thermistor has $R_0 = 10 \, k\Omega$ at $T_0 = 298.15 \, K$ and $\beta = 3950 \, K$. Calculate its resistance and TCR at $T = 323.15 \, K$ (i.e., 50 °C).

**Step 1 — Compute the resistance:**

$$R_T = 10{,}000 \times e^{3950 \left( \frac{1}{323.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} \right)}$$

$$\frac{1}{323.15} = 0.0030945, \quad \frac{1}{298.15} = 0.0033540$$

$$\Delta = 0.0030945 - 0.0033540 = -0.0002595$$

$$R_T = 10{,}000 \times e^{3950 \times (-0.0002595)} = 10{,}000 \times e^{-1.0252}$$

$$R_T = 10{,}000 \times 0.3589 = 3589.3 \, \Omega \approx 3.59 \, k\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Compute the TCR:**

$$\alpha_T = -\frac{3950}{(323.15)^2} = -\frac{3950}{104426} = -0.03783 \, /°C = -3.78 \, \%/°C$$

This is the **large negative TCR** characteristic of NTC thermistors.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — RTD Sensitivity and Linearity

For $T \ge 0 \, °C$, the RTD equation is:

$$R_T = R_0 (1 + A T + B T^2)$$

The sensitivity (slope) is:

$$\frac{dR_T}{dT} = R_0 (A + 2 B T)$$

The **fractional sensitivity** is:

$$\frac{1}{R_T} \cdot \frac{dR_T}{dT} = \frac{A + 2 B T}{1 + A T + B T^2}$$

**Numerical evaluation at $T = 100 \, °C$** (using IEC 60751 values):

$$A + 2BT = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} + 2 \times (-5.775 \times 10^{-7}) \times 100$$

$$= 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} - 1.155 \times 10^{-4} = 3.7928 \times 10^{-3} \, /°C$$

The **resistance change** between 0 °C and 100 °C (used to compute TCR experimentally):

$$\alpha = \frac{R_{100} - R_0}{100 \cdot R_0} = \frac{138.5 - 100}{100 \times 100} = \frac{38.5}{10{,}000} = 0.00385 \, /°C$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Linearity check for a Pt100 at 0 °C vs 100 °C:**
> - From the *linear assumption* $R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$: $R_{100}^{ideal} = 100 \times (1 + 0.00385 \times 100) = 138.5 \, \Omega$ ✓
> - From the *quadratic Callendar-Van Dusen equation* at $T = 100 \, °C$: $R_{100} = 100 (1 + 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 - 5.775 \times 10^{-7} \times 10{,}000) = 138.5 \, \Omega$
> - The quadratic correction is **engineered to vanish exactly at 100 °C** so the IEC 60751 fundamental interval $\Delta = 38.5 \, \Omega$ holds.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermocouple Sensitivity and Cold-Junction Correction

The Seebeck EMF for a small temperature difference is:

$$E = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} S(T) \, dT \approx S \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$

For the **Law of Intermediate Metals**, suppose we have a thermocouple with junctions at $T_h$ (measuring), $T_c$ (reference), and we insert a third metal M at temperature $T_m$:

$$E_{total} = \int_{T_c}^{T_m} S_A(T) - S_B(T) \, dT + \int_{T_m}^{T_h} S_A(T) - S_M(T) \, dT$$

If both junctions of M are at the same $T_m$, by reversing the second integral and using the linearity of integrals:

$$E_{total} = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} [S_A(T) - S_B(T)] \, dT = E \text{ (original)}$$

**Cold-Junction Compensation formula** (software / electronic method):

$$E_{abs}(T_h) = E_{meas}(T_h, T_c) + E_{reference}(T_c)$$

That is: add the EMF the thermocouple *would* have produced if the cold junction were at 0 °C, to the measured EMF (whose cold junction is at the actual ambient $T_c$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked numerical example:** A K-type thermocouple measures $4.096 \, mV$ with the hot junction in boiling water ($100 \, °C$) and the cold junction at room temperature $25 \, °C$.
> Reference EMF tables: $E(100\,°C) = 4.096 \, mV$, $E(25\,°C) = 1.000 \, mV$.
> The "ice-bath corrected" EMF = $4.096 - 1.000 = 3.096 \, mV$.
> Looking up $3.096 \, mV$ on the K-type cold-junction-zeroed table gives the **true hot-junction temperature** ≈ $75.5 \, °C$ (in this hypothetical, demonstrating the method — in practice the math is reversed: $E_{abs} = E_{meas} + E_{ref}$).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Property | Thermocouple | RTD (Pt100) | NTC Thermistor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Active element** | Two dissimilar metals | Platinum wire / film | Metal-oxide semiconductor |
| **Output signal** | mV (EMF) | Ω (resistance) | Ω (resistance) |
| **Sensitivity** | 10–60 μV/°C | ~0.385 Ω/°C | –100 to –500 Ω/°C |
| **Range** | –270 to +2300 °C | –200 to +850 °C | –55 to +150 °C |
| **Linearity** | Moderate (polynomial) | Excellent (quasi-linear) | Poor (exponential) |
| **Accuracy (raw)** | ±1 to ±2 °C | ±0.1 to ±0.5 °C | ±0.1 to ±1 °C |
| **Stability / Drift** | ~1 °C / year | <0.05 °C / year | ~0.2 °C / year |
| **Response time (τ)** | 0.1 to 10 s | 3 to 10 s | 0.5 to 5 s |
| **Self-heating** | None | Low (mW) | Significant (mW–W) |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Highest | Low |
| **Excitation required?** | No (self-generating) | Yes (current source) | Yes (voltage divider) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Heatable Water or Oil Bath with Precision Thermometer]
    srcB[Reference 0 deg C Ice Bath in Dewar Flask]
    pA[Thermistor in Probe Sheath]
    pB[Pt100 RTD in Probe Sheath]
    pC[K Type Thermocouple Hot Junction]
    mA[Digital Multimeter Ohm Meter for Thermistor]
    mB[Digital Multimeter Ohm Meter for RTD]
    mC[Digital Millivoltmeter for Thermocouple]
    tab[Observation Table]
    plot[Plot R vs T and E vs T Graphs]

    srcA --> pA
    srcA --> pB
    srcA --> pC
    pA --> mA
    pB --> mB
    pC --> mC
    pC -.Cold Junction.-> srcB
    mA --> tab
    mB --> tab
    mC --> tab
    tab --> plot
```

## 4.2 Voltage Divider for Thermistor / RTD Excitation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    vcc[+5V DC Regulated Supply]
    r1[Fixed Precision Resistor Rref 10k ohm]
    th[Thermal Sensor R T changing with T]
    gnd[Ground 0V]
    vout[Output Vout to ADC or Voltmeter]

    vcc --- r1
    r1 --- vout
    vout --- th
    th --- gnd
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of Thermocouple Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hotJ[Hot Junction Th at Measuring Point]
    tc[K Type Thermocouple Wires Chromel Alumel]
    coldJ[Cold Junction Tc at Terminal Block]
    cjc[Ice Bath or Electronic CJC Sensor]
    amp[Precision Low Offset Amplifier INA128]
    adc[24 bit Delta Sigma ADC]
    lcd[Digital Display or PC Data Logger]
    corr[Polynomial Linearization Software]
    refT[Reference Tables NIST ITS90]

    hotJ --> tc
    tc --> coldJ
    coldJ --> cjc
    coldJ --> amp
    amp --> adc
    adc --> corr
    cjc --> corr
    refT --> corr
    corr --> lcd
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow for Selecting a Thermal Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Choose Thermal Sensor]
    q1{Temperature above 500 deg C?}
    q2{Need fast response millisecond?}
    q3{Best accuracy and stability required?}

    start --> q1
    q1 -- Yes --> useTC[Use Thermocouple Type K or R]
    q1 -- No --> q2
    q2 -- Yes --> useTC2[Use Exposed Bead Thermocouple]
    q2 -- No --> q3
    q3 -- Yes --> useRTD[Use Pt100 or Pt1000 RTD with 4 wire]
    q3 -- No --> useNTC[Use NTC Thermistor for high sensitivity low cost]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram Showing Static Characteristic Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1 Heat Bath to First Setpoint T1]
    step2[Step 2 Wait for Thermal Equilibrium 2 minutes]
    step3[Step 3 Read Output R or E from All Three Sensors]
    step4[Step 4 Record in Tabular Form Repeat for T2 T3 ... Tn]
    step5[Step 5 Compute Sensitivity Delta Output per Delta T]
    step6[Step 6 Plot Graphs and Compute Non linearity percent]
    step7[Step 7 Verify against Theoretical Model]
    step8[Step 8 Write Conclusion in Lab Record]

    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> step7
    step7 --> step8
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Model]`
**Define the Seebeck effect. How does it form the basis of thermocouple operation?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Seebeck effect** is the phenomenon by which a continuous electromotive force (EMF) is generated in a closed circuit made of two dissimilar conductors when their junctions are maintained at different temperatures. The magnitude of the EMF is approximately proportional to the temperature difference and the **relative Seebeck coefficient** of the two materials. In a thermocouple, one junction (the *hot / measuring junction*) is exposed to the unknown temperature, while the other (the *cold / reference junction*) is held at a known reference temperature (commonly $0 \, °C$ in an ice bath). The resulting millivolt-level EMF is then read and converted to temperature using standardised reference tables (e.g., **NIST ITS-90** for K-type thermocouples). *(3 marks: definition 1, proportionality 1, application to thermocouple 1.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**A Pt100 RTD has a resistance of $138.5 \, \Omega$ at $100 \, °C$ and $100 \, \Omega$ at $0 \, °C$. Calculate the temperature coefficient of resistance (α).**

**Model Answer:**
The TCR is defined as the fractional change in resistance per degree Celsius, relative to the resistance at 0 °C:

$$\alpha = \frac{R_{100} - R_0}{100 \cdot R_0} = \frac{138.5 - 100}{100 \times 100}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{38.5}{10{,}000} = 0.00385 \, /°C = 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \, °C^{-1}$$

This matches the **IEC 60751 standard value** for platinum. *(3 marks: formula 1, substitution 1, final answer 1.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the experimental procedure to determine the static characteristics (sensitivity, linearity, range) of an NTC thermistor using a temperature-controlled water bath. Mention the apparatus, observation table format, and safety precautions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An NTC thermistor has $R_{25} = 10 \, k\Omega$ and $R_{50} = 3.6 \, k\Omega$. Calculate (i) the β-constant, (ii) the resistance at 80 °C, and (iii) the temperature coefficient of resistance at 80 °C. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 marks

**Apparatus Required (1.5 marks):**
- NTC thermistor probe (with manufacturer-specified $R_{25}$ and β)
- Heating water bath with stirring motor and temperature controller
- Mercury-in-glass thermometer (0–100 °C, 0.5 °C least count) **or** calibrated Pt100 reference sensor
- Digital multimeter (Ω mode, 4½ digit) **or** precision resistance bridge
- Connecting leads, beaker, distilled water

**Experimental Setup & Block Diagram (1.5 marks):**
The thermistor is inserted into a sealed glass probe sheath immersed in the water bath alongside the reference thermometer. The leads connect to the DMM set to resistance mode. A regulated stirrer ensures uniform bath temperature.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bath[Heated Water Bath]
    thermo[Mercury Thermometer]
    ntc[NTC Thermistor in Sheath]
    dmm[Digital Multimeter Ohm]
    op[Record R at Each T]
    pl[Plot R vs T Curve]

    bath --- thermo
    bath --- ntc
    ntc --- dmm
    dmm --- op
    op --- pl
```

**Procedure (2.5 marks):**
1. Fill bath with distilled water; insert thermistor probe and reference thermometer at the same depth.
2. Switch on the heater and stirrer. Heat slowly.
3. Allow the bath to stabilise at 30 °C (±0.5 °C) for ~2 minutes.
4. Read the resistance $R_T$ on the DMM and record the temperature on the observation table.
5. Increase temperature in 5 °C steps up to 90 °C. At each step wait for equilibrium and record.
6. Repeat the readings in descending order (cooling) to assess **hysteresis**.
7. Tabulate $(T, R_T)$, then plot $R_T$ vs $T$ on linear graph paper, and $\ln R_T$ vs $1/T$ on semi-log paper to verify the exponential model.

**Safety Precautions (0.5 mark):**
- Do not exceed the thermistor's maximum temperature rating.
- Avoid water ingress at the probe terminal.
- Use tongs when handling hot bath components.
- Ensure the heater is switched off when the experiment ends and the bath is cooling.

**Calculations to be performed (1 mark):**
- Sensitivity $S = \Delta R / \Delta T$ at multiple intervals.
- β-constant from two known $(R, T)$ pairs using the exponential equation.
- % non-linearity: $\frac{\max \vert R_{actual} - R_{ideal-line} \vert}{R_{FS}} \times 100$.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $R_{25} = 10{,}000 \, \Omega$ at $T_0 = 25 \, °C = 298.15 \, K$; $R_{50} = 3{,}600 \, \Omega$ at $T = 50 \, °C = 323.15 \, K$.

**Step 1 — Calculate β (2.5 marks):**

Rearrange the exponential equation:

$$\beta = \frac{\ln(R_T / R_0)}{\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

$$\frac{R_T}{R_0} = \frac{3600}{10000} = 0.36$$

$$\ln(0.36) = -1.0217$$

$$\frac{1}{323.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} = 0.0030945 - 0.0033540 = -2.5954 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\beta = \frac{-1.0217}{-2.5954 \times 10^{-4}} = 3936.4 \, K \approx 3936 \, K$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $R_{80}$ (2.5 marks):**

$T = 80 \, °C = 353.15 \, K$.

$$R_{80} = 10{,}000 \times e^{3936.4 \left( \frac{1}{353.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} \right)}$$

$$\frac{1}{353.15} = 2.8320 \times 10^{-3}, \quad \frac{1}{298.15} = 3.3540 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta = 2.8320 \times 10^{-3} - 3.3540 \times 10^{-3} = -5.220 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$R_{80} = 10{,}000 \times e^{3936.4 \times (-5.220 \times 10^{-4})} = 10{,}000 \times e^{-2.0548}$$

$$R_{80} = 10{,}000 \times 0.1282 = 1282 \, \Omega \approx 1.28 \, k\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the TCR at 80 °C (2 marks):**

$$\alpha_T = -\frac{\beta}{T^2} = -\frac{3936.4}{(353.15)^2} = -\frac{3936.4}{124715} = -0.03157 \, /°C$$

$$\alpha_T \approx -3.16 \, \%/°C$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a K-type thermocouple. With a labelled block diagram, describe the method of cold-junction compensation using an ice bath, and derive the expression for the absolute thermoelectric EMF. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A K-type thermocouple is used to measure the temperature of a furnace. The hot junction is at the unknown temperature $T_h$, and the cold junction is maintained at 0 °C in an ice bath. The measured EMF is $20.644 \, mV$. Using the K-type reference table (simplified, $S_{avg} \approx 40.5 \, \mu V/°C$), determine the furnace temperature. If the cold junction is now at $25 \, °C$ ambient and the measured EMF is $19.644 \, mV$, calculate the corrected absolute EMF and re-estimate $T_h$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 marks

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
A **K-type thermocouple** consists of a **Chromel (Ni–Cr)** positive leg and an **Alumel (Ni–Al)** negative leg joined at the hot junction. When the hot and cold junctions are at different temperatures, a thermoelectric EMF is generated by the **Seebeck effect**. The EMF is approximately a linear function of $\Delta T$ for moderate ranges, with $S \approx 40.5 \, \mu V/°C$.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hj[Hot Junction Th in Furnace]
    chromel[Chromel Wire Ni Cr]
    alumel[Alumel Wire Ni Al]
    cj[Cold Junction in Ice Bath 0 deg C]
    mv[Millivoltmeter High Impedance]
    ref[NIST K Type Reference Table]

    hj --> chromel
    chromel --> cj
    alumel --> hj
    cj --> alumel
    cj --> mv
    mv --> ref
```

**Derivation of Absolute EMF (3 marks):**

The Seebeck EMF between two materials A and B over a temperature gradient from $T_c$ to $T_h$ is:

$$E = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} \left[ S_A(T) - S_B(T) \right] dT = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} S_{AB}(T) \, dT$$

For the K-type thermocouple, $S_{AB} = S_{Chromel}(T) - S_{Alumel}(T)$. For small $\Delta T$, the relative Seebeck coefficient is approximately constant, so:

$$E \approx S_{AB} \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$

When the cold junction is at 0 °C (ice bath), $T_c = 273.15 \, K$ and the absolute EMF $E$ depends only on $T_h$. The K-type tables (NIST ITS-90) are constructed using exactly this reference condition. Any deviation of the cold junction from 0 °C requires the **cold-junction compensation correction** (covered in part b).

**Ice-bath CJC explanation:**
The reference junction is immersed in a slushed mixture of **distilled water and pure crushed ice** in a Dewar flask. The phase equilibrium of ice-water ensures the bath is at exactly $0 \, °C$ at 1 atm, giving an absolutely stable reference.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Case 1 — Cold junction at 0 °C (3 marks):**
Measured EMF = $20.644 \, mV$. Since the cold junction is at 0 °C, the table reading directly gives the hot-junction temperature.

$$T_h = \frac{E}{S_{avg}} = \frac{20.644 \times 10^{-3} \, V}{40.5 \times 10^{-6} \, V/°C}$$

$$T_h = \frac{20{,}644 \, \mu V}{40.5 \, \mu V/°C} \approx 509.7 \, °C \approx 510 \, °C$$

**Case 2 — Cold junction at 25 °C (4 marks):**
Measured EMF with cold junction at $25 \, °C$ ambient = $19.644 \, mV$.
We need to add the **reference EMF** of the K-type thermocouple at 25 °C to obtain the absolute EMF (as if cold junction were at 0 °C).

For a K-type thermocouple, $E(25\,°C) \approx 1.000 \, mV$ (standard table value).

$$E_{abs} = E_{meas} + E_{ref}(25\,°C) = 19.644 + 1.000 = 20.644 \, mV$$

Re-estimating $T_h$:

$$T_h = \frac{20.644 \, mV}{40.5 \, \mu V/°C} \approx 509.7 \, °C \approx 510 \, °C$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The corrected result is **identical** to Case 1, confirming the **law of intermediate metals** and validating the cold-junction compensation procedure. The 1 mV offset between the two measured EMFs is exactly the EMF that the K-type thermocouple would have produced between 0 °C and 25 °C.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls (Module 2 — Thermal Sensors):**
> 1. **Mixing up units in the β-equation:** Temperatures MUST be in **Kelvin** (°C + 273.15), NOT in Celsius. A 3-mark error.
> 2. **Forgetting cold-junction compensation in thermocouple questions:** The given EMF is from cold-junction to hot-junction; if the cold-junction is not at 0 °C, you MUST correct it. Examiners specifically test this in Part B.
> 3. **Confusing $R_0$ with $R_T$ in the α formula:** The denominator must be $100 \cdot R_0$, not $100 \cdot R_{100}$ or $100 \cdot R_T$.
> 4. **Sign of thermistor TCR:** It is **negative** for NTC. Writing a positive value loses a mark.
> 5. **Graph format:** Draw $R$ vs $T$ on the **x-axis = Temperature**, not the other way round. KTU records with axis inversion are penalised.
> 6. **Two-wire vs four-wire RTD:** In a real lab setup, lead-wire resistance ($R_{lead}$) adds directly to the RTD reading in a 2-wire configuration. For accurate Pt100 work, use 4-wire Kelvin connection. Examiners may ask for the correction: $R_{true} = R_{measured} - 2 R_{lead}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three thermal sensors** studied: **Thermistor** (NTC, exponential $R$–$T$ relation), **RTD** (Pt, near-linear positive $R$–$T$), **Thermocouple** (Seebeck-effect voltage, self-generating).
- **Steinhart-Hart equation** is the most accurate model for a thermistor, but the **β-equation** is the standard lab derivation.
- **IEC 60751** fixes the Pt100 α = **0.00385 /°C** and $R_0$ = **100 Ω** at 0 °C; the fundamental interval is **38.5 Ω** between 0 °C and 100 °C.
- **K-type thermocouple** is the most common lab type; sensitivity ≈ **40.5 μV/°C**; range roughly –200 °C to +1370 °C.
- **Seebeck effect** is the physical basis of thermocouples; **Law of Intermediate Metals** justifies copper lead-wire connections.
- **Cold-junction compensation** is mandatory for any non-ice-bath thermocouple reading: $E_{abs} = E_{meas} + E_{ref}(T_c)$.
- **Static characteristics** to extract in lab: sensitivity, range, linearity, repeatability, hysteresis, time constant τ.
- **Sensitivity formula** (universal): $S = \Delta \text{Output} / \Delta T$; report units as mV/°C or Ω/°C.
- **% Non-linearity** formula: $\frac{\max \vert y_{actual} - y_{ideal-line} \vert}{y_{FS}} \times 100$.
- **Self-heating** in RTD/thermistor: $P = I^2 R$; use minimum excitation current (typically 1 mA for Pt100) to avoid measurement error.
- **Lab-record essentials:** observation table (T, R, E columns), three graphs (R vs T for thermistor, R vs T for RTD, E vs T for thermocouple), computed sensitivities, comparison table, conclusion.
- **Selection rule of thumb:** **Thermocouple** for very high T / fast response; **RTD** for high accuracy industrial / lab use; **Thermistor** for narrow-range high-sensitivity low-cost applications.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of P-V characteristics of solar PV array and determination of fill factor (study of partial shading may be included)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL307 - CIRCUITS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of P-V characteristics of solar PV array and determination of fill factor (study of partial shading may be included)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Solar Photovoltaic (PV) Array

A **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) Array** is a collection of electrically interconnected solar cells, modules, and panels that convert incident solar irradiance directly into direct current (DC) electrical energy through the **photovoltaic effect**. For laboratory characterization purposes under the KTU 2024 scheme, the array is treated as a non-linear, irradiance-dependent DC source whose terminal behaviour is described by its **Current-Voltage (I-V)** and **Power-Voltage (P-V)** characteristics.

Mathematically, a solar PV array's behaviour is described using the **single-diode equivalent model**:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

Where:
- $I_{ph}$ = Photocurrent generated (proportional to irradiance)
- $I_0$ = Reverse saturation current of the diode
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C (electron charge)
- $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (Boltzmann constant)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature in Kelvin
- $n$ = Ideality factor of the diode
- $R_s$ = Series resistance
- $R_{sh}$ = Shunt resistance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment focuses on **experimental determination** of P-V characteristics, not on solving the implicit transcendental equation analytically. Students must plot the curve using a variable resistive load (rheostat) and identify the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sun-Powered Battery with a Personality"

Think of a solar PV array like a **stubborn rechargeable battery** that behaves oddly:

- **At sunrise (low light):** It produces very little current, like a sleepy engine.
- **At noon (peak light):** It produces its **maximum current (Isc)** when short-circuited, like a fully awake engine at full throttle.
- **When fully loaded (open circuit):** It shows its **maximum voltage (Voc)**, but cannot deliver any current — like an open tap with no water flowing.
- **At some "sweet spot" load:** It produces **maximum power (Pmax)**, where voltage and current are both reasonably high — this is the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why can't a PV panel produce both Isc and Voc simultaneously?**
> A short circuit clamps voltage to zero (V=0), and an open circuit clamps current to zero (I=0). Power (P = V × I) is therefore **zero** at both extremes. The maximum power occurs at an intermediate operating point — the MPP — where the trade-off between V and I is optimally balanced.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Board-Examiner Favourites)

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Unit |
|------|--------|------------------|------|
| Short Circuit Current | $I_{sc}$ | Current when terminals are shorted ($V = 0$) | Amperes (A) |
| Open Circuit Voltage | $V_{oc}$ | Voltage when terminals are open ($I = 0$) | Volts (V) |
| Maximum Power Voltage | $V_{mp}$ | Voltage at the knee of the P-V curve | Volts (V) |
| Maximum Power Current | $I_{mp}$ | Current at the knee of the P-V curve | Amperes (A) |
| Maximum Power | $P_{max}$ | Peak of the P-V curve ($V_{mp} \times I_{mp}$) | Watts (W) |
| Fill Factor | $FF$ | Squareness measure of the I-V curve | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| Efficiency | $\eta$ | Electrical output / solar input | Percentage (%) |

## 1.4 Definition of Fill Factor (FF)

> [!NOTE]
> **Fill Factor** is the ratio of the **actual maximum obtainable power** to the **product of short-circuit current and open-circuit voltage**. It is a measure of how "square" the I-V curve is — the closer FF is to unity, the higher the panel quality.

$$FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \times I_{sc}} = \frac{V_{mp} \times I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \times I_{sc}}$$

**Typical FF values for commercial crystalline silicon panels:** **0.70 to 0.85** (or 70% to 85%).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Single-Diode Equivalent Model — The Foundation

A real solar cell is electrically modelled as a **current source in parallel with a diode**, with parasitic series ($R_s$) and shunt ($R_{sh}$) resistances that account for real-world losses.

**The five physical processes inside a PV cell:**

1. **Photogeneration:** Photons with energy $E \geq E_g$ (bandgap) knock electrons free, generating a current $I_{ph}$.
2. **Recombination loss:** Diode current $I_d = I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$ opposes photocurrent.
3. **Series resistance loss ($R_s$):** Ohmic losses in metal contacts, busbars, and the semiconductor bulk.
4. **Shunt resistance loss ($R_{sh}$):** Leakage currents across the p-n junction (manufacturing defects, edge effects).
5. **Terminal current $I$:** The difference $I_{ph} - I_d - I_{sh}$, which the external load receives.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A **higher $R_{sh}$** and **lower $R_s$** give a **better FF**. Top-tier panels minimise both parasitic losses through careful cell design.

## 2.2 Standard Test Conditions (STC) — The Industry Benchmark

All manufacturer datasheets (and KTU lab evaluations) reference performance under **STC**:
- Irradiance: $G = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$ (AM 1.5 solar spectrum)
- Cell temperature: $T_{cell} = 25^\circ C = 298.15 \text{ K}$
- Air Mass: AM 1.5G (solar zenith angle $\approx 48.2^\circ$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Note:** In a real laboratory, STC conditions are usually achieved using a **solar simulator (Class A or B)**. If natural sunlight is used, students must record the actual irradiance using a **pyranometer** and the cell temperature using a **contact thermometer or RTD**.

## 2.3 Effect of Irradiance (G) and Temperature (T)

**Effect of Irradiance Increase (with T constant):**
- $I_{sc} \propto G$ (linear increase)
- $V_{oc}$ increases **logarithmically** with G
- $P_{max}$ increases approximately linearly

**Effect of Temperature Increase (with G constant):**
- $I_{sc}$ increases very slightly (negligible)
- $V_{oc}$ **decreases** linearly (~2 mV/°C per cell for crystalline Si)
- $P_{max}$ **decreases** — heat is the enemy of solar cells

$$V_{oc}(T) = V_{oc,STC} + \beta \cdot (T - 25)$$

Where $\beta$ is the **temperature coefficient of $V_{oc}$** (negative, typically **−0.3 %/°C to −0.5 %/°C**).

## 2.4 Partial Shading — The "Pitfall" Topic

When a single cell in a series-connected string is shaded, it becomes a **current bottleneck**:
- The shaded cell **cannot support** the string current.
- It becomes **reverse-biased** and may dissipate power as heat → **hot spot formation**.
- The **bypass diode** (anti-parallel across each cell or sub-string) turns ON, bypassing the shaded cell.

> [!WARNING]
> **Multiple Power Peaks Warning:** A partially shaded array exhibits a P-V curve with **multiple local maxima** (typically 2 peaks). A conventional MPPT algorithm (P&O) may get stuck at the local peak. Advanced algorithms (e.g., Global MPPT) or **PV reconfiguration** is needed to reach the true global MPP.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — The Definitive Table

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Physical Significance | Typical Boundary / Range |
|---|----------------------|--------------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | $I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\left(\frac{q(V+IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V+IR_s}{R_{sh}}$ | Single-diode implicit I-V equation | $0 \le I \le I_{sc}$ |
| 2 | $V_{oc} = \frac{nkT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Open circuit voltage (logarithmic in $I_{ph}$) | $\approx 0.5$–$0.7$ V per Si cell |
| 3 | $P = V \times I$ | Instantaneous electrical output power | $0 \le P \le P_{max}$ |
| 4 | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp}$ | Power at the Maximum Power Point (MPP) | Determined experimentally |
| 5 | $FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \times I_{sc}} = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor — squareness of I-V curve | $0.70 \le FF \le 0.85$ (c-Si) |
| 6 | $\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{G \times A_{array}}$ | Conversion efficiency of the array | $15\% \le \eta \le 22\%$ (c-Si) |
| 7 | $N_s = \frac{V_{oc,array}}{V_{oc,cell}}$ | Number of cells in series for a given array voltage | Integer $\ge 1$ |
| 8 | $N_p = \frac{I_{sc,array}}{I_{sc,cell}}$ | Number of parallel branches for a given array current | Integer $\ge 1$ |
| 9 | $V_{oc,array} = N_s \times V_{oc,cell}$ | Array open circuit voltage scales linearly with $N_s$ | Additive |
| 10 | $I_{sc,array} = N_p \times I_{sc,cell}$ | Array short circuit current scales linearly with $N_p$ | Additive |
| 11 | $V_{T} = \frac{nkT}{q}$ | Thermal voltage (≈ $25.85$ mV at 300 K) | Temperature dependent |
| 12 | $\frac{\partial P}{\partial V} = 0$ at MPP | Condition for locating MPP on the P-V curve | $V = V_{mp}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Hack:** Examiners **love** asking students to **derive the FF formula from the I-V curve geometry** (the ratio of the two rectangles). Memorise the geometric interpretation, not just the formula.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Solar Farm Sizing:** FF directly impacts the **Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)**. A 1% increase in FF can save crores over a 25-year project lifetime.
2. **MPPT Inverter Design:** The inverter's MPPT algorithm must operate at the **knee** of the P-V curve to extract $P_{max}$.
3. **Quality Control in Manufacturing:** FF is a **key acceptance metric** in production line testing of solar panels.
4. **Fault Diagnosis:** A drop in FF under constant irradiance/temperature indicates **degradation, PID, or soiling** — useful for predictive maintenance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fill Factor Formula from the I-V Curve Geometry

The Fill Factor is fundamentally a **geometric ratio** between two rectangles on the I-V plane.

**Step 1 — Define the Two Rectangles on the I-V Plane:**

Consider the I-V characteristic curve plotted in the **first quadrant** with voltage $V$ on the X-axis and current $I$ on the Y-axis.

- **Outer Rectangle (R1):** Bounded by $V = V_{oc}$ and $I = I_{sc}$. Its area represents the **theoretical maximum power** if the cell could simultaneously deliver $I_{sc}$ at $V_{oc}$.

$$\text{Area}(R_1) = V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$$

- **Inner Rectangle (R2):** Bounded by $V = V_{mp}$ and $I = I_{mp}$. Its area represents the **actual maximum power** delivered by the cell at the MPP.

$$\text{Area}(R_2) = V_{mp} \times I_{mp} = P_{max}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Fill Factor as the Ratio:**

By definition, the Fill Factor is the ratio of the **inner rectangle area** to the **outer rectangle area**:

$$FF = \frac{\text{Area}(R_2)}{\text{Area}(R_1)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Symbolic Expressions:**

Substituting the area expressions derived in Step 1:

$$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \times I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \times I_{sc}}$$

**Step 4 — Express in Terms of Maximum Power:**

Since $P_{max} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp}$, we can also write:

$$FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \times I_{sc}}$$

This is the **canonical, board-exam-ready form** of the Fill Factor equation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Insight:** A perfectly square I-V curve would give $FF = 1$ (100% squareness), but this is impossible in practice because the curve has a "knee" near the MPP. The rounded shape is what limits FF below unity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Power Equation at Any Operating Point

**Step 1 — General Power Definition:**

The instantaneous electrical power delivered by the PV array to the load is:

$$P = V \times I$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Implicit I-V Equation:**

Using the single-diode model (assuming $R_{sh} \to \infty$ for simplicity, which is acceptable for an ideal introductory analysis):

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$

Substituting into the power equation:

$$P(V) = V \times \left\{ I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] \right\}$$

Expanding the product:

$$P(V) = V \cdot I_{ph} - V \cdot I_0 \left[\exp\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 3 — Locating the Maximum Power Point (MPP):**

At MPP, the derivative of $P$ with respect to $V$ is zero (peak of the curve):

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = 0 \quad \text{at} \quad V = V_{mp}$$

Applying the product rule:

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = I + V \cdot \frac{dI}{dV} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$V_{mp} = - \frac{I_{mp}}{(dI/dV)_{V=V_{mp}}}$$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation:**

$V_{mp}$ occurs at the operating point where the **slope of the I-V curve equals $-I_{mp}/V_{mp}$** — i.e., the line from the origin is **tangent** to the I-V curve at the MPP. This is why the "knee" of the curve is geometrically the maximum power point.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Conversion Efficiency (η)

**Step 1 — Define Input Solar Power:**

The input power from the sun is the product of **irradiance** and the **effective array area**:

$$P_{in} = G \times A_{array}$$

**Step 2 — Define Output Electrical Power:**

The maximum electrical power output at MPP is:

$$P_{out} = P_{max} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp} = FF \times V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$$

**Step 3 — Form the Efficiency Ratio:**

By definition, efficiency is the ratio of output to input:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify:**

$$\eta = \frac{V_{mp} \times I_{mp}}{G \times A_{array}} = \frac{FF \times V_{oc} \times I_{sc}}{G \times A_{array}}$$

This shows that **efficiency is a product of three factors**: the fill factor (curve shape), the open circuit voltage (material bandgap), and the short circuit current (collection efficiency), all normalized by the incident solar flux and the geometric area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Hint:** A typical exam problem gives you $V_{oc}$, $I_{sc}$, $V_{mp}$, $I_{mp}$, array area, and irradiance. Plug into the above formula in **one shot** — no need to derive from scratch. Marks are awarded for **formula selection** and **correct unit consistency**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Solution: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Given:**
- A small PV module with the following STC ratings:
  - $V_{oc} = 22.5$ V
  - $I_{sc} = 3.5$ A
  - $V_{mp} = 18.0$ V
  - $I_{mp} = 3.1$ A
- Module area: $A = 0.5$ m²
- Irradiance: $G = 1000$ W/m²
- Cell temperature: $T = 25^\circ$C

**Required:** Find $P_{max}$, FF, and efficiency $\eta$.

**Step 1 — Calculate Maximum Power:**

$$P_{max} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp} = 18.0 \times 3.1$$

$$P_{max} = 55.8 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Fill Factor:**

First, compute the denominator:

$$V_{oc} \times I_{sc} = 22.5 \times 3.5 = 78.75 \text{ W}$$

Then:

$$FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \times I_{sc}} = \frac{55.8}{78.75}$$

$$FF = 0.7086 \text{ (or } 70.86\% \text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Conversion Efficiency:**

$$P_{in} = G \times A = 1000 \times 0.5 = 500 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{55.8}{500} = 0.1116$$

$$\eta = 11.16\%$$

**Verification:** A small educational module having $\eta \approx 11\%$ is consistent with older or amorphous-silicon technology. Modern monocrystalline panels achieve 18–22%.

---

## 3.5 Partial Shading Analytical Model

Consider a string of **two series-connected cells**, where Cell 1 receives full irradiance $G_1$ and Cell 2 is shaded to $G_2 < G_1$.

**Step 1 — Photocurrents in Each Cell:**

$$I_{ph,1} = C \times G_1 \quad \text{and} \quad I_{ph,2} = C \times G_2$$

Where $C$ is a constant of proportionality (cell area × spectral response).

**Step 2 — Series Constraint:**

The same current must flow through both cells:

$$I_{string} = I_{ph,1} - I_{d,1} = I_{ph,2} - I_{d,2}$$

**Step 3 — Bypass Diode Action:**

If the unshaded cell's photocurrent $I_{ph,1}$ exceeds the shaded cell's ability to support that current, the shaded cell becomes **reverse-biased**. A bypass diode across the shaded cell turns ON, clamping its voltage to roughly **−0.7 V** (one diode drop), effectively **removing the shaded cell from the circuit**.

**Step 4 — Resulting P-V Curve:**

The P-V curve develops **two local maxima**:
- **Local peak:** String operates with bypass diode ON, at reduced voltage.
- **Global peak:** String operates with bypass diode OFF, all cells active, but current limited by the shaded cell.

The total **power loss** is the difference between these two peaks.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Equivalent Circuit Model of a Solar Cell (Single-Diode Model)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[SOLAR IRRADIANCE G]:::input --> B1[PHOTOCURRENT SOURCE Iph]
    B1 --> C1[INTERNAL NODE A]
    C1 --- D1[DIODE D with reverse saturation I0]
    C1 --- E1[SHUNT RESISTANCE Rsh]
    C1 --> F1[SERIES RESISTANCE Rs]
    F1 --> G1[TERMINAL POSITIVE V and I]
    C1 --> H1[TERMINAL NEGATIVE]
    G1 --> I1[VARIABLE LOAD RL RHEOSTAT]:::load
    H1 --> I1
    I1 --> J1[MEASURE V ACROSS LOAD]:::meter
    I1 --> K1[MEASURE I THROUGH LOAD]:::meter
    classDef input fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    classDef load fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    classDef meter fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000
```

**Architecture Interpretation:**

1. **Solar irradiance G** strikes the cell, generating **photocurrent $I_{ph}$** in the equivalent current source.
2. The current source is in **parallel** with the **p-n junction diode** (recombination loss) and the **shunt resistance $R_{sh}$** (leakage loss).
3. The combined current then flows through the **series resistance $R_s$** (ohmic loss in metal contacts) before reaching the **external load $R_L$**.
4. The **rheostat (variable resistor)** is used in the lab to **sweep the operating point** from short-circuit to open-circuit.
5. **Voltmeter and Ammeter** record the $V$ and $I$ at each load setting, which are then plotted to obtain the **I-V and P-V characteristics**.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: PV Array Measurement Test Bench

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[PC WITH DATA ACQUISITION SOFTWARE]:::control --> B1[DC ELECTRONIC LOAD]
    A1 --> C1[IRRADIANCE METER PYRANOMETER]
    A1 --> D1[TEMPERATURE SENSOR RTD]
    B1 --> E1[PV ARRAY UNDER TEST]
    C1 --> E1
    D1 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[CABLE TERMINAL BOX]
    F1 --> G1[DIGITAL MULTIMETER V]
    F1 --> H1[DIGITAL MULTIMETER I]
    G1 --> A1
    H1 --> A1
    B1 --> I1[HEAT SINK WITH FORCED AIR COOLING]:::cooling
    I1 --> E1
    classDef control fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    classDef cooling fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000
```

**Functional Flow Description:**

1. The **PC + DAQ system** is the master controller.
2. The **DC electronic load** programmatically varies the resistance from $0 \, \Omega$ (short circuit) to $\infty$ (open circuit) — eliminating the need for manual rheostat operation.
3. The **pyranometer** continuously records incident irradiance $G$ in W/m².
4. The **RTD sensor** logs the **back-of-module temperature** in real time.
5. The **PV array under test** is the device under measurement.
6. **Voltmeter and ammeter** are isolated and fed back to the PC for real-time plotting.
7. **Forced air cooling** maintains stable temperature (otherwise temperature drift corrupts the I-V curve).

---

## 4.3 I-V and P-V Characteristic Curves — The Two Sides of the Same Coin

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph GRP1 [I-V CHARACTERISTIC CURVE SHAPE]
        A1[VOLTAGE V ON X AXIS] --> B1[CURRENT I ON Y AXIS]
        B1 --> C1[ISCC AT V EQUAL 0]
        C1 --> D1[SHARP DROP NEAR VOC]
        D1 --> E1[OPEN AT V EQUAL VOC I EQUAL 0]
    end
    subgraph GRP2 [P-V CHARACTERISTIC CURVE SHAPE]
        F1[VOLTAGE V ON X AXIS] --> G1[POWER P ON Y AXIS]
        G1 --> H1[ZERO AT V EQUAL 0]
        H1 --> I1[PEAK AT V EQUAL VMP]
        I1 --> J1[ZERO AT V EQUAL VOC]
    end
    GRP1 --- GRP2
```

**Key Observations:**

- The **I-V curve** has a shape like a **"L"** — flat near Isc, then sharply drops near Voc.
- The **P-V curve** is **unimodal** (single hump) under uniform irradiance — rising from zero, peaking at the **knee (Vmp)**, then falling to zero at Voc.
- Under **partial shading**, the P-V curve becomes **multimodal** (multiple humps), complicating MPPT.

---

## 4.4 Partial Shading Effects — Bypass Diode Action

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[PV STRING OF 3 CELLS IN SERIES]:::string --> B1[CELL A FULLY ILLUMINATED G1 EQUAL 1000]
    A1 --> C1[CELL B PARTIALLY SHADED G2 EQUAL 500]
    A1 --> D1[CELL C FULLY ILLUMINATED G3 EQUAL 1000]
    C1 --> E1[REVERSE BIASED STATE]:::shaded
    E1 --> F1[BYPASS DIODE TURNS ON]:::bypass
    F1 --> G1[SHADED CELL BYPASSED VOLTAGE DROPS BY 0.7V]:::bypass
    F1 --> H1[STRING LOSES POWER OUTPUT]:::warning
    H1 --> I1[LOCAL MPP SHIFT REDUCED GLOBAL PMAX]:::warning
    classDef string fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    classDef shaded fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef bypass fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    classDef warning fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF4500,color:#000000
```

**Architecture Walkthrough:**

1. A **3-cell series string** has Cell B partially shaded.
2. Cell B becomes **reverse-biased** because the string current is set by Cells A and C, but Cell B cannot supply that current.
3. The **bypass diode** across Cell B turns ON, clamping the reverse voltage to **~0.7 V**.
4. The string **loses the contribution** of Cell B's voltage (drops by ~0.7 V + forward voltage of shaded cell).
5. The **global MPP** shifts, and a **local MPP** appears at the bypass-on operating point.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Practice:** In real PV panels, a single bypass diode is typically connected across **20–24 cells** (a "sub-string"), not per individual cell — a balance between protection granularity and cost.

---

## 4.5 Standard Experimental Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[START EXPERIMENT] --> B1[VERIFY STC OR RECORD ACTUAL G AND T]
    B1 --> C1[CONNECT RHEOSTAT AS VARIABLE LOAD]
    C1 --> D1[SET RHEOSTAT TO MAXIMUM RESISTANCE]
    D1 --> E1[RECORD VOC AND ISC READINGS]
    E1 --> F1[GRADUALLY DECREASE LOAD RESISTANCE]
    F1 --> G1[RECORD V AND I AT EACH STEP]
    G1 --> H1[CONTINUE TILL RHEOSTAT IS ZERO SHORT CIRCUIT]
    H1 --> I1[COMPUTE P EQUAL V TIMES I AT EACH POINT]
    I1 --> J1[PLOT I-V AND P-V CURVES]
    J1 --> K1[IDENTIFY MPP VMP IMP PMAX]
    K1 --> L1[CALCULATE FF AND EFFICIENCY]
    L1 --> M1[REPEAT UNDER SHADED CONDITION OPTIONAL]
    M1 --> N1[RECORD OBSERVATIONS IN TABULAR FORM]
    N1 --> O1[END EXPERIMENT]
```

**Lab Workflow Summary:**

1. **Record** ambient and cell temperature, irradiance.
2. **Sweep** the rheostat from maximum to zero in small steps.
3. **Tabulate** $(V, I)$ at each step.
4. **Compute** $P = V \times I$ for each step.
5. **Plot** I-V and P-V on graph paper (or use Excel/Python for digital plotting).
6. **Identify** the MPP — the highest $P$ value in the table.
7. **Calculate** FF and $\eta$ using the formulae.
8. **Repeat** with a partial shade (paper overlay on a cell) to observe the P-V distortion.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Fill Factor (FF) of a solar PV cell. What is its significance in PV module performance evaluation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Word Limit: 50–60 words):**

> The **Fill Factor (FF)** is defined as the ratio of the **maximum obtainable power** ($P_{max} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp}$) to the **product of open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current** ($V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$). Mathematically:
>
> $$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \times I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \times I_{sc}}$$
>
> It is a **dimensionless quantity** (typically **0.70 to 0.85** for crystalline silicon cells) and serves as a **measure of the "squareness"** of the I-V curve. A higher FF indicates **lower internal power losses** (small $R_s$, large $R_{sh}$) and **better cell quality**. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List any three differences between a solar cell, a solar module, and a solar PV array.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Word Limit: 60–80 words):**

| Feature | Solar Cell | Solar Module | Solar Array |
|---------|------------|--------------|-------------|
| Definition | Single p-n junction device | Encapsulated group of cells | Multiple interconnected modules |
| Typical Power | ~1–3 W (per cell) | ~50–400 W | > 1 kW (up to MW scale) |
| Voltage Level | ~0.5–0.7 V (Si cell) | ~20–50 V | Hundreds of volts to kV |
| Application | Research, sensors | Rooftop residential | Solar farms, grid integration |

**(3 marks — 1 mark for each valid distinguishing point with example)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Each question typically has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)**, each carrying **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the **single-diode equivalent model of a solar cell**. Derive the governing I-V equation and identify the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During an experiment on a PV module, the following readings were obtained:

| S.No | Voltage $V$ (V) | Current $I$ (A) |
|------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | 0.0 | 3.50 |
| 2 | 5.0 | 3.45 |
| 3 | 10.0 | 3.35 |
| 4 | 15.0 | 3.10 |
| 5 | 18.0 | 2.80 |
| 6 | 20.0 | 2.00 |
| 7 | 21.5 | 1.20 |
| 8 | 22.3 | 0.50 |
| 9 | 22.5 | 0.00 |

Calculate the **Maximum Power**, **Fill Factor**, and **Efficiency** of the module. Given: Array area = 0.5 m², Irradiance = 1000 W/m². **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Single-Diode Model (7 Marks)

**Circuit Diagram Description (2 marks):**
The single-diode equivalent circuit consists of:
- A **current source** representing photocurrent $I_{ph}$ generated by incident light.
- A **diode D** in parallel representing the p-n junction recombination loss.
- A **shunt resistance $R_{sh}$** in parallel representing leakage current paths.
- A **series resistance $R_s$** in series representing ohmic losses in metal contacts and semiconductor bulk.
- The terminals across which the load voltage $V$ and load current $I$ are measured.

**Governing Equation Derivation (3 marks):**

Applying KCL at the internal node:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_{D} - I_{sh}$$

Where the diode current follows the Shockley equation:

$$I_{D} = I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q V_D}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

The voltage across the diode is:

$$V_D = V + I R_s$$

The shunt current is:

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

Substituting into the KCL equation:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

**Component Roles (2 marks):**
- **$I_{ph}$:** Light-generated current, directly proportional to irradiance G.
- **Diode ($I_0$, n):** Represents recombination losses in the semiconductor junction.
- **$R_s$:** Causes voltage drop; higher $R_s$ reduces $V_{oc}$ and degrades FF.
- **$R_{sh}$:** Represents leakage; lower $R_{sh}$ reduces $I_{sc}$ and degrades FF.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Power at Each Point (2 marks):**

| S.No | $V$ (V) | $I$ (A) | $P = V \times I$ (W) |
|------|---------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | 0.0 | 3.50 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 5.0 | 3.45 | 17.25 |
| 3 | 10.0 | 3.35 | 33.50 |
| 4 | 15.0 | 3.10 | 46.50 |
| 5 | 18.0 | 2.80 | **50.40** |
| 6 | 20.0 | 2.00 | 40.00 |
| 7 | 21.5 | 1.20 | 25.80 |
| 8 | 22.3 | 0.50 | 11.15 |
| 9 | 22.5 | 0.00 | 0.00 |

**Step 2 — Identify Maximum Power Point (1 mark):**

The maximum power occurs at S.No 5:

$$P_{max} = 50.40 \text{ W} \quad \text{at} \quad V_{mp} = 18.0 \text{ V}, \quad I_{mp} = 2.80 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Fill Factor (2 marks):**

From the table:
- $V_{oc} = 22.5$ V (S.No 9)
- $I_{sc} = 3.50$ A (S.No 1)

$$V_{oc} \times I_{sc} = 22.5 \times 3.50 = 78.75 \text{ W}$$

$$FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \times I_{sc}} = \frac{50.40}{78.75} = 0.6400$$

$$\boxed{FF = 0.640 \text{ or } 64.0\%}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$P_{in} = G \times A = 1000 \times 0.5 = 500 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{50.40}{500} = 0.1008$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 10.08\%}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **effect of partial shading on the I-V and P-V characteristics of a solar PV array**. With a neat diagram, show the role of **bypass diodes** in mitigating hot-spot formation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solar PV array consists of **36 cells in series**. The following data is available at STC for a single cell:
- $V_{oc,cell} = 0.6$ V, $I_{sc,cell} = 3.5$ A
- $V_{mp,cell} = 0.48$ V, $I_{mp,cell} = 3.1$ A

Calculate: **(i)** Array $V_{oc}$ and $I_{sc}$, **(ii)** Array $P_{max}$, **(iii)** Array Fill Factor, and **(iv)** Array efficiency, given area per cell = 0.0125 m², total cells = 36, irradiance = 1000 W/m². **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Partial Shading and Bypass Diodes (7 Marks)

**Explanation of Effect (3 marks):**

When a portion of a series-connected PV string is shaded (e.g., due to a tree, bird droppings, building shadow), the photocurrent of the shaded cell drops. However, the unshaded cells try to push their full current through the series string. The shaded cell **cannot support** this current and becomes **reverse-biased**. The shaded cell now acts as a **load**, dissipating power as heat — a phenomenon known as **hot-spot formation**. This can permanently damage the cell.

**P-V Curve Distortion (2 marks):**

The P-V curve of a partially shaded array exhibits **multiple peaks** (bimodal or multimodal). The global MPP is significantly lower than the unshaded case. Conventional MPPT algorithms (e.g., Perturb & Observe) may **get stuck at a local peak**, failing to extract the true maximum power.

**Bypass Diode Mitigation (2 marks):**

A **bypass diode** is connected in **anti-parallel** across a sub-string (typically 20–24 cells). When a cell is shaded and reverse-biased, the bypass diode turns ON, **bypassing the shaded section** and limiting the reverse voltage to ~0.7 V. This:
- **Prevents hot-spot heating.**
- **Limits power loss** to only the bypassed sub-string.
- **Allows the rest of the string** to operate near its original MPP.

(Include a labelled diagram showing: shaded cell, bypass diode conduction path, and resulting P-V curve with two peaks.)

#### Part (b) — Array Parameter Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $N_s = 36$, $N_p = 1$ (assumed single string).

**Step 1 — Array $V_{oc}$ and $I_{sc}$ (2 marks):**

$$V_{oc,array} = N_s \times V_{oc,cell} = 36 \times 0.6 = 21.6 \text{ V}$$

$$I_{sc,array} = N_p \times I_{sc,cell} = 1 \times 3.5 = 3.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Array $P_{max}$ (2 marks):**

$$V_{mp,array} = N_s \times V_{mp,cell} = 36 \times 0.48 = 17.28 \text{ V}$$

$$I_{mp,array} = N_p \times I_{mp,cell} = 1 \times 3.1 = 3.1 \text{ A}$$

$$P_{max} = V_{mp,array} \times I_{mp,array} = 17.28 \times 3.1 = 53.568 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Array Fill Factor (1.5 marks):**

$$V_{oc} \times I_{sc} = 21.6 \times 3.5 = 75.6 \text{ W}$$

$$FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \times I_{sc}} = \frac{53.568}{75.6} = 0.7086$$

$$\boxed{FF = 0.709 \text{ or } 70.9\%}$$

**Step 4 — Array Efficiency (1.5 marks):**

$$A_{array} = 36 \times 0.0125 = 0.45 \text{ m}^2$$

$$P_{in} = G \times A_{array} = 1000 \times 0.45 = 450 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{53.568}{450} = 0.1190$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 11.90\%}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **MARK-LOSING PITFALLS — READ CAREFULLY BEFORE THE EXAM**

1. **Confusing $I_{sc}$ with $I_{mp}$:** Many students use $I_{sc}$ in the FF formula instead of $I_{mp}$. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
2. **Forgetting units in efficiency:** If the area is given in cm², students forget to convert to m². **Penalty: 1 mark.**
3. **Mixing $N_s$ and $N_p$:** Students often incorrectly multiply current by $N_s$ or voltage by $N_p$. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
4. **Not identifying $V_{mp}$ correctly:** The MPP is the operating point with the **highest product $V \times I$**, not the highest $V$ or highest $I$ individually. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
5. **Ignoring temperature/irradiance corrections:** If the data is at non-STC, students must apply **temperature coefficients** or **irradiance scaling** for FF/efficiency calculations. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
6. **In partial shading questions, forgetting bypass diode drop:** A forward-biased bypass diode drops ~0.7 V, which **reduces the string voltage**. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
7. **Reporting FF as a percentage without context:** FF is dimensionless (0 to 1). If reported as a percentage, write both forms (e.g., 0.71 or 71%). **Penalty: 0.5 marks (presentation).**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FINAL REVISION BULLET POINTS — Revise 5 Minutes Before the Exam**

- [ ] **Fill Factor (FF)** is the ratio of $P_{max}$ to $(V_{oc} \times I_{sc})$ — a **squareness metric** of the I-V curve.
- [ ] **FF range:** 0.70 to 0.85 for **crystalline silicon**; can drop to 0.5 under partial shading.
- [ ] **Efficiency ($\eta$)** = $P_{max} / (G \times A_{array})$ — always expressed as a decimal or percentage, never as a raw number.
- [ ] **Series connection** of $N_s$ cells: voltages add, current stays the same. **Parallel connection** of $N_p$ strings: currents add, voltage stays the same.
- [ ] **I-V curve** has the shape of a backwards "L": flat at $I_{sc}$, sharply dropping to $V_{oc}$.
- [ ] **P-V curve** is unimodal under uniform irradiance: rises from 0, peaks at **MPP ($V_{mp}$, $I_{mp}$)**, falls to 0.
- [ ] **Maximum Power Point** is found by setting $\frac{dP}{dV} = 0$, not by visual estimation.
- [ ] **Irradiance effect:** $I_{sc} \propto G$ (linear); $V_{oc}$ changes logarithmically; $P_{max}$ scales roughly linearly.
- [ ] **Temperature effect:** $V_{oc}$ **decreases** with temperature; $I_{sc}$ nearly constant; $P_{max}$ **decreases** at higher $T$.
- [ ] **Partial shading** creates **multiple peaks** in the P-V curve; bypass diodes mitigate hot-spot damage.
- [ ] **STC conditions:** $G = 1000$ W/m², $T = 25^\circ$C, AM 1.5G — always quote FF and $\eta$ at STC.
- [ ] **Thermal voltage $V_T$** = $nkT/q$ ≈ **25.85 mV** at 300 K (1 sun at room temp).
- [ ] **Bypass diode drop** ≈ **0.7 V** when forward-biased — reduces string voltage when activated.
- [ ] **Always tabulate** $(V, I, P)$ readings — examiners award marks for proper data presentation.
- [ ] **Units check:** $V$ in Volts, $I$ in Amperes, $P$ in Watts, $A$ in m², $G$ in W/m². Mixed units = lost marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of insulation resistance and earth resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL307 - CIRCUITS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of insulation resistance and earth resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Determination of Insulation Resistance and Earth Resistance

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Insulation Resistance (IR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCEEL307):**
> *Insulation Resistance is the total resistance offered by the insulating material of an electrical apparatus (cable, winding, transformer, motor) between any conductor and the earth, or between two conductors, when a specified DC test voltage is applied.*

In formal SI terms, insulation resistance is the **ohmic resistance** of an insulating medium (rubber, PVC, enamel, oil, mica, paper) that prevents the flow of leakage current between live parts and earthed parts, or between two live conductors of differing potential.

**Physical Units & Magnitudes:**
- **Standard Unit:** Ohm ($\Omega$)
- **Practical Unit:** Mega-ohm ($M\Omega = 10^6 \, \Omega$)
- **High-Voltage Insulation:** Giga-ohm ($G\Omega = 10^9 \, \Omega$)
- **Acceptable Industrial Threshold:** > **1 MΩ** (per IS 732 for LV installations) and > **1000 $\Omega$/volt** of operating voltage (per IEEE 43 for rotating machines).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Leaky Bucket:**
