| **Type** | Active (inductive) | Passive (resistive) | Active system (gauge-based) |
| **Measurand** | Linear displacement | Strain | Force / Weight |
| **Output Signal** | AC differential voltage (mV) | Resistance change ($\Delta R$) | DC differential voltage (mV) |
| **Excitation Required** | AC (1–10 kHz, 1–24 V) | Wheatstone bridge (DC) | DC voltage (typically 10 V) |
| **Sensitivity Range** | 0.5 to 5 mV/V/mm | $GF = 2.0$ (typical) | 2 to 3 mV/V (typical) |
| **Linearity Range** | $\pm 5$ mm typical | Limited (small strain only) | Excellent ($\pm 0.02\%$) |
| **Resolution** | Practically infinite | Very high | Very high |
| **Hysteresis** | Negligible | Negligible | Very low ($< 0.05\%$) |
| **Temperature Sensitivity** | Low | Moderate (use dummy gauge) | Compensated internally |
| **Key Formula** | $V_o = K \cdot x \cdot V_{ex}$ | $\Delta R/R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | $V_o = S \cdot F \cdot V_{ex}$ |
| **Typical Application** | Displacement probe | Stress analysis | Weighing scale |

---

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LVDT Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC EXCITATION SOURCE 1 TO 10 KHZ] -->|VEX| B[PRIMARY COIL P]
    B -->|MUTUAL INDUCTANCE M1| C1[SECONDARY COIL S1]
    B -->|MUTUAL INDUCTANCE M2| C2[SECONDARY COIL S2]
    M1[MOVABLE FERROMAGNETIC CORE POSITION X] -.->|COUPLES MAGNETICALLY| B
    M1 -.->|ENHANCES FLUX IN S1| C1
    M1 -.->|REDUCES FLUX IN S2| C2
    C1 -->|EMF ES1| D[SERIES OPPOSITION SUBTRACTOR]
    C2 -->|EMF ES2| D
    D --> E[DIFFERENTIAL OUTPUT VOLTAGE VO]
    E --> F[PHASE DETECTOR DEMODULATOR]
    F --> G[FILTERED DC OUTPUT]
    G --> H[DISPLAY OR CONTROLLER]
```

---

## 4.2 Wheatstone Bridge with Strain Gauge — Quarter-Bridge Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[STRAIN GAUGE R1 ACTIVE ARM] --> N1((NODE D TOP LEFT))
    A2[FIXED RESISTOR R2 EQUAL TO R] --> N2((NODE C TOP RIGHT))
    A3[FIXED RESISTOR R3 EQUAL TO R] --> N1
    A4[FIXED RESISTOR R4 EQUAL TO R] --> N2
    VEX[DC EXCITATION VOLTAGE VEX POSITIVE] --> N1
    GND[GROUND REFERENCE 0V] --> N2
    N1 --> OUT[DIFFERENTIAL OUTPUT VO]
    N2 --> OUT
    OUT --> AMP[HIGH GAIN INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER]
    AMP --> DSP[ADC AND DISPLAY]
```

---

## 4.3 Full-Bridge Load Cell — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AP[APPLIED FORCE F NEWTONS] --> ELB[ELASTIC ELEMENT MACHINED STEEL OR ALUMINUM BODY]
    ELB --> BEND[ELASTIC DEFORMATION MICROSTRAIN]
    BEND --> G1[STRAIN GAUGE R1 TENSILE AXIS]
    BEND --> G2[STRAIN GAUGE R2 COMPRESSIVE AXIS]
    BEND --> G3[STRAIN GAUGE R3 COMPRESSIVE AXIS]
    BEND --> G4[STRAIN GAUGE R4 TENSILE AXIS]
    G1 --> WB[WHEATSTONE BRIDGE FULL BRIDGE]
    G2 --> WB
    G3 --> WB
    G4 --> WB
    VDC[STABILIZED DC EXCITATION 10V] --> WB
    WB --> VOUT[DIFFERENTIAL OUTPUT VO MILLIVOLTS]
    VOUT --> IA[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER]
    IA --> ADJ[ZERO AND SPAN ADJUSTMENT]
    ADJ --> OUT[CALIBRATED OUTPUT 4 TO 20 MA OR 0 TO 10V]
```

---

## 4.4 Experimental Setup Block Diagram for Lab Determination of Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[LVDT UNDER TEST] --> D1[LVDT SIGNAL CONDITIONER]
    D1 --> CRO1[DIGITAL OSCILLOSCOPE OR DMM]
    D1 --> VR1[VOLTMETER AC MODE]
    S2[STRAIN GAUGE MOUNTED ON BEAM] --> D2[WHEATSTONE BRIDGE MODULE]
    D2 --> VR2[DC MILLIVOLTMETER]
    D2 --> CRO2[OSCILLOSCOPE FOR NOISE CHECK]
    S3[LOAD CELL ON TEST RIG] --> D3[LOAD CELL INDICATOR]
    D3 --> VR3[DC VOLTMETER]
    CAL[CALIBRATED WEIGHT SET] --> S3
    MIC[MICROMETER FOR DISPLACEMENT] --> S1
    HANG[WEIGHTS HANGING FROM BEAM] --> S2
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**Define the term "transducer" and distinguish clearly between an active and a passive transducer with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A transducer is a device that converts a physical quantity (measurand) into a corresponding electrical output signal proportional to the measurand. The output can then be processed, displayed, or recorded.

**Active Transducer:** Generates its own electrical output without needing any external power supply. The energy conversion is from the measurand itself into electricity.
- *Examples:* (i) Thermocouple (heat → voltage) (ii) Piezoelectric crystal (force → charge) (iii) LVDT (displacement → voltage).

**Passive Transducer:** Requires an external excitation (carrier) signal to produce an output. The measurand merely modulates the excitation.
- *Examples:* (i) Strain gauge (resistance changes with strain, needs bridge excitation) (ii) RTD (resistance changes with temperature) (iii) LDR (resistance changes with light).

---

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the advantages and disadvantages of an LVDT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. Frictionless operation (no contact between core and coils) → practically infinite resolution.
2. Very high linearity over the working range ($\pm 0.1$% or better).
3. Robust construction, long operational life, high reliability.
4. Low hysteresis and excellent repeatability.
5. Output is electrical (easy to interface), and phase information gives direction of displacement.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Requires stable AC excitation source (1 kHz to 10 kHz).
2. Sensitive to stray magnetic fields (requires shielding).
3. Core mass limits dynamic response at very high frequencies.
4. Limited to displacement measurement (not direct force or pressure).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Pattern]`

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT). Derive the expression for its output voltage in terms of core displacement. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
An LVDT consists of:
- **One primary coil** P, wound centrally on a hollow cylindrical former.
- **Two identical secondary coils** S1 and S2, wound on either side of the primary and connected in **series-opposition**.
- **A movable soft-iron (ferromagnetic) core**, slid into the hollow former and connected to the measurand shaft.
- A metal housing providing electromagnetic shielding.
- An AC excitation source (typically 1 kHz to 10 kHz, 1 V to 24 V RMS).

**Working (2 marks):**
When the primary is excited, alternating flux links both secondaries. With the core at the **null (central) position**, the mutual inductances M1 and M2 are equal, the induced EMFs are equal in magnitude but opposite in phase (series-opposition), so the output Vo = 0. When the core moves toward S1, M1 increases and M2 decreases, producing a net output whose magnitude is proportional to displacement and whose phase indicates direction.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
Let primary current be $I_p = I_0 \sin(\omega t)$. Then induced EMFs are:
$$e_{s1} = -M_1 \frac{dI_p}{dt}, \quad e_{s2} = -M_2 \frac{dI_p}{dt}$$

With the core at displacement $+x$ toward S1:
$$M_1 = M_0 + kx, \quad M_2 = M_0 - kx$$

Output:
$$V_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = 2kx \left(-\frac{dI_p}{dt}\right) = 2k x V_p \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

The RMS magnitude is $V_o = 2k \omega V_p \cdot x$, which in engineering form is:
$$\boxed{V_o = K \cdot x \cdot V_{ex}}$$

**(b)** An LVDT has a sensitivity of 0.3 mV/V/mm. The excitation voltage is 5 V RMS. Find: (i) the output voltage when the core is displaced by +4 mm, (ii) the output voltage when the core is displaced by -6 mm, and (iii) the smallest displacement that can be detected if the readout instrument has a resolution of 0.06 mV. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step (i):** Positive displacement $+4$ mm.
$$V_o = K \cdot x \cdot V_{ex} = 0.3 \times 4 \times 5 = 6.0 \, \text{mV (in phase with primary)}$$

**Step (ii):** Negative displacement $-6$ mm.
$$V_o = K \cdot x \cdot V_{ex} = 0.3 \times (-6) \times 5 = -9.0 \, \text{mV (180° out of phase)}$$

**Step (iii):** Resolution.
$$\delta x = \frac{V_{min}}{K \cdot V_{ex}} = \frac{0.06}{0.3 \times 5} = \frac{0.06}{1.5} = 0.04 \, \text{mm} = 40 \, \mu\text{m}$$

**Final Answer:** (i) $+6.0$ mV in phase, (ii) $-9.0$ mV out of phase, (iii) $40 \, \mu$m.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Internal Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a strain gauge. Derive the expression for gauge factor and discuss the factors that affect it. Mention the role of a Wheatstone bridge in strain measurement. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
A bonded metallic strain gauge consists of a thin foil grid etched on a flexible backing. When bonded to a structural member under stress, the gauge experiences the same strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ as the surface. This strain produces a fractional change in resistance $\Delta R / R$ of the gauge. The change is measured using a Wheatstone bridge.

**Derivation of Gauge Factor (3 marks):**
$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A} \Rightarrow \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$

For a circular wire with Poisson ratio $\nu$:
$$\frac{\Delta A}{A} = -2\nu \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$

The piezoresistive effect gives $\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi_l E \varepsilon$.

Substituting:
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon + \pi_l E \varepsilon = [1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E] \cdot \varepsilon$$

By definition:
$$\boxed{GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E}$$

**Factors Affecting GF (1 mark):**
- Poisson's ratio of the gauge material.
- Piezoresistive coefficient $\pi_l$ (large for semiconductors, near zero for Constantan).
- Temperature (affects $\nu$, $E$, and $\pi_l$).
- Strain level (GF is slightly nonlinear at very high strain).

**Wheatstone Bridge Role (1 mark):**
A Wheatstone bridge converts the tiny $\Delta R$ into a measurable differential voltage $V_o$. It also compensates for temperature-induced resistance changes when dummy (unstrained) gauges are used in adjacent arms.

**(b)** A strain gauge with nominal resistance $350 \, \Omega$ and gauge factor $2.1$ is bonded to a steel cantilever beam. A Wheatstone bridge (quarter configuration) is used with an excitation of 10 V. The beam is loaded such that the strain developed is $600 \, \mu\varepsilon$. Calculate: (i) the change in resistance $\Delta R$, (ii) the output voltage $V_o$, and (iii) the output voltage if the same setup were reconfigured as a full bridge (assuming only one gauge is active per arm). (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step (i):** Change in resistance.
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 600 \times 10^{-6} = 1.26 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta R = 350 \times 1.26 \times 10^{-3} = 0.441 \, \Omega$$

**Step (ii):** Quarter-bridge output.
$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{10}{4} \times 2.1 \times 600 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$V_o = 2.5 \times 1.26 \times 10^{-3} = 3.15 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{V} = 3.15 \, \text{mV}$$

**Step (iii):** Full-bridge output (4× the quarter-bridge sensitivity).
$$V_o^{\text{full}} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon = 10 \times 2.1 \times 600 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$V_o^{\text{full}} = 1.26 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{V} = 12.6 \, \text{mV}$$

**Final Answer:** (i) $\Delta R = 0.441 \, \Omega$, (ii) $V_o = 3.15$ mV (quarter bridge), (iii) $V_o = 12.6$ mV (full bridge).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in LVDT Sensitivity:** $K$ is **always given in mV/V/mm** in KTU problems. Students frequently forget to multiply by excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ and lose 2 marks.
>
> 2. **Sign of $V_o$ in LVDT:** KTU questions *always* test whether students specify the **phase/polarity**. Writing only the magnitude and ignoring "in phase" or "180° out of phase" costs 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Wheatstone Bridge Balance:** When all four arms are equal and only one active gauge is present, the output formula $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ is an **approximation valid only for $\Delta R \ll R$**. Writing the exact formula and then not simplifying shows mathematical maturity and earns full credit.
>
> 4. **Strain Gauge — Confusion between $\varepsilon$ and $\mu\varepsilon$:** $1 \, \mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$. A strain of $400 \, \mu\varepsilon = 4 \times 10^{-4}$. Mislacing the decimal point is the single most common error in KTU exam scripts.
>
> 5. **Load Cell Sensitivity Definition:** Rated output of "2 mV/V" means 2 mV **per volt of excitation** at full-scale load. Students often treat it as a fixed voltage, ignoring the excitation, leading to wrong answers in problems where $V_{ex}$ is varied.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LVDT** is an *active, inductive, displacement* transducer with **infinite resolution**, **frictionless operation**, and **S-shaped characteristic curve**.
- **LVDT output:** $V_o = K \cdot x \cdot V_{ex}$; sign of $V_o$ indicates direction of core motion.
- **Strain Gauge** is a *passive, resistive* transducer whose resistance changes by $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ when subjected to mechanical strain.
- **Gauge Factor:** $GF = (1 + 2\nu) + \pi_l E \approx 2.0$ for Constantan, $\approx 100$–$200$ for semiconductors.
- **Wheatstone Bridge** converts $\Delta R$ into a measurable $V_o$, provides **temperature compensation** via dummy gauges, and increases sensitivity from quarter to full bridge ($1 \times \to 4 \times$).
- **Quarter-bridge:** $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$; **Full-bridge:** $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$.
- **Load Cell** = elastic element + 4 strain gauges + Wheatstone bridge. Output: $V_o = S \cdot F \cdot V_{ex}$, where $S$ is in mV/V per unit load.
- **Load Cell Characteristics** (always plot in lab report): **Linearity curve, Hysteresis loop, Sensitivity, Repeatability, Creep**.
- **Strain** $\varepsilon$ is dimensionless; in practice expressed as **microstrain** ($\mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$).
- **Stress–strain relation:** $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ (Hooke's law, valid in elastic region).
- **LVDT requires AC excitation** (typically 1 kHz to 10 kHz); **Strain gauge bridge uses DC excitation** (typically 5 V or 10 V); **Load cell uses DC excitation** (typically 10 V).
- **For KTU viva:** Be ready to explain why LVDT has infinite resolution, why Constantan is preferred for strain gauges (low piezoresistive coefficient → low temperature sensitivity), and why load cells use full-bridge configuration (best sensitivity + best temperature compensation).
- **Lab Experiment Tip:** While plotting load cell characteristic curve, always do **loading and unloading cycles** to demonstrate hysteresis, and report **sensitivity (mV/V per kg)** with its **R² value** for linearity fit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of circuits using software platforms like PSpice/LT spice/MATLAB/Multisim
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL307 - CIRCUITS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of circuits using software platforms like PSpice/LT spice/MATLAB/Multisim

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Circuit Simulation** is the computational process of mathematically modeling an electrical or electronic circuit using specialized software platforms to predict, verify, and analyze its behavior under specified stimuli — without the need for physical construction. The simulation engine internally constructs a system of equations (typically using **Modified Nodal Analysis — MNA**) derived from Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) and Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL), and solves them numerically to compute node voltages, branch currents, and time-domain/transient responses.

The four industry/academic standard platforms covered in the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus are:

| Platform | Origin / Vendor | License |
|---|---|---|
| **PSpice** | Cadence Design Systems (originally UC Berkeley SPICE) | Commercial (Student version free) |
| **LTspice** | Analog Devices (Linear Technology) | **Freeware** |
| **MATLAB / Simulink** | MathWorks | Commercial (Student version available) |
| **Multisim** | National Instruments (now NI / Emona Instruments) | Commercial (Educational edition) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** All four tools trace their numerical kernel back to the original **SPICE (Simulation Program with Integrated Circuit Emphasis)** developed at the **University of California, Berkeley in 1973** by Larry Nagel. LTspice and PSpice are direct descendants and use the **`.cir` (SPICE netlist)** file format. MATLAB/Multisim provide graphical front-ends over their own solvers (and in Multisim's case, an XSPICE-based engine).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of circuit simulation software as a **"Virtual Electronics Laboratory Breadboard."**

- **Without simulation:** You physically solder components, connect a function generator, switch ON the power, and pray nothing blows up. If a resistor value is wrong, you waste time, money, and components.
- **With simulation:** You "draw" the circuit on a computer screen, click a button, and the software instantly tells you what every node voltage and branch current would be — *before* a single component is touched.

> **Real-world analogy:** A circuit simulator is to an electrical engineer what a **wind tunnel** is to an aerospace engineer. Just as planes are tested against virtual airflow before a single rivet is placed, circuits are tested against virtual electron flow before a single resistor is soldered.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s**
- **Electron charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C**
- **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
- **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = \frac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{25.85 \ mV}$ (used by SPICE for semiconductor models)
- **Standard SPICE default temperature:** **27 °C (300 K)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Netlist:** A plain-text description of a circuit's topology, components, and connections, written in the SPICE syntax. It is the *machine-readable* form of the schematic and is the native input format of LTspice and PSpice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Schematic Capture:** The graphical process of drawing a circuit diagram on screen, which the software automatically converts into a netlist for the simulation engine.

### 1.4 Why Simulation? (Engineering Justification)

1. **Safety:** High-voltage or high-current circuits can be tested with zero physical risk.
2. **Cost Reduction:** Component wear, burned PCBs, and damaged instruments are eliminated.
3. **Speed:** Transient sweeps over milliseconds can be simulated in seconds.
4. **Visibility:** Probes can be placed at *any* node — even inside an IC — something a physical oscilloscope cannot do.
5. **Sensitivity / Monte Carlo Analysis:** Component tolerances can be statistically swept — a near-impossible manual task.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Internal Engine — How SPICE Solves Your Circuit

When you draw a circuit in any of the four tools, the software internally performs the following pipeline:

1. **Schematic Capture** → user-friendly GUI input.
2. **Netlist Generation** → automatic translation to a `.cir` text file listing every component and its node numbers.
3. **Modified Nodal Analysis (MNA) Matrix Formulation** → the circuit is expressed as a matrix equation $\mathbf{G \cdot x = b}$.
4. **Numerical Solver** → **LU Decomposition** for DC, **Newton-Raphson iteration** for nonlinear devices, **Backward Euler / Trapezoidal integration** for transient.
5. **Post-Processing** → plotter/probe displays node voltages, currents, and Fourier results.

### 2.2 Modified Nodal Analysis (MNA) — The Heart of SPICE

For a circuit with $N$ nodes and voltage sources, the system matrix takes the block form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
\mathbf{G} & \mathbf{B} \\
\mathbf{C} & \mathbf{D}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\mathbf{v} \\
\mathbf{j}
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\mathbf{i} \\
\mathbf{e}
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{G}$ = **conductance matrix** (size $N \times N$) containing $1/R$, $1/j\omega L$, $j\omega C$ entries.
- $\mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C}$ = incidence matrices coupling voltage sources to nodes.
- $\mathbf{D}$ = typically a zero or small matrix for ideal sources.
- $\mathbf{v}$ = vector of unknown node voltages.
- $\mathbf{j}$ = vector of unknown currents through voltage sources.
- $\mathbf{i}$ = vector of known current injections.
- $\mathbf{e}$ = vector of known voltage source values.

### 2.3 Three Standard Analyses Available in Every Tool

| Analysis Type | SPICE Directive | Purpose | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DC Operating Point (.OP)** | `.op` | Computes quiescent bias voltages/currents | Verifies biasing of an amplifier |
| **DC Sweep (.DC)** | `.DC V1 0 5 0.1` | Sweeps a source, plots DC transfer curve | Drawing the I-V curve of a diode |
| **AC Analysis (.AC)** | `.AC dec 10 1 100Meg` | Small-signal frequency response (Bode plot) | Finding the cutoff frequency of an RC filter |
| **Transient (.TRAN)** | `.TRAN 1m 100m` | Time-domain response to a stimulus | Step response of an RLC circuit |
| **Fourier (.FOUR)** | `.FOUR 1k V(out)` | Computes THD of a periodic waveform | Measuring distortion of an amplifier |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Command | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | Standard sinusoidal stimulus | V, rad/s |
| 2 | $f = \frac{1}{T}$ | Frequency ↔ Period relationship | Hz, s |
| 3 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular frequency | rad/s |
| 4 | $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Capacitive reactance | Ω |
| 5 | $X_L = 2\pi f L$ | Inductive reactance | Ω |
| 6 | $Z = R + j(X_L - X_C)$ | Complex impedance | Ω |
| 7 | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ (RC low-pass) | Cutoff frequency (-3 dB) | Hz |
| 8 | $f_c = \frac{R}{2\pi L}$ (RL high-pass) | Cutoff frequency | Hz |
| 9 | $\tau = RC$ | Time constant of RC circuit | s |
| 10 | $v(t) = V_{final} + (V_{initial} - V_{final})e^{-t/\tau}$ | Universal exponential charging/discharging law | V |
| 11 | $\mathbf{i} = \mathbf{Y} \mathbf{v}$ | Nodal admittance form of Ohm's Law | A, S, V |
| 12 | $\mathbf{G} \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{I}$ | MNA matrix equation (resistive part) | — |
| 13 | $V_T = \frac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85 \text{ mV}$ | Thermal voltage at 300 K | V |
| 14 | $I_D = I_S \left( e^{V_D / (nV_T)} - 1 \right)$ | Shockley diode equation | A |
| 15 | $\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Total Harmonic Distortion | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Note — Sign Convention:** LTspice and PSpice define positive current as flowing **into** the positive terminal of a component (the *passive sign convention*). Multisim and Simulink may use the *opposite convention* in some blocks. Always verify the arrow direction on current probes during your lab record.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Analog IC Design:** Every op-amp and ADC on the market was first verified in SPICE before tape-out. A single fabrication run of a modern IC costs over **$1 million (₹8+ crore)** — making SPICE an indispensable cost-saver.
- **Power Electronics:** Engineers simulate SMPS (Switched-Mode Power Supplies) using transient analysis with non-linear MOSFET models.
- **PCB Design:** LTspice and PSpice integrate with **Altium Designer** and **OrCAD** for board-level co-simulation.
- **Control Systems:** MATLAB/Simulink is the *de facto* standard for simulating closed-loop control of electrical drives.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical Worked Example: Simulating an RC Low-Pass Filter in LTspice / PSpice

Consider the circuit below — a series resistor $R = 1 \ \text{k}\Omega$ and capacitor $C = 1 \ \mu\text{F}$, driven by a sinusoidal source $V_{in} = 10 \sin(2\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot t)$ V. The output is taken across the capacitor.

**Theoretical prediction of cutoff frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_c &= \frac{1}{2\pi R C} \\
&= \frac{1}{2 \pi \cdot (1 \times 10^3) \cdot (1 \times 10^{-6})} \\
&= \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{1000}{2 \pi} \\
&\approx 159.155 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Transfer Function (Why Simulation Must Match This)

Using the voltage divider rule in the **s-domain (Laplace)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(s) &= \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1/sC}{R + 1/sC} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 + sRC} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 + s\tau}, \quad \text{where } \tau = RC
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $s = j\omega$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(j\omega) &= \frac{1}{1 + j\omega R C} \\
\vert H(j\omega) \vert &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega R C)^2}} \\
\angle H(j\omega) &= -\arctan(\omega R C)
\end{aligned}
$$

At cutoff, $\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (the famous **−3 dB point**):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c R C)^2}} \\
\Rightarrow \quad 1 + (\omega_c R C)^2 &= 2 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \omega_c R C &= 1 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \omega_c &= \frac{1}{RC} \\
\Rightarrow \quad f_c &= \frac{1}{2 \pi R C}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **derivation is exactly what SPICE numerically solves** when you click "Run."

### 3.3 Constructing the MNA Matrix by Hand (for a 2-Node Resistive Network)

**Circuit:** Node 1 connected to ground via $R_1 = 1 \ \text{k}\Omega$; node 1 connected to node 2 via $R_2 = 2 \ \text{k}\Omega$; node 2 driven by a 10 V DC source through $R_3 = 3 \ \text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 1 — Identify the nodes:** ground (0), node 1, node 2.

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at each non-reference node:**

At node 1:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_1 - 0}{R_1} + \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_2} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

At node 2:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_2 - V_1}{R_2} + \frac{V_2 - 10}{R_3} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into the standard $\mathbf{Gv = I}$ form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \right) V_1 - \frac{1}{R_2} V_2 &= 0 \\
-\frac{1}{R_2} V_1 + \left( \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3} \right) V_2 &= \frac{10}{R_3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values (S = siemens):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left( 10^{-3} + 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \right) V_1 - 0.5 \times 10^{-3} V_2 &= 0 \\
-0.5 \times 10^{-3} V_1 + \left( 0.5 \times 10^{-3} + 0.333 \times 10^{-3} \right) V_2 &= 3.333 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Express as the MNA matrix equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
1.5 & -0.5 \\
-0.5 & 0.833
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
V_1 \\
V_2
\end{bmatrix}
&=
\begin{bmatrix}
0 \\
3.333
\end{bmatrix} \quad (\text{all values in mS, mA, V})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve (e.g., Cramer's Rule or Gaussian Elimination — exactly what SPICE does internally via LU decomposition):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= (1.5)(0.833) - (-0.5)(-0.5) = 1.2495 - 0.25 = 0.9995 \\
V_2 &= \frac{(1.5)(3.333) - (-0.5)(0)}{0.9995} = \frac{5.0}{0.9995} \approx 5.0025 \text{ V} \\
V_1 &= \frac{(0.833)(0) - (-0.5)(3.333)}{0.9995} = \frac{1.6667}{0.9995} \approx 1.6675 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lab Validation:** When you draw this exact circuit in LTspice and run **`.op`**, the displayed values of $V(1)$ and $V(2)$ will be **1.667 V and 5.003 V** — matching the hand-solved MNA result to within SPICE's default numerical tolerance of **1e-6**. This proves that SPICE *literally* solves the matrix you just wrote on paper.

### 3.4 SPICE Netlist for the Same Circuit (What the Software Internally Sees)

```
* RC Low-Pass Filter — Netlist form
V1  2  0  SIN(0 10 1k)        ; sine source at node 2, ref at node 0
R1  1  0  1k                  ; R1 between node 1 and ground
R2  1  2  2k                  ; R2 between node 1 and node 2
C1  1  0  1u                  ; C1 from node 1 to ground
.TRAN 0.1m 5m                 ; transient from 0 to 5 ms, step 0.1 ms
.PROBE                        ; ask for all node voltages
.END
```

When you draw this **graphically** in LTspice/PSpice/Multisim, the software auto-generates this exact netlist behind the scenes.

### 3.5 Transient Charging Verification — Hand vs. SPICE

For a DC step of $V_s = 5 \text{ V}$ applied to the same RC network (no source resistor ideal), the capacitor voltage at any time is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_C(t) = V_s \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right) = 5 \left( 1 - e^{-t/(1 \text{ ms})} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

| Time $t$ | $t / \tau$ | $v_C$ (Hand) | $v_C$ (SPICE `.TRAN`) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | **0.000 V** | 0.000 V |
| $\tau = 1$ ms | 1 | **3.160 V** | 3.160 V |
| $3\tau = 3$ ms | 3 | **4.751 V** | 4.751 V |
| $5\tau = 5$ ms | 5 | **4.966 V** | 4.966 V |
| $\infty$ | $\infty$ | **5.000 V** | 5.000 V |

This table is the **ground-truth check** every KTU lab examiner expects you to place in your record when validating a simulation experiment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Workflow Diagram — The Simulation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Problem Statement] --> B[Identify Circuit Topology]
    B --> C[Schematic Capture in GUI]
    C --> D[Component Selection: R L C Sources]
    D --> E[Assign Node Numbers and Values]
    E --> F[Auto Netlist Generation by Software]
    F --> G[Choose Analysis Type: OP / DC / AC / TRAN]
    G --> H[Solver Builds MNA Matrix Gx = b]
    H --> I[Numerical Solve: LU Decomposition or Newton Raphson]
    I --> J{Convergence Achieved?}
    J -- No --> K[Adjust Step Size or Model Parameters]
    K --> G
    J -- Yes --> L[Post Processor: Plot Probes and Markers]
    L --> M[Compare with Hand Calculation]
    M --> N[Conclude: Lab Report and Viva]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of a Generic Circuit Simulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT STAGE"]
        A1[Schematic Editor GUI]
        A2[Netlist Parser]
        A1 --> A2
    end
    subgraph CORE["SOLVER ENGINE"]
        B1[MNA Matrix Builder]
        B2[Model Library: R L C Diode BJT MOSFET]
        B3[Numerical Solver: Newton Raphson]
        B1 --> B3
        B2 --> B3
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["POST PROCESSING"]
        C1[Waveform Viewer]
        C2[Fourier / THD Calculator]
        C3[Probe and Cursor Tools]
        C1 --> C2
        C1 --> C3
    end
    A2 --> B1
    B3 --> C1
```

### 4.3 Comparison Block — Tool Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to Simulate Circuit?] --> Q1{Budget Available?}
    Q1 -- No --> Free[Use LTspice: Free Full Version]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Type of Analysis}
    Q2 -- Analog Mixed Signal --> PSp[PSpice: Industry Standard]
    Q2 -- Control System with Electrical --> Mat[MATLAB Simulink: Block Diagram]
    Q2 -- Teaching and Lab Demonstration --> Mul[Multisim: Educational Friendly]
    Q2 -- Power Electronics and SMPS --> LT[LTspice: Fast Switching Solver]
```

### 4.4 RC Low-Pass Filter — Schematic to be Simulated

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vin[Vin: Sine Source 10V 1kHz] --> Rnode[Node 1]
    Rnode --> R1[R = 1 kilo ohm]
    R1 --> Cnode[Node 2: Vout]
    Cnode --> C1[C = 1 micro F]
    C1 --> Gnd[Ground]
    Cnode -.Probe.-> Probe[Oscilloscope Vout]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define circuit simulation. List any four software platforms used for circuit simulation and state the common algorithmic kernel on which they are based.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Circuit simulation is the computational technique of modeling and analyzing the behavior of an electrical or electronic circuit using specialized software, without the need for physical construction.

**Four platforms:** (i) PSpice, (ii) LTspice, (iii) MATLAB/Simulink, (iv) Multisim.

**Common kernel:** All four tools are derived from or compatible with **SPICE (Simulation Program with Integrated Circuit Emphasis)**, originally developed at the **University of California, Berkeley (1973)**. Internally, they all use **Modified Nodal Analysis (MNA)** to formulate the circuit equations and solve them numerically using techniques such as **Newton-Raphson iteration** for nonlinear devices and **LU decomposition** for the resulting sparse matrix.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a SPICE netlist? Mention any four elements that can be described in a netlist with one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **SPICE netlist** is a plain-text, line-by-line description of a circuit's topology. It lists each component, the nodes it connects to, and its value. The netlist is the machine-readable form of the schematic and is the native input file format for LTspice, PSpice, and most SPICE-compatible simulators.

**Four element types and examples:**

1. **Resistor:** `R1 1 0 1k` — resistor `R1` between node 1 and ground, value 1 kΩ.
2. **Capacitor:** `C1 2 0 1u` — capacitor `C1` between node 2 and ground, value 1 µF.
3. **Inductor:** `L1 3 4 10m` — inductor `L1` between node 3 and node 4, value 10 mH.
4. **Independent Voltage Source:** `V1 1 0 DC 5` — 5 V DC source at node 1 referenced to ground.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the general workflow of circuit simulation using any SPICE-based tool such as LTspice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the RC low-pass filter shown below, calculate the cutoff frequency theoretically, and verify the result by writing a SPICE netlist and stating the expected AC analysis output. Take $R = 4.7 \ \text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 10 \ \text{nF}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Workflow of circuit simulation (7 marks):**

The simulation workflow consists of the following sequential steps, identical across PSpice, LTspice, Multisim, and Simulink (electrical blocks):

1. **Problem Statement & Schematic Capture:** The user draws the circuit on a schematic editor by placing components (R, L, C, sources) and wiring them.
2. **Component Value Assignment:** Each component is assigned a value (e.g., R = 1 kΩ, C = 1 µF) and a model (e.g., ideal, 2N2222 BJT).
3. **Netlist Generation:** The software automatically converts the schematic into a netlist text file.
4. **Analysis Type Selection:** The user selects DC Operating Point, DC Sweep, AC Analysis, or Transient Analysis.
5. **Simulation Engine:** The solver builds the MNA matrix and solves it numerically.
6. **Post-Processing:** The waveform viewer displays node voltages, branch currents, and frequency responses. Probes and cursors are used to read off values.
7. **Validation:** The simulation results are compared with hand calculations or expected theoretical values.

**(b) RC Low-Pass Filter Cutoff Frequency (7 marks):**

**Theoretical calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_c &= \frac{1}{2 \pi R C} \\
&= \frac{1}{2 \pi \cdot (4.7 \times 10^3) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-9})} \\
&= \frac{1}{2 \pi \cdot 4.7 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2.9531 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&\approx 3386.27 \text{ Hz} \approx \mathbf{3.39 \ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**SPICE Netlist:**

```
* RC Low-Pass Filter
Vin  1  0  AC 1  SIN(0 1 1k)
R1   1  2  4.7k
C1   2  0  10n
.AC  DEC  100  10  100k
.PROBE
.END
```

**Expected AC Analysis Output:**
At $f = 3.386$ kHz, the magnitude of $V_{out}$ will be exactly **−3 dB** (= 0.7071) of its DC value, and the phase will be exactly **−45°**.

**Transfer function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC} = \frac{1}{1 + 4.7 \times 10^{-5} \cdot s}
\end{aligned}
$$

The Bode plot will show a flat response of 0 dB up to ~3.39 kHz, then a roll-off at **−20 dB/decade**.

---

**Q3. Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Compare PSpice, LTspice, MATLAB/Simulink, and Multisim based on **(i) license type, (ii) best-suited application area, (iii) input format, and (iv) solver engine**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A series RLC circuit has $R = 10 \ \Omega$, $L = 100 \ \text{mH}$, and $C = 10 \ \mu\text{F}$. Calculate the resonant frequency and quality factor theoretically. Write the SPICE netlist to plot its AC frequency response and describe the expected output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Feature | PSpice | LTspice | MATLAB/Simulink | Multisim |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **License** | Commercial (Student version free) | **Freeware** | Commercial (Student version) | Commercial (Educational edition) |
| **Best Application** | Analog & mixed-signal IC design | Switched-mode power supplies, fast transient sim | Control systems, signal processing, electrical drives | Undergraduate teaching labs, instrumentation |
| **Input Format** | Schematic + `.cir` netlist | Schematic + `.asc` / `.net` | Block diagram (`.slx`, `.mdl`) | Schematic + netlist |
| **Solver Engine** | SPICE 3 / XSPICE | SPICE-based proprietary (fast convergence) | ODE solvers (ode45, ode23tb) + Simscape | XSPICE-based |

**(b) Series RLC Resonance (7 marks):**

**Theoretical calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_0 &= \frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{LC}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{(0.1)(10 \times 10^{-6})}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{10^{-6}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{1000}{2 \pi} \\
&\approx \mathbf{159.15 \ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Quality factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{1}{R} \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{1}{10} \sqrt{\frac{0.1}{10 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{10} \sqrt{10^4} = \frac{100}{10} = \mathbf{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Bandwidth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BW} = \frac{f_0}{Q} = \frac{159.15}{10} \approx 15.92 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**SPICE Netlist:**

```
* Series RLC Resonant Circuit
Vin  1  0  AC 1
R1   1  2  10
L1   2  3  100m
C1   3  0  10u
.AC  LIN  1000  100  300
.PROBE
.END
```

**Expected Output:** The magnitude plot of $I$ versus frequency will show a sharp peak at **159.15 Hz** with a 3 dB bandwidth of approximately **15.92 Hz** (cutoff frequencies near 151.2 Hz and 167.1 Hz).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing DC Operating Point (`.op`) with DC Sweep (`.dc`):** The `.op` directive computes bias at *one* point only; `.dc` sweeps over a range. Many students report a "sweep" using `.op` and lose full 3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting ground reference (node 0):** SPICE *will not* simulate without a designated ground node (labeled `0`). LTspice guesses; PSpice refuses to run.
> 3. **Wrong current sign convention:** In SPICE, current flows **into** the positive terminal of a component. Students often write down a current with the wrong sign in their record and lose method marks.
> 4. **Unit confusion:** `1u` = 1 µF (micro) in SPICE, but `M` = milli (NOT mega) in PSpice/LTspice. Use `MEG` for mega. This is a **famous examiner trap**.
> 5. **No convergence message ignored:** If the simulator says "Time step too small" or "Singular matrix," the result is **invalid**. Always report the convergence status in your lab record.
> 6. **Mixing up AC and Transient sources:** `SIN(0 10 1k)` is a **transient** source used with `.TRAN`. For `.AC`, use `AC 1` with a separate frequency range.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Circuit simulation** is a *virtual breadboard* that uses **Modified Nodal Analysis (MNA)** to solve the circuit mathematically.
- The four KTU-prescribed tools are **PSpice, LTspice, MATLAB/Simulink, and Multisim**.
- All four trace their lineage to **Berkeley SPICE (1973)**.
- The **netlist** is the machine-readable text form of the schematic.
- **Five essential analyses:** `.OP`, `.DC`, `.AC`, `.TRAN`, `.FOUR`.
- **Key formulas** to memorize: $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$, $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$, $Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$, $v(t) = V_f + (V_i - V_f)e^{-t/\tau}$.
- **Thermal voltage** $V_T \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K — used in all SPICE semiconductor models.
- **In SPICE netlists:** `M` = milli, `MEG` = mega, `u` = micro, `n` = nano, `p` = pico.
- **Always** include a `.PROBE` (PSpice) or probe markers (LTspice) to view waveforms.
- **Always** validate simulation results against at least **one hand calculation** in your lab record — this is what examiners look for.
- **Convergence errors** = invalid results; reduce step size or relax tolerances.
- **LTspice is free and is the de facto choice** for most KTU student simulations; PSpice student version and Multisim educational edition are also acceptable.
- **MATLAB/Simulink** is preferred when the circuit is *part of a larger control system* (e.g., motor drive, PLL).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation of IoT-based data acquisition system
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL307 - CIRCUITS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Implementation of IoT-based data acquisition system # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **IoT-based Data Acquisition System (IoT-DAQ)** is an integrated measurement architecture in which physical analog signals from the real world are sensed, conditioned, digitized, processed by a microcontroller, and transmitted over an Internet Protocol (IP) network to a remote cloud server or dashboard for storage, visualization, and analysis. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCEEL307 - Circuits and Measurements Lab)**, the experiment requires the student to build a working prototype that: 1. Interfacing at least one analog sensor (e.g., **LM35** temperature sensor, **LDR**, or a voltage/current transducer) to the **ADC** pin of a Wi-Fi enabled microcontroller (commonly **NodeMCU ESP8266** or **ESP32**). 2. Reading the digital value, converting it to engineering units, and publishing the data using an application-layer protocol such as **MQTT** or **HTTP** to a free cloud platform like **ThingSpeak**, **Blynk**, or **Firebase**. 3. Verifying the remote reception and graphical plotting of the same on a web dashboard. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEEL307 - Module 2):** > The experiment tests the practical integration of three foundational measurement concepts: (i) **transduction** (sensor to voltage), (ii) **signal conditioning** (amplification / level shifting), and (iii) **digitization + telemetry** (ADC + Wi-Fi transmission). All three must be demonstrated live during the continuous assessment viva. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Nervous System" Imagine the human body. Your **skin** is a sensor that detects heat, cold, and pressure. Your **spinal cord** amplifies and conditions the tiny nerve signals. Your **brain** digitizes and processes them. Finally, your **voice** (or smartphone) transmits a simplified message to a friend in another city. An IoT-DAQ works exactly the same way: | Human Body Part | IoT-DAQ Equivalent | Function | |---|---|---| | Skin / Eyes | **Sensor** (LM35, LDR, DHT11) | Converts physical quantity to voltage | | Spinal Cord | **Signal Conditioning Circuit** (Op-Amp, Voltage Divider) | Amplifies/levels the weak signal | | Brain | **Microcontroller (ESP32/NodeMCU)** | ADC + Processing + Decision | | Mouth / Phone | **Wi-Fi Module + MQTT/HTTP** | Transmits the data to the cloud | | Friend's Ears | **Cloud Dashboard** | Receives, stores, plots, and alarms | > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Definition:** > An IoT-DAQ is simply a "smart digital thermometer/voltmeter that you can read from your mobile phone, sitting anywhere in the world, as long as the device has Wi-Fi." ## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standardized) The following metrics are universally used to grade any DAQ system and are essential for the KTU viva: - **Accuracy:** Closeness of reading to true value. Unit: **% of Full Scale (%FS)**. - **Resolution:** Smallest detectable change. For a 10-bit ADC with 3.3 V reference, it is **3.3 V / 1023 ≈ 3.22 mV**. - **Sampling Rate (f_s):** Number of samples acquired per second. Unit: **Samples per Second (SPS)**. - **Quantization Error:** Theoretical maximum error of ±½ LSB. - **Latency:** Time delay between physical event and dashboard update. Unit: **milliseconds (ms)**. - **Power Consumption:** Critical for battery-operated nodes. Unit: **milliwatts (mW)** or **milliampere-hours (mAh)**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Block Architecture A complete IoT-DAQ system operates in **five sequential stages**. Understanding each stage is the single most important requirement to score full marks in the KTU lab record. ### Stage 1 — The Transducer (Sensor) The sensor converts a non-electrical physical quantity (temperature, light, humidity) into a proportional **analog voltage, current, or resistance**. ### Stage 2 — Signal Conditioning Most microcontrollers accept **0 V to 3.3 V**. A raw sensor output (e.g., a thermocouple giving µV, or a 0–10 V industrial transducer) must be: - **Amplified** to match the ADC input range. - **Level-shifted** so that the minimum sensor output maps to **0 V**. - **Filtered** (low-pass RC filter) to remove 50 Hz mains noise. ### Stage 3 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) The conditioned analog voltage is fed to the on-chip **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** of the ESP32/NodeMCU. - ESP8266 has a single **10-bit ADC** (input range: **0 – 1.0 V** internally; external divider required for 3.3 V). - ESP32 has two **12-bit SAR ADCs** (input range: **0 – 3.3 V**). ### Stage 4 — Embedded Processing The digital count is converted into engineering units using a **linear transfer function**: $$y = mx + c$$ where *x* is the ADC count, *m* is the slope, and *c* is the offset. The firmware packages this value into a JSON string or MQTT payload. ### Stage 5 — Wireless Telemetry The microcontroller connects to a **2.4 GHz Wi-Fi router**, obtains an IP via **DHCP**, opens a **TCP socket** to the cloud broker (e.g., ThingSpeak), and posts the data using **HTTP GET/POST** or **MQTT Publish**. > [!TIP] > **Real-World Utility:** The same architecture is used in **smart energy meters** (Kerala KSEB prepaid meters), **patient monitoring systems** in ICUs, **cold-chain logistics** for vaccine transport, and **industrial SCADA** systems for remote plant monitoring. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table contains every equation you are likely to need for the KTU 2024 ESE question on this experiment. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **ADC Resolution** | $V_{res} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{N} - 1}$ | $V_{res}$ = step size, $V_{ref}$ = reference, $N$ = bits | **Volts (V)** | | 2 | **Digital Count** | $D = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{res}}\right)$ | $D$ = output code, $V_{in}$ = input voltage | Dimensionless | | 3 | **Quantization Noise RMS** | $V_{Q,rms} = \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}}$ | Theoretical RMS noise floor | **Volts (V)** | | 4 | **Theoretical SNR** | $\text{SNR} = 6.02N + 1.76$ | $N$ = ADC bit resolution | **Decibels (dB)** | | 5 | **Sensor Transfer (LM35)** | $T \left(^{\circ}C\right) = \dfrac{V_{out}}{10\,mV}$ | Linear, 0 °C → 0 V, 150 °C → 1.5 V | **°C** | | 6 | **Voltage Divider (LDR)** | $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \dfrac{R_{LDR}}{R_{LDR} + R_{fixed}}$ | $R_{LDR}$ varies with light | **Volts (V)** | | 7 | **Nyquist Sampling** | $f_{s} \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | To avoid aliasing of the measurand | **Hertz (Hz)** | | 8 | **MQTT Payload Size** | $P_{bytes} = L_{topic} + 2 + L_{payload}$ | Topic + length byte + payload | **Bytes** | | 9 | **NodeMCU ADC Input** | $V_{in} = \dfrac{D}{1024} \cdot 3.3\,V$ | 10-bit ADC, 3.3 V supply | **Volts (V)** | | 10 | **Power Consumption** | $P = V_{cc} \cdot I_{avg}$ | Average current during 1 cycle | **Watts (W)** | | 11 | **Percentage Error** | $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{\vert M - T \vert}{T} \times 100$ | $M$ = measured, $T$ = true | **%** | | 12 | **Standard Deviation (Repeatability)** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{N-1}}$ | Used to report DAQ precision | Same as measurand | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Tables):** When writing the voltage divider equation in your lab record, never write `|R_LDR|` with the vertical pipe character — it breaks the markdown table layout. Use the word "magnitude of" or write it as `R_LDR` without the absolute-value bars. ## 2.3 Why Each Step Exists — The "Why & How" Logic - **Why condition the signal?** Because a 10-bit ADC with a 3.3 V reference has a 3.22 mV step. A raw thermocouple outputs microvolts — it would simply read zero. Amplification is **mandatory**. - **Why use 12-bit over 10-bit?** Because **6 dB of SNR is gained per extra bit**. A 12-bit ADC has 4× finer resolution than a 10-bit ADC for the same reference voltage. - **Why Wi-Fi and not Bluetooth?** Because IoT explicitly requires an **IP-addressable** device reachable over the public internet. Bluetooth is personal-area only. - **Why MQTT over HTTP?** Because MQTT is a **publish/subscribe** protocol with a 2-byte fixed header, ideal for low-bandwidth, battery-powered nodes. HTTP carries ~200 bytes of overhead per request. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU board examiner frequently asks: *"With a 3.3 V reference and 10-bit ADC, what is the smallest voltage change detectable on an ESP8266?"* The full derivation below is the model answer. ## 3.1 Derivation: ADC Resolution and Quantization **Given:** - Reference voltage of the ESP8266, $V_{ref}$ = **3.3 V** - Number of bits, $N$ = **10** **Step 1 — Write the fundamental resolution formula.** The number of discrete output codes for an N-bit ADC is $2^{N}$. $$N_{codes} = 2^{10} = 1024$$ **Step 2 — Determine the actual code range.** The codes run from 0 to $2^{N} - 1$, so the **total number of intervals** is $2^{N} - 1$. $$N_{intervals} = 2^{10} - 1 = 1023$$ **Step 3 — Compute the step size (LSB voltage).** Each Least Significant Bit (LSB) corresponds to one interval. $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{N} - 1} = \frac{3.3\,V}{1023}$$ $$V_{LSB} \approx 3.226 \times 10^{-3}\,V = 3.226\,mV$$ **Step 4 — Convert to microvolts for intuitive reporting.** $$V_{LSB} = 3.226\,mV = 3226\,\mu V$$ > **Conclusion (model answer for KTU):** > "The smallest voltage change detectable on the NodeMCU ESP8266 is **3.226 mV (≈ 3226 µV)**." ## 3.2 Derivation: LM35 Temperature Reading (Worked Numerical) **Given:** LM35 output $V_{out}$ = **0.425 V**, 10-bit ADC, 3.3 V reference, voltage divider used so the input pin reads 0–1.0 V scaled to 0–3.3 V (assume scaling factor $K = 0.303$). **Step 1 — Read the ADC count.** The NodeMCU ADC actually reads 0–1.0 V, so the actual pin voltage is $V_{pin} = V_{out} \times K$ — but for this derivation we directly use the formula $D = (V_{out}/V_{ref}) \times 1023$ after calibration. $$D = \frac{0.425}{3.3} \times 1023$$ $$D = 0.12879 \times 1023$$ $$D \approx 131.75 \rightarrow 132 \text{ (rounded)}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the LM35 transfer equation.** The LM35 data sheet specifies **10 mV per °C**. $$T = \frac{V_{out}}{10\,mV} = \frac{0.425\,V}{0.010\,V/^{\circ}C}$$ $$T = 42.5\,^{\circ}C$$ **Step 3 — Compute the percentage error** if a mercury thermometer reads $T_{true}$ = 43.0 °C. $$\%\text{Error} = \frac{|42.5 - 43.0|}{43.0} \times 100$$ $$\%\text{Error} = \frac{0.5}{43.0} \times 100 \approx 1.16\%$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Sampling Theorem Boundary **Given:** A temperature signal in a room changes no faster than 0.1 °C/sec. The relevant "frequency content" can be approximated as $f_{max}$ ≈ **0.5 Hz** (5× the rate of change rule of thumb). **Step 1 — Apply Nyquist criterion.** $$f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max}$$ $$f_s \geq 2 \times 0.5\,Hz = 1\,Hz$$ **Step 2 — Choose a safe engineering factor (5× oversampling).** $$f_{s,design} = 5 \times 1\,Hz = 5\,SPS$$ > **Conclusion:** A sampling interval of **200 ms (5 samples per second)** is more than sufficient for room-temperature IoT-DAQ, leaving the ESP32 mostly in **light-sleep mode** to save power. ## 3.4 Derivation: Voltage Divider for LDR Light Sensor **Given:** LDR resistance $R_{LDR}$ = **10 kΩ** under normal room light, fixed resistor $R$ = **10 kΩ**, $V_{cc}$ = **3.3 V**. **Step 1 — Write the standard voltage divider.** $$V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_{LDR}}{R_{LDR} + R}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the values.** $$V_{out} = 3.3 \cdot \frac{10\,k\Omega}{10\,k\Omega + 10\,k\Omega}$$ $$V_{out} = 3.3 \cdot 0.5 = 1.65\,V$$ **Step 3 — Convert to ADC count.** $$D = \frac{1.65}{3.3} \times 1023 = 511.5 \rightarrow 512$$ > **Conclusion:** Mid-scale illumination (LDR = 10 kΩ) yields exactly half-scale ADC count (**512 out of 1023**), as expected from a balanced Wheatstone-type divider. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Communication Protocol | Parameter | HTTP (REST) | MQTT | LoRaWAN | Bluetooth BLE | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Header Overhead** | ~200 bytes | **2 bytes (fixed)** | ~12 bytes | ~10 bytes | | **Range** | Wi-Fi coverage (~30 m) | Wi-Fi coverage | **Up to 10 km** | ~10 m | | **Power** | High | **Low** | Very Low | Low | | **Topology** | Client-Server | **Pub/Sub** | Star | P2P / Mesh | | **Cloud Ready** | Yes | **Yes (designed for it)** | Via Gateway | Needs phone | | **Best For (IoT-DAQ)** | Infrequent POSTs | **High-frequency telemetry** | Field/agri sensors | Wearables | > **KTU One-Liner (memorize):** *"For a room-lab IoT-DAQ, MQTT over Wi-Fi is the most exam-defensible choice because it offers the lowest overhead, native pub-sub semantics, and direct cloud-broker integration."* # 4.
