KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Q1.a, 3 Marks]** **Q1.** Define an **IoT-based Data Acquisition System**. List its **five functional blocks** with one-line descriptions of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** An IoT-based Data Acquisition System is an embedded measurement architecture that senses physical quantities, converts them to digital form, and transmits the digitized values over the Internet for remote storage and visualization. The five functional blocks are: 1. **Sensor / Transducer** — Converts physical quantity (heat, light) to an electrical signal. 2. **Signal Conditioning Circuit** — Amplifies, filters, and level-shifts the raw signal to match the ADC range (typically 0 – 3.3 V). 3. **ADC + Microcontroller** — Digitizes the signal and applies a transfer function to obtain engineering units. 4. **Wireless Communication Module** — Packages the data and transmits over Wi-Fi/MQTT/HTTP. 5. **Cloud Server / Dashboard** — Receives, time-stamps, stores, and graphically plots the data for the end user. > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q1.b, 3 Marks]** **Q2.** With a **3.3 V reference** and a **10-bit ADC**, calculate the **resolution in mV**. Justify why the NodeMCU ESP8266 cannot directly read a 5 V signal. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Resolution calculation:** $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{N}-1} = \frac{3.3}{1023} \approx 3.226\,mV$$ **Why 5 V cannot be read directly:** The NodeMCU ESP8266 ADC pin is internally scaled to a **0 – 1.0 V** range and is powered by a 3.3 V regulator. Feeding 5 V into the ADC pin will permanently damage the chip and cause an out-of-range reading. A **resistive voltage divider** (R1 = 2 × R2) must be used to scale 5 V down to 3.3 V. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Module 2, ESE Pattern, 14 Marks with Internal Choice]** ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Design-Oriented **(a)** Design an **IoT-based temperature monitoring system** for a server room. Specify the sensor, microcontroller, and cloud platform. Draw the block diagram and explain the role of **signal conditioning**. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** With a **12-bit ADC** and **3.3 V reference**, compute the resolution, quantization noise RMS, and theoretical SNR. If the sensor output is **0.725 V**, find the digital count and equivalent temperature in °C for an LM35. **[7 Marks]** ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Analysis-Oriented (Internal Choice) **(a)** Compare **MQTT and HTTP** as application-layer protocols for IoT-DAQ. Justify which one is preferred for a **battery-powered** sensor node transmitting every 5 seconds. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** An LDR-based light sensor is interfaced to a NodeMCU through a voltage divider with **R_fixed = 4.7 kΩ** and **V_cc = 3.3 V**. The ADC reads **770**. Find the LDR resistance, the illuminance ratio (assuming linear LDR), and explain what the cloud dashboard would display. **[7 Marks]** --- ## 5.3 Model Solutions to Part B (Question A) ### Part (a) — System Design (7 Marks) | # | Design Element | Selected Component | Justification | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Temperature Sensor** | **LM35** | 10 mV/°C linear output, 0–150 °C range, no calibration needed, low cost (₹40) | | 2 | **Microcontroller** | **NodeMCU ESP8266** | On-board Wi-Fi, 10-bit ADC, 4 MB flash, Arduino IDE support | | 3 | **Cloud Platform** | **ThingSpeak** | Free tier (8 channels, 1 update/15 s), MATLAB visualisations built-in | | 4 | **Signal Conditioning** | **Op-Amp LM358** (Non-inverting, gain = 11) | Boosts 0–1.5 V LM35 output to 0–3.3 V full ADC range | | 5 | **Display (Optional)** | **0.96" OLED (I2C)** | Local read-out, SSD1306 driver | **Role of Signal Conditioning (model 2-mark sub-answer):** Signal conditioning ensures the LM35's 0 – 1.5 V output spans the **entire** 0 – 3.3 V ADC range. Without amplification, the resolution of the temperature reading is only **1.5 V / 1023 ≈ 1.47 mV/LSB** instead of the full **3.226 mV/LSB**, effectively wasting 2 bits of dynamic range. ### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Resolution for 12-bit, 3.3 V reference:** $$V_{LSB} = \frac{3.3}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{4095} \approx 0.000806\,V = 0.806\,mV$$ **Step 2 — Quantization noise RMS:** $$V_{Q,rms} = \frac{V_{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{0.806\,mV}{3.464}$$ $$V_{Q,rms} \approx 0.233\,mV$$ **Step 3 — Theoretical SNR:** $$\text{SNR} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00\,dB$$ **Step 4 — Digital count for V_in = 0.725 V:** $$D = \frac{0.725}{0.000806} \approx 899.5 \rightarrow 900$$ **Step 5 — Temperature using LM35 transfer (10 mV/°C):** $$T = \frac{0.725\,V}{0.010\,V/^{\circ}C} = 72.5\,^{\circ}C$$ > **Conclusion (model 1-mark closing line):** A 12-bit ADC gives a finer resolution of **0.806 mV** (≈ 4× better than 10-bit) and a theoretical SNR of **74 dB**, which is well above the 40 dB noise floor of typical industrial environments. --- ## 5.4 Model Solutions to Part B (Question B — Internal Choice) ### Part (a) — MQTT vs HTTP Comparison (7 Marks) | Criterion | HTTP (REST) | **MQTT** | |---|---|---| | **Architecture** | Request–Response (client pulls) | **Publish–Subscribe (broker pushes)** | | **Header Size** | ~200 bytes | **2-byte fixed header** | | **Power Efficiency** | High (TCP handshake every time) | **Low (persistent TCP connection)** | | **Bandwidth** | Wasteful for small packets | **Optimized for small IoT payloads** | | **Server Load** | High (one connection per request) | **Low (single broker connection)** | | **Real-Time Push** | Needs polling or WebSocket | **Native (broker pushes to subscribers)** | | **QoS Levels** | None | **3 levels (0, 1, 2)** | | **Battery Life (Estimated)** | ~7 days on 2000 mAh | **~30 days on 2000 mAh** | **Verdict (2 marks):** For a battery-powered node sending **1 reading every 5 s**, **MQTT is preferred** because (i) the persistent connection avoids repeated TCP handshakes, (ii) the 2-byte header is ~100× smaller than HTTP, and (iii) the QoS = 0 level enables fire-and-forget transmission that suits non-critical telemetry. ### Part (b) — LDR Voltage Divider Numericals (7 Marks) **Given:** R_fixed = 4.7 kΩ, V_cc = 3.3 V, ADC count = 770, V_ref = 3.3 V, 10-bit ADC. **Step 1 — Reconstruct the voltage at the ADC pin:** $$V_{pin} = \frac{770}{1023} \times 3.3\,V = 0.7527 \times 3.3\,V \approx 2.484\,V$$ **Step 2 — Apply the voltage divider formula in reverse:** $$V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_{LDR}}{R_{LDR} + R_{fixed}}$$ $$2.484 = 3.3 \cdot \frac{R_{LDR}}{R_{LDR} + 4700}$$ $$\frac{2.484}{3.3} = \frac{R_{LDR}}{R_{LDR} + 4700}$$ $$0.7527 = \frac{R_{LDR}}{R_{LDR} + 4700}$$ **Step 3 — Cross-multiply and solve for R_LDR:** $$0.7527 \cdot (R_{LDR} + 4700) = R_{LDR}$$ $$0.7527 R_{LDR} + 3537.7 = R_{LDR}$$ $$3537.7 = R_{LDR} - 0.7527 R_{LDR}$$ $$3537.7 = 0.2473 \cdot R_{LDR}$$ $$R_{LDR} = \frac{3537.7}{0.2473} \approx 14307\,\Omega \approx 14.3\,k\Omega$$ **Step 4 — Compute the "illuminance ratio" assuming linear LDR** (with 10 kΩ as the bright reference): $$\text{Ratio} = \frac{14.3}{10} = 1.43$$ > **Conclusion (2 marks):** A **higher LDR resistance (14.3 kΩ) means lower light intensity** (because LDR resistance increases in the dark). The cloud dashboard will plot a value around **770/1023 ≈ 75 %** of full scale, which the user can interpret as "moderately dim lighting" if the mapping is set with 0 = bright sun, 1023 = total darkness. --- ## 5.5 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in PCEEL307 Module 2:** > 1. **Forgetting to divide by 1023** — Many students write $V = D \times 3.3$ instead of $V = (D/1023) \times 3.3$. This is an **instant 1-mark loss** and a marker of incomplete understanding. > 2. **Using ESP8266 ADC for 5 V signals** — The pin is **0 – 1 V internal**; failing to mention a voltage divider in the circuit diagram is considered a design error (**-2 marks**). > 3. **Confusing MQTT topic with HTTP URL** — Examiners specifically test whether the student understands that MQTT uses a **broker (broker.hivemq.com:1883)** and not an HTTP URL. > 4. **Ignoring the Nyquist criterion** — Reporting a sampling rate *lower* than 2 × signal bandwidth when asked to justify the choice of f_s will cost the full 2 marks allocated to that sub-part. > 5. **Writing `|x|` inside a markdown table** — Always use $\vert x \vert$ or `magnitude of x` to avoid breaking the table layout, which can lead to evaluator misreading. > 6. **No mention of "scaling factor"** for ESP8266 — The on-board ADC is scaled to 1.0 V; mention of the **1/3.3 resistive divider on the board** is required for full marks on a hardware question. --- ## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Definition (1 line):** An IoT-DAQ system is a sensor-plus-microcontroller-plus-cloud pipeline that digitizes physical phenomena and pushes them to a remote dashboard. - **Five blocks (in order):** Sensor → Signal Conditioner → ADC → Microcontroller → Wi-Fi → Cloud → Dashboard. - **Two most-used sensors in the KTU lab:** **LM35 (temperature, 10 mV/°C)** and **LDR (light, voltage divider)**. - **Two most-used boards:** **NodeMCU ESP8266 (10-bit ADC, 0–1 V pin)** and **ESP32 (12-bit ADC, 0–3.3 V pin)**. - **Two most-used clouds:** **ThingSpeak (HTTP-friendly, 15 s update limit)** and **MQTT broker (broker.hivemq.com / test.mosquitto.org on port 1883)**. - **Key formula #1 (MUST memorize):** $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{N} - 1}$ - **Key formula #2:** $\text{SNR (dB)} = 6.02N + 1.76$ - **Key formula #3 (LM35):** $T (^{\circ}C) = V_{out} / 0.01$ - **Key formula #4 (Voltage Divider):** $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot R_{LDR} / (R_{LDR} + R_{fixed})$ - **Nyquist rule:** Sample at **≥ 2×** the highest frequency in the measurand. For room temp, **5 SPS** is safe. - **MQTT vs HTTP (memorize the table above):** MQTT has 2-byte header, pub-sub, ideal for battery nodes. - **ESP8266 ADC caveat:** Internal scaling is **0–1 V**; you must use a **resistive divider** to read 0–3.3 V. - **ThingSpeak rate limit:** Minimum **15 seconds** between successive updates on the free tier. - **Quantization error bound:** **± ½ LSB** — always state this when discussing ADC accuracy. - **Reporting accuracy:** Always compute **% Error = |Measured − True| / True × 100** and compare against sensor datasheet tolerance. - **Power tip:** ESP8266 deep-sleep draws **~10 µA** vs active **~70 mA** — for battery IoT-DAQ, use **deep-sleep + wake-on-timer**. - **For the KTU record book, always include:** (i) Aim, (ii) Apparatus list, (iii) Circuit/Block diagram, (iv) Procedure, (v) Observation table, (vi) Sample calculation, (vii) Result with % error, (viii) Viva questions and answers. > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "implement" the system, do not just describe it — **draw the block diagram, write one paragraph of code logic, show one sample calculation with the 1023 divisor, and one ThingSpeak screenshot.** This is the **guaranteed 14/14** formula for PCEEL307 Module 2.

topics.title: Demo experiments: measurement of energy using TOD meter/digital meters/bidirectional meter; measurement of electrical variables and frequency using CRO and DSO; harmonic analysers; instrumentation systems for gas/fire/smoke detection systems; virtual instrumentation experiments using LABVIEW
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL307 - CIRCUITS AND MEASUREMENTS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Demo experiments: measurement of energy using TOD meter/digital meters/bidirectional meter; measurement of electrical variables and frequency using CRO and DSO; harmonic analysers; instrumentation systems for gas/fire/smoke detection systems; virtual instrumentation experiments using LABVIEW

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Smart Energy Meters (TOD / Digital / Bidirectional)

### TOD (Time-of-Day) Meter
A **Time-of-Day (TOD) energy meter** is an advanced electronic metering device that records energy consumption during different time periods (e.g., peak hours, normal hours, off-peak hours) of a day, applying different tariff rates for each slot. It enables utilities to incentivize consumers to shift loads to off-peak periods, flattening the demand curve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** A TOD meter is a multi-rate electronic energy meter employing an internal real-time clock (RTC) and memory registers to log cumulative energy consumption (kWh) and maximum demand (kW) partitioned across predefined time zones, with tariff switching driven by the RTC.

**Real-World Analogy:** Think of a toll plaza that charges ₹100 for cars entering between 8–10 AM (peak), but only ₹40 for the same car if it enters after 10 PM (off-peak). A TOD meter does the same thing for electricity — it charges more for the *same unit (kWh)* if you use it during peak hours.

### Digital Energy Meter
A **digital energy meter** is a static (electronic) meter that uses ADCs, microcontrollers/DSPs, and a shunt or CT/VT to sample voltage and current waveforms, computing real power, reactive power, and energy digitally. It replaces the electromechanical induction disc meter.

### Bidirectional (Net) Meter
A **bidirectional meter** measures energy flow in **both directions** — import (consumption from grid) and export (injection to grid, e.g., from rooftop solar PV). It is the cornerstone of net-metering for prosumer installations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 explicitly tests the difference between unidirectional electromechanical meters, TOD digital meters, and bidirectional net meters in the context of renewable integration.

---

## 1.2 CRO and DSO for Electrical Variables & Frequency

### Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO)
A **CRO** is an analog instrument that uses a focused electron beam striking a phosphor-coated CRT screen to display voltage waveforms as a function of time. It allows direct visual measurement of **amplitude (V)**, **period (T)**, and hence **frequency (f = 1/T)** and **phase**.

### Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO)
A **DSO** samples the input signal with a high-speed ADC, stores the digitized samples in memory, and reconstructs the waveform on an LCD. It offers superior features: **auto-measurement, FFT, persistence, triggering, and waveform storage**.

**Real-World Analogy:** A CRO is like writing on a blackboard with chalk — the picture exists only as long as the chalk dust glows. A DSO is like a digital camera that captures the same picture, stores it, and lets you zoom, replay, and run math on it forever.

---

## 1.3 Harmonic Analyser
A **harmonic analyser** is an instrument that decomposes a periodic, non-sinusoidal waveform into its fundamental and harmonic frequency components using **Fourier analysis (FFT)**. It quantifies **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** in power systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **THD Definition:** $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$

---

## 1.4 Gas / Fire / Smoke Detection Systems
These are **safety-critical instrumentation systems** that use physical/chemical sensors to detect hazardous conditions and trigger alarms or suppression systems. KTU 2024 treats them as standard transducer-signal-conditioning-actuator chains.

| Hazard | Primary Sensor | Working Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **Gas (LPG/CH₄/CO)** | Semiconductor / Electrochemical | Resistance change on gas adsorption |
| **Fire (Flame)** | UV / IR photodiode | Detection of flame-specific radiation |
| **Smoke** | Photoelectric / Ionization chamber | Light scattering or ion current change |

---

## 1.5 Virtual Instrumentation using LABVIEW
**Virtual Instrumentation (VI)** is a paradigm where traditional hardware instruments (oscilloscope, function generator, multimeter) are emulated in software on a PC, with I/O performed via DAQ (Data Acquisition) cards. **LABVIEW (Laboratory Virtual Instrument Engineering Workbench)** by National Instruments uses a graphical **G-programming (dataflow)** language instead of textual code.

**Real-World Analogy:** A traditional lab has 10 physical boxes (oscilloscope, counter, FFT analyzer, etc.). Virtual instrumentation replaces all 10 with one PC + DAQ card, where the "boxes" are just different *software windows* (VIs).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theory of Smart Energy Meter Operation

The fundamental energy equation integrated by all modern digital meters is:

$$E = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt \quad [\text{Wh}]$$

**Operational Steps:**
- **Step 1 — Sensing:** Voltage is sensed via a **potential divider / VT**; current via a **shunt / CT / Rogowski coil**.
- **Step 2 — Signal Conditioning:** The analog signals are scaled, anti-aliased filtered, and fed to the ADC.
- **Step 3 — Sampling & Quantization:** The ADC samples v(t) and i(t) at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist).
- **Step 4 — Power Computation:** The DSP/microcontroller computes **instantaneous power** $p(n) = v(n) \cdot i(n)$, averages it over a number of cycles, and integrates over time to yield energy.
- **Step 5 — Storage & Display:** The energy in kWh is stored in non-volatile memory and displayed on a 7-segment / LCD.
- **Step 6 — TOD Logic:** The RTC generates time-of-day tags; energy registers are split into multiple slots.
- **Step 7 — Bidirectional Logic:** The sign of instantaneous power determines whether energy is credited to the *Import* or *Export* register.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to differentiate digital vs. bidirectional meters, the keyword is **"sign of average power"** — a unidirectional meter only sums $|P|$, while a bidirectional meter tracks $\sum P$ with direction.

## 2.2 CRO / DSO Frequency Measurement

### CRO Method (Time Base Calibration)
The unknown signal $v(t)$ is connected to the Y-input, and the time/div knob is calibrated. Frequency is derived from:

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{N \cdot (\text{time/div})} \quad [\text{Hz}]$$

where $N$ is the number of horizontal divisions for one complete cycle.

### DSO Method (Auto-Measurement)
Modern DSOs perform **zero-crossing counting** on a digitized waveform, or apply **FFT** to extract the fundamental. Accuracy is typically **±0.001%**, vastly superior to analog CRO.

## 2.3 Harmonic Analyser Theory
A non-sinusoidal periodic signal obeys the **Fourier Series**:

$$v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n \sin(n\omega t + \phi_n)$$

A harmonic analyser computes the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** or its fast variant **FFT** on $N$ sampled points:

$$V_k = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v(n) \cdot e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad k = 0,1,\ldots,N-1$$

The **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** is the key index reported:

$$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{H} V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$

## 2.4 Detection System Theory
A complete safety instrumentation chain has four blocks: **Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Comparator/Controller → Actuator/Alarm**.

## 2.5 LABVIEW Dataflow Theory
LABVIEW programs are called **VIs (Virtual Instruments)**. They have:
- **Front Panel** = User Interface (controls = inputs, indicators = outputs).
- **Block Diagram** = Graphical code; a node executes only when **all its inputs are available** (dataflow paradigm).

---

### 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \int v(t) i(t)\, dt$ | Energy | Wh | Energy meter basis |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v(t) i(t)\, dt$ | Average Power | W | Digital meter DSP |
| 3 | $P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi$ | AC Real Power | W | Single-phase metering |
| 4 | $E_{bill} = \sum_k E_k \cdot r_k$ | TOD Billing | ₹ | TOD tariff |
| 5 | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Frequency | Hz | CRO/DSO |
| 6 | $T = N \times \text{(time/div)}$ | Period from graticule | s | CRO |
| 7 | $X_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Capacitive reactance | Ω | Lissajous (f ratio) |
| 8 | $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{H} V_n^2}}{V_1}$ | Distortion | % | Harmonic analyser |
| 9 | $R_f = \dfrac{\text{cycles of } f_y}{\text{cycles of } f_x}$ | Freq. ratio (Lissajous) | – | Phase/freq CRO |
| 10 | $S = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$ | Sensitivity | V/V | Detector amplifier |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: True RMS from Sampled Data (used in Digital Meters)

The **root mean square** value of a sampled waveform of $N$ samples per cycle is the basis of accurate digital metering.

**Starting point:** The continuous-time RMS definition is

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^2(t)\, dt}$$

**Step 1 — Discretization:** Replace the continuous integral with a discrete summation. Let $v[n]$ be the $n$-th sample of $v(t)$ over one fundamental period $T$, with $N$ samples.

$$V_{rms} \approx \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v^2[n]}$$

**Step 2 — Square each sample** to obtain $v^2[n]$, then **sum** all squared samples:

$$V_{rms} \approx \sqrt{\frac{\sum v^2[n]}{N}}$$

**Step 3 — Identical operation for current:**

$$I_{rms} \approx \sqrt{\frac{\sum i^2[n]}{N}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute instantaneous power** $p[n] = v[n] \cdot i[n]$, then average:

$$P_{avg} \approx \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v[n] \cdot i[n]$$

**Step 5 — Energy over a billing interval** $(t_1, t_2)$:

$$E = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} P_{avg}(t)\, dt \quad [\text{Wh}]$$

**Step 6 — TOD segmentation:** The MCU splits the cumulative energy into $K$ time slots using RTC:

$$E_{total} = E_{slot1} + E_{slot2} + \cdots + E_{slotK}$$

**Step 7 — Bill computation:**

$$\text{Bill} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} E_{slot\,k} \times r_{slot\,k}$$

where $r_{slot\,k}$ is the per-unit tariff for slot $k$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently give a 3-slot TOD problem and ask for the total bill. The above is the full derivation chain you must show.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Frequency from Lissajous Pattern (CRO method)

When a CRO is in **X-Y mode**, applying $v_x = V_x \sin(2\pi f_x t)$ on X and $v_y = V_y \sin(2\pi f_y t + \phi)$ on Y produces a **Lissajous figure**. The frequency ratio is obtained by counting tangent intersections.

**Step 1 — Horizontal tangent count:** $N_H$ = number of times the figure touches a horizontal tangent line.

**Step 2 — Vertical tangent count:** $N_V$ = number of times the figure touches a vertical tangent line.

**Step 3 — Apply the formula:**

$$\frac{f_y}{f_x} = \frac{N_H}{N_V}$$

**Worked Example:** A Lissajous figure has $N_H = 3$ horizontal lobes and $N_V = 2$ vertical lobes, with $f_x = 1\,\text{kHz}$ known. Then:

$$f_y = f_x \cdot \frac{N_H}{N_V} = 1000 \cdot \frac{3}{2} = 1500\,\text{Hz}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: THD from Spectral Components

Given the harmonic magnitudes $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_H$ measured by the analyser:

**Step 1 — Compute the RMS of all harmonic content** (from $n=2$ to $H$):

$$V_{harm} = \sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots + V_H^2}$$

**Step 2 — Divide by the fundamental RMS:**

$$\text{THD} = \frac{V_{harm}}{V_1} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{H} V_n^2}}{V_1}$$

**Step 3 — Express as a percentage:**

$$\text{THD}_{\%} = \text{THD} \times 100$$

**Worked Example:** A waveform has $V_1 = 230\,\text{V}$, $V_3 = 12\,\text{V}$, $V_5 = 8\,\text{V}$, $V_7 = 4\,\text{V}$.

$$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{12^2 + 8^2 + 4^2}}{230} = \frac{\sqrt{144+64+16}}{230} = \frac{\sqrt{224}}{230} = \frac{14.97}{230} = 0.0651$$

$$\text{THD}_{\%} = 6.51\%$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Detector System Signal Path

| Block | Function | Typical Component | Transfer Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensor | Transduces hazard → electrical | MQ-6 (gas), photodiode (smoke) | $R_s = f(\text{concentration})$ |
| Wheatstone Bridge | Converts $\Delta R$ to $\Delta V$ | Precision resistors | $V_{out} = V_{ex}\dfrac{\Delta R}{4R}$ |
| Amplifier | Boosts $\Delta V$ to usable level | Op-amp (INA128) | $V_{amp} = G \cdot V_{out}$ |
| Comparator | Threshold decision | LM393 | $V_{out} = V_{CC}$ if $V_{amp} > V_{ref}$ |
| Microcontroller | Decision + alarm logic | Arduino/8051 | Algorithm based |
| Actuator | Siren / Relay | Buzzer, contactor | Switch closure |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Digital / Bidirectional Energy Meter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Mains Supply] --> B[VT Voltage Sensor]
    A --> C[CT Current Sensor]
    B --> D[Signal Conditioner and Anti Alias Filter]
    C --> D
    D --> E[ADC 16 bit]
    E --> F[DSP Microcontroller]
    F --> G[Real Time Clock RTC]
    F --> H[Energy Register Import]
    F --> I[Energy Register Export]
    H --> J[LCD Display]
    I --> J
    F --> K[Non Volatile Memory]
    F --> L[Optical Communication Port]
```

**Reading guide:** Voltage and current are sensed, conditioned, digitised, then processed by the DSP. The RTC drives TOD slot indexing. The bidirectional decision routes energy to Import or Export register.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Analog Input CH1] --> B1[Attenuator and Amplifier]
    A2[Analog Input CH2] --> B2[Attenuator and Amplifier]
    B1 --> C[ADC High Speed]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[Acquisition Memory]
    D --> E[Trigger Circuit]
    D --> F[DSP Processor]
    F --> G[FFT Engine]
    F --> H[Measurement Engine]
    F --> I[Display Memory]
    I --> J[LCD Color Display]
    F --> K[Front Panel Controls]
```

**Reading guide:** Two channels are conditioned in parallel, digitised, and stored. The trigger circuit freezes the waveform in time-alignment. The DSP computes parameters (Vpp, T, f, THD) and drives the LCD.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Harmonic Analyser (FFT-based)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Voltage / Current Signal] --> W[Anti Alias LPF]
    W --> AD[ADC Sampler]
    AD --> B[Buffer N samples]
    B --> FFT[FFT Processor N Point]
    FFT --> MAG[Compute Magnitude Spectrum]
    MAG --> THD[THD Calculator]
    MAG --> DISP[Spectrum Display]
    THD --> DISP
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Fire / Smoke / Gas Detection System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[Gas Sensor MQ 6] --> SC1[Signal Conditioner]
    S[Smoke Sensor Photoelectric] --> SC2[Signal Conditioner]
    F[Flame Sensor IR / UV] --> SC3[Signal Conditioner]
    SC1 --> MC[Microcontroller 8051 or Arduino]
    SC2 --> MC
    SC3 --> MC
    MC --> BUZ[Buzzer Alarm]
    MC --> REL[Relay for Sprinkler]
    MC --> LCD[LCD Status Display]
    MC --> TX[GSM or WiFi Transmitter]
```

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: LABVIEW Virtual Instrumentation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PC[PC with LABVIEW] --> DAQ[DAQ Card PCI or USB]
    DAQ --> SC[Signal Conditioning Panel]
    SC --> V1[Virtual Oscilloscope VI]
    SC --> V2[Virtual Function Generator VI]
    SC --> V3[Virtual Multimeter VI]
    SC --> V4[Virtual Spectrum Analyser VI]
    V1 --> FP[Front Panel User Interface]
    V2 --> FP
    V3 --> FP
    V4 --> FP
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between a unidirectional and a bidirectional energy meter. Why is a bidirectional meter essential in a prosumer (rooftop solar) household?

**Model Answer:**
- A *unidirectional* meter records only the magnitude of energy drawn from the grid (i.e., import), irrespective of any reverse power flow. A *bidirectional* meter maintains **two separate registers**: Import (kWh drawn from grid) and Export (kWh injected to grid). The sign of the instantaneous average power determines which register is incremented.
- In a *prosumer* household, the rooftop PV inverter feeds surplus solar energy back into the grid during midday. A unidirectional meter would either ignore the reverse flow or even *run backward* illegally, while a bidirectional meter correctly accounts for both flows, enabling **net-metering billing**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write "bidirectional meter measures both AC and DC" — this is **wrong**. Direction refers to *energy flow direction*, not the type of current.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]** What is the role of a **Real-Time Clock (RTC)** inside a TOD energy meter? List any two disadvantages of electromechanical induction meters compared to digital meters.

**Model Answer:**
- The **RTC** provides a stable time reference (date, hour, minute). The meter's microcontroller uses this to decide which **time slot** (e.g., 06:00–09:00 = peak) the currently consumed energy belongs to, and increments the corresponding tariff register.
- Disadvantages of electromechanical meters:
  1. **Lower accuracy class** (typically 2.0–2.5) and drift with time/temperature.
  2. **No TOD or remote reading capability**; they cannot record time-slot energy, exports, or power-quality data.
  3. Susceptible to **tampering via magnetic or mechanical interference**.
  4. Higher power consumption of the meter itself (≈2 W vs <0.5 W).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **digital bidirectional energy meter** used in a rooftop solar net-metering application. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A consumer has the following half-hourly energy consumption slots in a 3-rate TOD tariff:

| Time Slot | Duration (h) | Energy (kWh) | Tariff (₹/kWh) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 06:00–18:00 (Normal) | 12 | 9.0 | 6.50 |
| 18:00–22:00 (Peak) | 4 | 4.0 | 9.00 |
| 22:00–06:00 (Off-peak) | 8 | 3.0 | 4.00 |

The rooftop PV system **exports 5.0 kWh** during 12:00–13:00. Compute the **net bill** assuming a 1:1 net-metering policy (exports directly offset imports at the slot's tariff). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block diagram & working:** (Draw the bidirectional meter block diagram from Section 4.1)
- **VT** senses line voltage; **CT** senses line current.
- **Signal conditioner** scales the signals; **ADC** digitises.
- **DSP/MCU** computes $p(t) = v(t) i(t)$; the **sign of the mean** gives the direction.
- If $\bar{P} > 0$ → energy added to **Import register**; if $\bar{P} < 0$ → energy added to **Export register**.
- The **RTC** partitions the import/export kWh by TOD slot.
- **Non-volatile memory** retains data during power failure; **optical port** allows remote reading.
- The **LCD** displays import kWh, export kWh, instantaneous power, and TOD slot.

**(b) Bill calculation:**

**Step 1 — Compute import energy in each slot (already given in the table):**
- Normal: 9.0 kWh
- Peak: 4.0 kWh
- Off-peak: 3.0 kWh

**Step 2 — Distribute the 5.0 kWh export.** The export occurred during 12:00–13:00, which lies in the **Normal slot (06:00–18:00)**. Per net-metering policy, 5.0 kWh of export offsets 5.0 kWh of import in the same slot at the **same tariff**.

**Step 3 — Net import in Normal slot:**
$$E_{normal,net} = 9.0 - 5.0 = 4.0\,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Net bill (Peak & Off-peak remain unchanged):**
$$\text{Bill} = (4.0 \times 6.50) + (4.0 \times 9.00) + (3.0 \times 4.00)$$
$$\text{Bill} = 26.00 + 36.00 + 12.00 = \mathbf{₹\,74.00}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students forget to *net* the export against the correct time slot, and instead apply a single average credit — this loses 2 marks.

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram how a **Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO)** measures frequency, peak-to-peak voltage, and **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** of a distorted sine wave. Compare DSO with a conventional analog CRO. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The voltage waveform at the terminals of a non-linear load is:
$$v(t) = 230\sqrt{2}\sin(314 t) + 30\sqrt{2}\sin(942 t) + 15\sqrt{2}\sin(1570 t) \quad [\text{V}]$$
Compute the **THD (%)** and the **RMS value** of the waveform. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DSO block diagram and working:** (Refer to Section 4.2)
- Input attenuated/amplified → sampled by ADC → stored in acquisition memory.
- **Time measurement engine** finds the time between two consecutive zero-crossings → period $T$ → frequency $f = 1/T$.
- **Amplitude engine** finds $V_{max}$ and $V_{min}$ → $V_{pp} = V_{max} - V_{min}$.
- **FFT engine** computes the magnitude spectrum; **THD engine** applies the formula $\sqrt{\sum_{n\ge2} V_n^2}/V_1 \times 100$.

| Feature | Analog CRO | DSO |
|---|---|---|
| Display | Phosphor CRT (transient) | LCD (persistent, stored) |
| Bandwidth | Limited by CRT Y-amp | Up to GHz with equivalent-time sampling |
| Measurement | Manual graticule | Auto cursor + on-screen readout |
| FFT | Not available | Built-in |
| Trigger | Edge only | Edge, pulse, logic, video, serial |

**(b) THD and RMS calculation:**

**Step 1 — Identify fundamental and harmonics:**
- $V_1 = 230\sqrt{2}$ V peak → $V_{1,rms} = 230$ V, fundamental
- $V_3 = 30\sqrt{2}$ V peak → $V_{3,rms} = 30$ V (3rd harmonic, 942/314 = 3)
- $V_5 = 15\sqrt{2}$ V peak → $V_{5,rms} = 15$ V (5th harmonic, 1570/314 = 5)

**Step 2 — RMS of harmonic content:**
$$V_{h,rms} = \sqrt{V_3^2 + V_5^2} = \sqrt{30^2 + 15^2} = \sqrt{900 + 225} = \sqrt{1125} \approx 33.54\,\text{V}$$

**Step 3 — THD:**
$$\text{THD} = \frac{33.54}{230} \times 100\% \approx 14.58\%$$

**Step 4 — Total RMS (fundamental + harmonics, orthogonal):**
$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + V_3^2 + V_5^2} = \sqrt{230^2 + 30^2 + 15^2} = \sqrt{52900 + 900 + 225} = \sqrt{54025} \approx 232.43\,\text{V}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Energy equation**: $E = \int v(t) i(t)\, dt$ — the unifying formula behind all energy meters.
- ✅ **TOD meter** = RTC + multi-register; the **RTC** is the heart of TOD.
- ✅ **Bidirectional meter** tracks **sign of average power** → import vs export.
- ✅ **Net-metering rule**: exports are netted against imports of the **same time slot** at the **same tariff** (unless policy says otherwise — read the question!).
- ✅ **Frequency from CRO**: $f = 1/(N \times \text{time/div})$; from Lissajous: $f_y/f_x = N_H/N_V$.
- ✅ **DSO features**: auto-measurement, FFT, persistent display, deep memory — CRO cannot do FFT.
- ✅ **THD formula**: $\text{THD} = \sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{H}V_n^2}/V_1 \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Orthogonality of harmonics** means total RMS = $\sqrt{V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots}$.
- ✅ **Detector system chain**: Sensor → Conditioner → Comparator/MCU → Actuator. Know one example for each of gas (MQ-6, semiconductor), smoke (photoelectric), fire (UV/IR).
- ✅ **LABVIEW** uses **dataflow G-programming**; the **Front Panel** is the UI, the **Block Diagram** is the code; **DAQ card** replaces physical instruments.
- ✅ **Lissajous** is the CRO's trick for *unknown* frequency measurement when calibrated time-base is unavailable.
- ✅ **Bidirectional ≠ AC/DC**; examiners love this trap.
- ✅ **Electromechanical meters** are obsolete for KTU numericals; always assume **digital/electronic** for any modern problem.
- ✅ Always show **units (kWh, Hz, %, V)** in the final answer — KTU examiners deduct marks for unit omissions.
